paper_title: Federated Deep Reinforcement Learning for Open RAN Slicing in 6G Networks
paper_claims: Federated deep reinforcement learning, in which MVNOs locally train DDPG-based bandwidth allocation policies and share model updates for weighted aggregation at a non-RT RIC, produces privacy-preserving and generalizable RAN slicing policies that maintain URLLC delay and eMBB throughput SLAs under heterogeneous non-i.i.d conditions while enabling low-latency online inference at the near-RT RIC.

verbatim_claims.text: In this paper, we propose a federated deep reinforcement learning approach for RAN slicing.
In this approach, MVNOs collaborate to improve the performance of their DRL-based RAN slicing models.
The simulation results show the effectiveness of the proposed DRL approach.
Accordingly, we propose an FL-based cooperative radio resource allocation mechanism for MVNOs.

The proposed RAN slicing approach is consistent with the zero-touch network framework since it aims to autonomously manage the allocation of radio resources to users by adapting the allocation policy to the constraints of the environment and the requirements of users.
In this article, we proposed a FDRL RAN slicing mechanism in a multi-MVNOs environment to allocate radio resources to URLLC and eMBB users.
The proposed FDRL mechanism has proven to be robust in meeting the diverse requirements of URLLC and eMMB users in different scenarios and more resilient to the environment changes.

qa_by_claim.claim: Federated deep reinforcement learning, in which MVNOs locally train DDPG-based bandwidth allocation policies and share model updates for weighted aggregation at a non-RT RIC, produces privacy-preserving and generalizable RAN slicing policies that maintain URLLC delay and eMBB throughput SLAs under heterogeneous non-i.i.d conditions while enabling low-latency online inference at the near-RT RIC.

mcq.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which complete statement accurately reflects the approach and outcomes: federated deep reinforcement learning where MVNOs locally train DDPG-based bandwidth allocation policies and share model updates for weighted aggregation at a non-RT RIC, yielding privacy-preserving and generalizable RAN slicing policies that maintain URLLC delay and eMBB throughput SLAs under heterogeneous non-i.i.d conditions while enabling low-latency online inference at the near-RT RIC? [A] MVNOs locally train DDPG-based bandwidth allocation policies and share model updates for weighted aggregation at a non-RT RIC, producing privacy-preserving and generalizable RAN slicing policies that maintain URLLC delay and eMBB throughput SLAs under heterogeneous non-i.i.d conditions and enabling low-latency online inference at the near-RT RIC. [B] Centralized training at the near-RT RIC with raw data sharing and unweighted aggregation yields policies that are not privacy-preserving, maintain only eMBB throughput SLAs, and require high-latency inference at the non-RT RIC. [C] MVNOs locally train A2C policies and send gradients to a centralized unit, achieving generalization only under i.i.d data, failing URLLC delay SLAs, and performing inference on user equipment. [D] MVNOs locally train DDPG policies but aggregate at the near-RT RIC, producing non–privacy-preserving policies that maintain URLLC SLAs only under homogeneous data and require inference at the core network.

Choose the correct option (A–D): In the described setup, what combination of training, aggregation, policy properties, SLA guarantees, data heterogeneity, and inference correctly characterizes the outcome? [A] Local DDPG training with weighted aggregation at the near-RT RIC yields privacy-preserving but not generalizable policies that maintain only eMBB throughput SLAs under i.i.d conditions and use high-latency inference at the non-RT RIC. [B] Centralized PPO training with raw data sharing yields policies that violate URLLC delay SLAs and rely on offline, high-latency inference at the near-RT RIC. [C] MVNOs locally train DDPG-based bandwidth allocation policies and share model updates for weighted aggregation at a non-RT RIC, producing privacy-preserving, generalizable RAN slicing policies that maintain URLLC delay and eMBB throughput SLAs under heterogeneous non-i.i.d conditions while enabling low-latency online inference at the near-RT RIC. [D] Federated supervised learning with gradient averaging at the core network generalizes only under i.i.d data, fails to maintain SLAs, and performs inference at a far-RT controller.

Federated deep reinforcement learning that centralizes raw data at the near-RT RIC and performs unweighted aggregation produces non–privacy-preserving RAN slicing policies that generalize only under i.i.d conditions, fail to maintain URLLC delay and eMBB throughput SLAs, and require high-latency online inference at the non-RT RIC.

paper_title: Web-Based Volunteer Distributed Computing for Handling Time-Critical Urgent Workloads
paper_claims: Browser-based volunteer distributed computing using WebWorkers and a centralized cross-origin task manager can viably execute embarrassingly parallel urgent workloads without client-side software installation across heterogeneous platforms.

In web-based volunteer computing, a small minority of long dwell time sessions complete most tasks while many short sessions complete none, making performance dependent on value sessions and imposing overhead from non-value sessions.

Checkpointing partial results, batching tasks, and asynchronously prefetching new tasks increase the amount of useful work completed during volatile browsing sessions at the cost of higher server-side load.
Tuning task granularity is essential because smaller tasks increase the likelihood of value sessions but also amplify task management and communication overheads.

For data-bound workloads, XMLHttpRequest communication can outperform WebSockets, whereas compute-bound kernels are relatively insensitive to protocol choice and their browser-executed performance relative to native code depends on algorithm characteristics and browser engine optimizations.

qa_by_claim.claim: Browser-based volunteer distributed computing using WebWorkers and a centralized cross-origin task manager can viably execute embarrassingly parallel urgent workloads without client-side software installation across heterogeneous platforms.

mcq.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which statement accurately describes the viability and requirements of using browsers for volunteer distributed computing to handle urgent tasks? [A] Browser-based volunteer distributed computing that uses WebWorkers and a centralized cross-origin task manager can viably execute embarrassingly parallel urgent workloads without requiring any client-side software installation across heterogeneous platforms. [B] Browser-based volunteer distributed computing can only execute non-urgent workloads and requires client-side software installation on each device, even when WebWorkers are used. [C] Such computing is viable only on homogeneous platforms with native agents, regardless of whether tasks are embarrassingly parallel. [D] Viability is limited to shared-memory workloads coordinated by a same-origin manager and requires browser extensions.

Choose the correct option (A–D): Under what conditions can a browser-based volunteer distributed computing setup viably execute embarrassingly parallel urgent workloads across diverse devices without installing software on clients? [A] When it relies on peer-to-peer coordination among tabs without WebWorkers and restricts cross-origin communication. [B] When it uses Service Workers and offline caching with a decentralized task manager. [C] When it employs WebWorkers in conjunction with a centralized cross-origin task manager so tasks run in the browser across heterogeneous platforms without client-side installation. [D] Only when devices share identical hardware and a native client is preinstalled.

[B] For data-bound workloads, WebSockets always outperform XMLHttpRequest, and compute-bound kernels are highly sensitive to protocol choice while their browser performance is independent of algorithm and engine optimizations. [C] For data-bound workloads, protocol choice is irrelevant, whereas compute-bound kernels depend primarily on protocol and not on algorithm characteristics or browser engine optimizations. [D] For both data-bound and compute-bound workloads, WebSockets universally outperform XMLHttpRequest, and browser-executed performance always matches native code regardless of algorithm or engine.

[C] For data-bound workloads, XMLHttpRequest can outperform WebSockets, and compute-bound kernels are relatively insensitive to protocol choice, with their browser-executed performance relative to native code depending on algorithm characteristics and browser engine optimizations. [D] Data-bound workloads may favor XMLHttpRequest, but compute-bound kernels are sensitive to protocol and achieve parity with native code whenever the browser is optimized.

paper_title: A topological analysis of cointegrated data: a Z24 Bridge case study
paper_claims: Time-delay embeddings of Z24 Bridge natural frequency time series exhibit a toroidal topology driven by cyclic temperature effects, whereas embeddings of cointegrated residuals exhibit an open-ball topology characteristic of approximately Gaussian white-noise processes.

The nonlinear dependence of the second natural frequency on temperature leads linear cointegration to leave residual topological structure, while Gaussian process-based nonlinear cointegration trained on data that include the nonlinear regime more effectively removes these effects.

Persistent homology comparisons using Wasserstein distances on Vietoris-Rips complexes of embedded series provide a quantitative topology-based metric for assessing the effectiveness of cointegration in detrending environmental and operational variability.

verbatim_claims.text: Cointegration is used in this paper to remove effects from Environmental and Operational Vari-ations, by cointegrating the first four natural frequencies for the Z24 Bridge data.
Topological data analysis is used to analyse the raw time series, and the cointegrated equivalents.
The persistent homology is then analysed, to determine the topological struc-ture of all the time series.
This paper will explore the effects of cointegration and how the topology is affected, before and after.
Two cointegration schemes will be used in this paper: linear and nonlinear.
To account for nonlinear effects, in the case of the nonlinear cointegration scheme, Gaussian Processes (GPs) are fitted to the natural frequency, these are then used in the calculations.
To form a topology from the univariate time series, a time-delay embedding is used [6, 7].
With respect to the Z24 data set, topological methods have been used to analyse time series data, before and after cointegration.
When temperature effects are not adequately represented in the GP model, the topology of the original time series seems to break through into the residuals.
This work has shown that time-delay embeddings and TDA are able to quantify how well cointegration has detrended a time series, by analysing the topology of the residuals.

qa_by_claim.claim: Time-delay embeddings of Z24 Bridge natural frequency time series exhibit a toroidal topology driven by cyclic temperature effects, whereas embeddings of cointegrated residuals exhibit an open-ball topology characteristic of approximately Gaussian white-noise processes.

mcq.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): For Z24 Bridge natural frequency data, time-delay embeddings of the raw time series and of cointegrated residuals show which combination of topologies and drivers? [A] Raw embeddings are toroidal due to cyclic temperature effects, whereas residual embeddings are open-ball and characteristic of approximately Gaussian white-noise processes. [B] Raw embeddings are open-ball and characteristic of approximately Gaussian white-noise processes, whereas residual embeddings are toroidal due to cyclic temperature effects. [C] Both raw and residual embeddings are toroidal due to seasonal forcing. [D] Both raw and residual embeddings are open-ball due to de-trended white-noise fluctuations.

Choose the correct option (A–D): Which statement best summarizes the delay-embedding topology contrast in Z24 Bridge natural frequency analysis? [A] Raw series form a spherical shell driven by diurnal temperature cycles, while cointegrated residuals form a torus associated with colored noise. [B] Raw series form a torus induced by cyclic temperature, while cointegrated residuals form an open ball consistent with approximately Gaussian white noise. [C] Raw series and cointegrated residuals both form open balls, but only the residuals are approximately Gaussian. [D] Raw series and cointegrated residuals both form tori, reflecting persistent periodicity in both.

true_false.question: For Z24 Bridge natural frequency data, time-delay embeddings of the raw series exhibit a toroidal topology driven by cyclic temperature effects, whereas embeddings of cointegrated residuals exhibit an open-ball topology characteristic of approximately Gaussian white-noise processes.

In the Z24 Bridge natural frequency data, delay embeddings of the raw series are open-ball and characteristic of approximately Gaussian white-noise processes, while embeddings of cointegrated residuals are toroidal due to cyclic temperature effects.

assertion_reason.question: Assertion (A): Time-delay embeddings of Z24 Bridge natural frequency time series are toroidal due to cyclic temperature effects, while embeddings of cointegrated residuals are open-ball and consistent with approximately Gaussian white-noise processes. Reason (R): Cyclic temperature induces periodic structure that yields a torus in delay space, and approximately Gaussian white-noise processes fill an open ball under delay embedding.

paper_title: ADAS: A Simple Active-and-Adaptive Baseline for Cross-Domain 3D Semantic Segmentation
paper_claims: A unified domain adaptive 3D semantic segmentation approach uses symmetrical attention-based 2D–3D feature interaction to produce cross-modal representations that train a domain discriminator for domainness-driven active selection of informative source and target frames, enabling effective adaptation under unsupervised, few-shot unsupervised, and active annotation settings and enhancing existing UDA methods through targeted pseudo-labeling of high-scoring target data.

verbatim_claims.text: In this paper, we introduce a Uniﬁed Domain Adaptive 3D semantic segmentation pipeline (UniDA3D) to enhance the weak generalization ability, and bridge the point distribution gap between domains.

Different from previous studies that only focus on a single adapta- tion task, UniDA3D can tackle several adaptation tasks in 3D segmentation ﬁeld, by designing a uniﬁed source-and- target active sampling strategy, which selects a maximally- informative subset from both source and target domains for effective model adaptation.

Besides, beneﬁting from the rise of multi-modal 2D-3D datasets, UniDA3D investigates the possibility of achieving a multi-modal sampling strategy, by developing a cross-modality feature interaction module that can extract a representative pair of image and point features to achieve a bi-directional image-point feature interaction for safe model adaptation.
Experimentally, UniDA3D is veriﬁed to be effective in many adaptation tasks includ- ing: 1) unsupervised domain adaptation, 2) unsupervised few-shot domain adaptation; 3) active domain adaptation.
Their results demonstrate that, by easily coupling UniDA3D with off-the-shelf 3D segmentation baselines, domain gen- eralization ability of these baselines can be enhanced.

Thus, we pro- pose a Uniﬁed Domain Adaptive 3D semantic segmenta- tion (UniDA3D) pipeline, which can pick up a maximally- informative subset from both source and target domains to reduce the difﬁculty of model source-to-target adaptation.
We explore the possibilities of leveraging bi-domain cross-modal sampling method to achieve a uniﬁed do- main adaptive 3D semantic segmentation.
UniDA3D pipeline is proposed, which utilizes a cross- modality feature interaction module to fuse the fea- ture’s multi-modal information, boosting the model’s adaptation performance.

UniDA3D can be deployed to many adaptation tasks simultaneously, which include: 1) UDA task where all data from the target domain are unlabeled; 2)UFDA task where we can only access few-shot un- labeled samples from the target domain; 3) ADA task where a portion of unlabeled target data is se- lected to be annotated by an oracle.
In this paper, we have presented a UniDA3D to tackle many adaptation tasks, including UDA, UFDA, and ADA tasks, by means of a uniﬁed pipeline.

By the merits of the designed bi-domain cross-modality feature interaction module, UniDA3D can fully leverage features from differ- ent modalities to achieve an informative sample selection process from different domains.
Experiments are conducted on widely-used 3D semantic segmentation datasets, show- ing the superiority of UniDA3D in boosting the segmenta- tion model transferability.

paper_title: Predicting Companies' ESG Ratings from News Articles Using Multivariate Timeseries Analysis
paper_claims: Annual corporate ESG ratings can be inferred from news by modeling monthly multivariate timeseries that integrate weakly supervised ESG-relevance detection, sentiment ratios for relevant and irrelevant articles, and semantic topic cluster frequencies.

Formulating ESG rating prediction as a regression task over these timeseries with deep convolutional architectures yields superior predictive performance compared to classification and to index-aggregation baselines based on document-level transformer outputs.

Weak supervision using sentence-embedding similarity to ESG definitions effectively bootstraps a DistilBERT-based classifier to generate scalable ESG-relevance signals without manual keyword selection.

A large-scale news corpus aligned to thousands of US companies and annual third‑party ESG scores over multiple years is compiled and publicly released to support training and evaluation of ESG prediction models.

verbatim_claims.text: A news dataset for about 3,000 US companies together with their ratings is also created and released for training.

Through the experimental evaluation we find out that our approach provides accurate results outperforming the state-of-the-art, and can be used in practice to support a manual determination or analysis of ESG ratings.
We create ESG-related news dataset for about 3,000 US com- panies for the years 2018, 2019 and 2020.
We propose a multi-variate timeseries based approach that har- nesses different signals from data based on sentiment analysis, semantic and topics analysis.
We then use CNN and transformer- based models for generating the final ratings.
We test our model against state-of-the-art, and perform thor- ough analysis of the model capability and characteristics.

paper_title: Semi-Supervised Learning of Monocular Depth Estimation via Consistency Regularization with K-way Disjoint Masking
paper_claims: A semi-supervised monocular depth estimation framework enforces uncertainty-weighted consistency between predictions from weakly and strongly augmented views generated via K-way disjoint masking, enabling effective learning from unlabeled images without stereo or video sequences.

K-way disjoint masking forms strong augmentations by independently encoding multiple disjoint token subsets and decoding their concatenation, which preserves global context, retains small objects, and mitigates scale ambiguity in depth prediction.

An auxiliary uncertainty estimation module weights pixelwise consistency to filter noisy pseudo-labels, while a predictor-headed feature consistency constraint stabilizes training and prevents collapse.
The approach remains robust under extremely sparse depth annotations and can be directly applied to unsupervised domain adaptation from synthetic to real data without additional generative models.

verbatim_claims.text: We propose MaskingDepth, a novel semi-supervised learning framework for monocular depth estimation to mitigate the reliance on large ground-truth depth quan- tities.

MaskingDepth is designed to enforce consistency between the strongly-augmented unlabeled data and the pseudo-labels derived from weakly-augmented unlabeled data, which enables learning depth without supervision.

In this framework, a novel data augmentation is proposed to take the advantage of a na ¨ıve masking strategy as an augmentation, while avoiding its scale ambiguity prob- lem between depths from weakly- and strongly-augmented branches and risk of missing small-scale instances.

To only retain high-conﬁdent depth predictions from the weakly- augmented branch as pseudo-labels, we also present an uncertainty estimation technique, which is used to deﬁne robust consistency regularization.

Experiments on KITTI and NYU-Depth-v2 datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of each component, its robustness to the use of fewer depth- annotated images, and superior performance compared to other state-of-the-art semi-supervised methods for monoc- ular depth estimation.
In this paper, for the ﬁrst time, we present a novel semi- supervised learning framework, called MaskingDepth, for monocular depth estimation based on an uncertainty-aware consistency regularization.

To apply enough perturbations to an input image, we propose a new data augmentation, called K-way disjoint masking, inspired by recent masked image modeling strate- gies for vision Transformers [4, 66, 29].

The K-way disjoint masking jointly decodes scattered tokens that are encoded from a K-disjoint set of tokens indepen- dently, and thus mitigates the scale ambiguity and restores the full context from the image while retaining the beneﬁts of masking-based data augmentation.
In addition, we propose an uncertainty estimation technique [32, 50] to aid depth consistency and facilitate convergence by ﬁltering out the noise of pseudo labels.
In this paper, we presented MaskingDepth, a novel semi- supervised framework for monocular depth estimation us- ing consistency regularization.
MaskingDepth allows lever- aging unlabeled data without requiring either stereo or se- quential frames.
We have shown that K-way disjoint masking makes consis- tency regularization framework more stable by utilizing an uncertainty network.
Our proposed method showed signiﬁcant improvement on extremely sparse labeled data and superior results compared to other semi-supervised ap- proaches.

paper_title: Bayesian optimization with partially specified queries
paper_claims: Bayesian optimization with partially specified queries formalizes optimization where only a subset of input variables are specified and the remainder are randomly drawn, optimizing the conditional expectation over unspecified variables, and posterior-sampling algorithms for known and unknown input distributions—incorporating GP samples and UCB-style bonuses for empirical distribution estimates under an independence assumption—admit sublinear Bayesian cumulative regret for popular kernels without exponential dependence on dimensionality.

verbatim_claims.text: In this paper, we propose a novel Gaussian process bandit problem, BO with partially specified queries (BOPSQ).

In BOPSQ, unlike the standard BO setting, a learner specifies only the values of some input variables, and the values of the unspecified input variables are randomly determined according to a known or unknown distribution.
We propose two algorithms based on posterior sampling for cases of known and unknown input distributions.
We further derive their regret bounds that are sublinear for popular kernels.
We demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithms using test functions and real-world datasets.
In this paper, we propose a novel Gaussian process (GP) bandit framework, which we call Bayesian optimization with partially specified queries (BOPSQ).
We propose two algorithms for known and unknown input distributions.
In the case of the known input distribution, the uncertainty lies only in the function estimation, and we can naturally extend a posterior sampling approach to this setting.
We adopt a multi-armed bandit approach to appropriately explore the input distribution.
We propose two algorithms for known and unknown input distribution cases with their sublinear regret bounds for popular kernels.
We demonstrate the empirical effectiveness of the proposed algorithms using a set of test functions and real-world datasets.

qa_by_claim.claim: Bayesian optimization with partially specified queries formalizes optimization where only a subset of input variables are specified and the remainder are randomly drawn, optimizing the conditional expectation over unspecified variables, and posterior-sampling algorithms for known and unknown input distributions—incorporating GP samples and UCB-style bonuses for empirical distribution estimates under an independence assumption—admit sublinear Bayesian cumulative regret for popular kernels without exponential dependence on dimensionality.

[A] It does, and the resulting algorithms for known and unknown input distributions combine GP posterior samples with UCB-style bonuses on empirical input-distribution estimates under an independence assumption and enjoy sublinear Bayesian cumulative regret for popular kernels without exponential dimensional dependence. [B] It requires full specification of all input variables and optimizes an unconditional expectation, with regret that scales exponentially in dimension. [C] It optimizes a worst-case value over unspecified variables and requires dependence across variables, yielding linear regret in time. [D] It applies only when the input distribution is known and uses GP samples without any UCB bonuses, resulting in bounds that depend exponentially on dimensionality.

[A] The marginal expectation is optimized and cumulative regret is sublinear but grows exponentially with dimension. [B] The conditional expectation over unspecified variables is optimized and Bayesian cumulative regret is sublinear for popular kernels without exponential dependence on dimensionality. [C] The worst-case value over unspecified variables is optimized and regret is constant regardless of dimension. [D] A deterministic objective is optimized and regret bounds require all variables to be specified.

paper_title: Push-the-Boundary: Boundary-aware Feature Propagation for Semantic Segmentation of 3D Point Clouds
paper_claims: A boundary-aware feature propagation mechanism for 3D point cloud semantic segmentation, implemented in a multitask framework with a shared encoder that jointly predicts object boundary locations, directions pointing toward object interiors, and semantic labels, fuses the predicted boundaries and directions to propagate features toward true boundaries, and consequently reduces boundary-related segmentation errors.

verbatim_claims.text: To improve the segmentation near object boundaries, we propose a boundary-aware feature propagation mechanism.
This mechanism is achieved by exploiting a multitask learning framework that aims to explicitly guide the boundaries to their original locations.
With one shared encoder, our network outputs (i) boundary localization, (ii) prediction of directions pointing to the object's interior, and (iii) semantic segmentation, in three parallel streams.
The predicted boundaries and directions are fused to propagate the learned features to refine the segmentation.

We conduct extensive experiments on the S3DIS and SensatUrban datasets against various baseline methods, demonstrating that our proposed approach yields consistent improvements by reducing boundary errors.

qa_by_claim.claim: A boundary-aware feature propagation mechanism for 3D point cloud semantic segmentation, implemented in a multitask framework with a shared encoder that jointly predicts object boundary locations, directions pointing toward object interiors, and semantic labels, fuses the predicted boundaries and directions to propagate features toward true boundaries, and consequently reduces boundary-related segmentation errors.

Which option correctly reflects this description? [A] A multitask, shared-encoder design jointly predicts boundary locations, interior-pointing directions, and semantic labels; boundary and direction predictions are fused to propagate features toward true boundaries, reducing boundary-related segmentation errors. [B] A single-task design predicts only semantic labels without boundary or direction cues; features are propagated away from boundaries to reduce computation time. [C] Separate encoders in a multitask setup predict boundary locations and directions only; features are propagated toward object centers to reduce overall segmentation error. [D] A multitask, shared-encoder design predicts boundaries and labels but not directions; boundaries are used only for post-processing and do not affect feature propagation.

[C] Implemented as a multitask framework with a shared encoder, it jointly predicts object boundary locations, directions pointing toward object interiors, and semantic labels, fuses boundaries and directions to propagate features toward true boundaries, and reduces boundary-related segmentation errors. [D] Implemented for 2D image segmentation, it estimates semantic labels alone and applies a smoothing filter near edges to reduce boundary errors.

paper_title: Personalized Federated Recommender Systems with Private and Partially Federated AutoEncoders
paper_claims: A federated recommender architecture that trains client-specific encoders in an autoencoder locally while aggregating only a global decoder enables private personalized recommendations, improves convergence and accuracy relative to federated averaging and attains performance close to centralized training, while substantially reducing computation and communication by updating and transmitting only decoder parameters associated with rated items.

verbatim_claims.text: We propose Personalized Fed- erated Recommender Systems (PersonalFR), which introduces a personalized autoencoder-based recommendation model with Federated Learning (FL) to address these challenges.
PersonalFR guarantees that each user can learn a personal model from the local dataset and other participating users’ data without sharing local data, data embeddings, or models.
Extensive experiments on two real-world datasets demonstrate that PersonalFR can achieve private and personalized perfor- mance comparable to that trained by centralizing all users’ data.
Moreover, PersonalFR requires signiﬁcantly less computation and communication overhead than standard FL baselines.

Motivated by the aforementioned issues, this work pro- poses Personalized Federated Recommender Systems (Person- alFR), which introduces a personalized AutoEncoder-based recommendation model with Federated Learning (FL).
The basic architecture of PersonalFR is training the personalized encoder for each client to establish client-speciﬁc mapping information and averaging the decoder on the server side.
The suggested PersonalFR with PFL shows faster convergence than standard FL baselines in our extensive experiments.
We present a new framework of Recommender Systems (RSs), which can provide precise, private, and person- alized recommendations without sharing local data, data embeddings, or models.

By leveraging Partially Federated Learning (PFL), we show that our PersonalFR can outperform FedAvg and achieve private and personalized performance comparable to that trained by centralizing all the users’ data.
We propose Partial Compression (PC) that only computes and transfers the active model parameters.
In this work, we propose Personalized Federated Recom- mender Systems (PersonalFR), which combines a personal- ized AutoEncoder-based recommendation model with Feder- ated Learning (FL).

We demonstrate that by using Partially Federated Learning (PFL), our PersonalFR can surpass the FedAvg and obtain private and customized performance close to that achieved by centralizing all user data.
Furthermore, the PersonalFR requires far less computation and communication overhead than the FedAvg by applying Partial Compression (PC).

qa_by_claim.claim: A federated recommender architecture that trains client-specific encoders in an autoencoder locally while aggregating only a global decoder enables private personalized recommendations, improves convergence and accuracy relative to federated averaging and attains performance close to centralized training, while substantially reducing computation and communication by updating and transmitting only decoder parameters associated with rated items.

paper_title: Neural Network Learner for Minesweeper
paper_claims: Supervised neural networks trained on large sets of Minesweeper positions can learn to choose safe next moves with success rates comparable to constraint-satisfaction solvers on beginner and intermediate boards, but they require substantially more computation time than deterministic approaches.

verbatim_claims.text: This project proposes a neural network based learner for solving Minesweeper.
Surprisingly, the proposed neural network based learner has shown to be a very good approximation function for solving Minesweeper.
The neural network learner competes well with the CSP solvers, especially in Beginner and Intermediate modes of the game.
It was also observed that despite having high success rates, the best neural learner was considerably slower than the best deterministic solver.

[B] They learn to choose safe next moves with higher success rates than constraint-satisfaction solvers on beginner and intermediate boards while using less computation time than deterministic approaches. [C] They fail to choose safe next moves reliably on beginner and intermediate boards and require computation time similar to deterministic approaches. [D] They match constraint-satisfaction solvers only on expert boards and require no additional computation time relative to deterministic approaches.

[D] Inability to learn safe next moves on beginner and intermediate boards and equal computation time to deterministic approaches.

paper_title: Progressive Backdoor Erasing via connecting Backdoor and Adversarial Attacks
paper_claims: In a linear classifier with an additive patch trigger, the optimal untargeted adversarial perturbation has a substantial projection onto the trigger direction, theoretically explaining the similarity between adversarial and triggered inputs.

Fine-tuning an infected model on untargeted adversarial examples associates trigger-like patterns with multiple labels and thereby disrupts the trigger–target correlation that drives backdoor behavior while preserving performance on clean data.

Backdoors can be erased without a clean extra dataset by progressively alternating adversarial fine-tuning with clean-sample identification based on logit-similarity between infected and purified models to iteratively refine both the model and the auxiliary dataset.

verbatim_claims.text: However, in this paper we ﬁnd an intriguing con- nection between them: for a model planted with backdoors, we observe that its adversarial examples have similar be- haviors as its triggered images, i.e., both activate the same subset of DNN neurons.
Based on these observations, a novel Pro- gressive Backdoor Erasing (PBE) algorithm is proposed to progressively purify the infected model by leveraging untar- geted adversarial attacks.
Different from previous backdoor defense methods, one signiﬁcant advantage of our approach is that it can erase backdoor even when the clean extra dataset is unavailable.

We empirically show that, against 5 state-of-the-art backdoor attacks, our PBE can effectively erase the backdoor without obvious performance degrada- tion on clean samples and signiﬁcantly outperforms existing defense methods.
But in this paper, we illustrate that there is an underlying connection between them, i.e., planting a backdoor into one model will signiﬁcantly affect the model’s adversarial examples.
In particular, we observe that: for a model planted with backdoors, its adversarial examples have similar behaviors as its triggered images.

Particularly, these phe- nomena are present regardless of what target-label is, what backdoor attack setting is ( i.e., all-to-one or all-to-all set- tings), and even what trigger embedding mechanism is ( e.g., adding [7], blending [3], reﬂection [17] or warping [21]).

In this paper, we propose a new backdoor defense method based on the discovered connections between ad- versarial and backdoor attacks, which could not only de-fend against modern backdoor attacks but also work with- out a clean extra dataset.
To the best of knowledge, our approach is the ﬁrst work to defend against backdoor attack without a clean extra dataset.
In this work, we propose a new progressive backdoor erasing approach by leveraging adversarial attack tech- niques.
It stems from our intriguing observations that for an infected model, its adversarial examples have similar be- haviors as the triggered images.
And an theoretical analysis is given to explain such observations.

paper_title: Sensing-Throughput Tradeoffs with Generative Adversarial Networks for NextG Spectrum Sharing
paper_claims: In NextG spectrum sharing with periodic retraining over channel coherence frames, augmenting limited real spectrum sensing data with GAN-generated synthetic samples enables shorter sensing phases while maintaining low false alarm and misdetection rates, thereby increasing user throughput and protecting incumbents under both AWGN and Rayleigh fading.

The benefit of GAN-based sensing data augmentation is greater under Rayleigh fading than AWGN due to higher channel variability, but excessive reliance on synthetic samples can degrade classifier accuracy, implying an optimal balance between real and generated training data.

verbatim_claims.text: In this paper, we present a generative adversarial network (GAN) approach to generate synthetic sensing results to augment the training data for the deep learning classiﬁer so that the sensing time can be reduced (and thus the transmission time can be increased) while keeping high accuracy of the classiﬁer.

We consider both additive white Gaussian noise (A WGN) and Rayleigh channels, and show that this GAN-based approach can signiﬁcantly improve both the protection of the high-priority user and the throughput of the NextG user (more in Rayleigh channels than A WGN channels).
In this paper, we use the GAN to generate more sensing results to train a high-ﬁdelity detector for the incumbent user activities.

Compared to the previous studies that have focused on the data generation objectives, our goal in this paper is to study the effect of the GAN on the sensing-throughput trade-offs by shortening the length of the learning phase via the GAN and thus obtaining a long transmission phase for the NextG user to achieve high throughput.
Our results indicate that the GAN can be effectively used to improve spectrum utilization for NextG spectrum sharing systems without extra communication or sensing capabilities.
One way to improve the performance is using the GAN to generate synthetic sensing data and using such data, along with real sensing data, as the training data to build a classiﬁer.
We showed that for both AWGN and Rayleigh channels, the GAN can achieve a larger NextG user throughput than the approach without the GAN and still protect the incumbent user transmissions.

qa_by_claim.claim: In NextG spectrum sharing with periodic retraining over channel coherence frames, augmenting limited real spectrum sensing data with GAN-generated synthetic samples enables shorter sensing phases while maintaining low false alarm and misdetection rates, thereby increasing user throughput and protecting incumbents under both AWGN and Rayleigh fading.

mcq.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): In NextG spectrum sharing with periodic retraining over channel coherence frames, augmenting limited real spectrum sensing data with GAN-generated synthetic samples has which combined effect across channel conditions? [B] It requires longer sensing phases and increases false alarms, reducing throughput while protecting incumbents only under AWGN. [C] It leaves sensing duration unchanged but raises misdetection rates to boost throughput under Rayleigh fading alone. [D] It removes the need for periodic retraining and is effective only in non-fading, interference-free channels.

Choose the correct option (A–D): Which configuration and outcome correctly describe the setting in which NextG spectrum sharing benefits occur? [A] Data augmentation with GAN-generated synthetic samples combined with periodic retraining over channel coherence frames yields shorter sensing phases with low false alarm and misdetection rates, increasing user throughput and protecting incumbents under both AWGN and Rayleigh fading. [B] No data augmentation and continuous per-slot retraining yield longer sensing phases with higher false alarm rates, increasing throughput only under AWGN. [C] One-time GAN-based augmentation at deployment in static channels increases throughput by allowing higher misdetection and reduced incumbent protection. [D] Classical oversampling with periodic retraining over coherence frames provides unchanged sensing duration with low false alarms but higher misdetection under AWGN only.

In NextG spectrum sharing without periodic retraining, augmenting limited real spectrum sensing data with GAN-generated synthetic samples necessitates longer sensing phases and yields higher false alarm or misdetection rates, reducing user throughput and compromising incumbent protection only under AWGN and not Rayleigh fading.

paper_title: Foresight - Deep Generative Modelling of Patient Timelines using Electronic Health Records
paper_claims: A generative autoregressive transformer trained on SNOMED-coded patient timelines derived from both free-text and structured EHR data can forecast multi-step future biomedical concepts (disorders, substances, procedures, findings) across hospitals, yielding clinically relevant predictions and performing best for recurring events when using daily temporal resolution.

verbatim_claims.text: We present Foresight, a novel transformer-based pipeline that uses named entity recognition and linking tools to convert document text into structured, coded concepts, followed by providing probabilistic forecasts for future medical events such as disorders, substances, procedures and findings.
As a generative model, it can forecast follow-on biomedical concepts for as many steps as required.

Foresight is a general-purpose model for biomedical concept modelling that can be used for real-world risk forecasting, virtual trials and clinical research to study the progression of disorders, simulate interventions and counterfactuals, and educational purposes.
We use the unstructured and structured data within the EHR to train a novel model, Foresight, for disorder and more generally biomedical concept forecasting.
We see the same in Foresight because the same model can be used to forecast the risk of disorders, offer differential diagnosis, suggest substances to be used and more.
We test the model across multiple hospitals covering both physical and mental health and make it publicly available via a web application ( https://foresight.sites.er.kcl.ac.uk/ ).

We present a novel deep learning generative model of patients using EHRs that is composed of both natural language processing and a longitudinal forecasting, with broad utility across many healthcare domains.
Foresight opens the door for digital health twins, synthetic dataset generation, real world risk forecasting, longitudinal research, emulation of virtual trials, medical education and more.

qa_by_claim.claim: A generative autoregressive transformer trained on SNOMED-coded patient timelines derived from both free-text and structured EHR data can forecast multi-step future biomedical concepts (disorders, substances, procedures, findings) across hospitals, yielding clinically relevant predictions and performing best for recurring events when using daily temporal resolution.

mcq.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which option best summarizes the demonstrated capability and conditions of a system for forecasting biomedical concepts from SNOMED-coded EHR timelines? [B] A discriminative recurrent network trained only on structured EHR data predicts single-step future diagnoses within a single hospital and is most effective for rare, nonrecurring events at weekly temporal resolution, with limited clinical relevance. [C] A generative autoregressive transformer trained on SNOMED-coded timelines from free-text alone forecasts multi-step future lab measurements across hospitals, but shows the strongest performance for isolated events at monthly resolution without clear clinical relevance. [D] A generative encoder-only transformer trained on ICD-coded claims data forecasts only next-visit diagnoses in a single institution and performs best at hourly resolution for nonrecurring events, providing primarily research-oriented predictions.

Choose the correct option (A–D): Which option identifies the correct combination of model type, training data source, prediction target and horizon, evaluation setting, and temporal-resolution finding described? [A] A generative seq2seq model trained on medication orders predicts single-step procedure codes within one hospital and performs best at weekly resolution for nonrecurring events, with marginal clinical utility. [B] A discriminative logistic model trained on structured EHR features forecasts mortality risk across hospitals and performs best at daily resolution for rare events, with clinically relevant predictions. [C] A generative autoregressive transformer trained on SNOMED-coded patient timelines derived from both free-text and structured EHR data forecasts multi-step future biomedical concepts (disorders, substances, procedures, findings) across hospitals, yields clinically relevant predictions, and performs best for recurring events at daily temporal resolution. [D] An unsupervised topic model trained on clinical notes forecasts single-step future findings within one system and is most accurate at monthly resolution for one-time events, with uncertain clinical relevance.

A generative autoregressive transformer trained on SNOMED-coded patient timelines from both free-text and structured EHR data is limited to single-step predictions within a single hospital, yields minimal clinical relevance, and performs best for nonrecurring events at weekly temporal resolution.

Reason (R): The use of SNOMED-coded timelines integrating free-text and structured EHR and evaluation at a daily time scale across hospitals aligns with achieving the strongest predictability for recurring events and clinically relevant multi-step forecasts.

paper_title: Difformer: Empowering Diffusion Model on Embedding Space for Text Generation
paper_claims: In embedding diffusion models for text, training with the standard denoising objective causes collapse of the learnable embedding space, producing anisotropic and poorly distinguishable token representations and destabilizing optimization.

An anchor loss that maximizes token likelihood given the denoiser’s predicted clean embeddings enforces well-distributed and distinguishable embeddings while stabilizing training by directly coupling denoiser outputs to discrete targets.

In high-dimensional embedding spaces, conventional noise schedules provide insufficient corruption that drives the denoising model to a condition-agnostic nearest-neighbor predictor, with the degenerate solution becoming globally optimal as noise vanishes and dimension grows.

A noise rescaling framework guided by a degeneration score that quantifies the accuracy of the degenerate predictor increases schedule variance via a multiplicative factor to prevent degeneration, independently of the base noise schedule.

A Transformer-based embedding diffusion model equipped with explicit length prediction and two-dimensional parallel decoding over candidate lengths and initial noises enables fast inference via reverse-step subsampling while maintaining high generation quality and diversity.

verbatim_claims.text: In this paper, we conduct systematic studies of the optimization challenges encountered with both the embedding space and the denoising model, which have not been carefully explored.
To alleviate this problem, we propose a new objective called the anchor loss which is more efficient than previous methods.
Secondly, we find the noise levels of conventional schedules are insufficient for training a desirable denoising model while introducing varying degrees of degeneration in consequence.
To address this challenge, we propose a novel framework called noise rescaling.
Based on the above analysis, we propose Difformer, an embedding diffusion model based on Transformer.
To avoid the collapse caused by dynamically shifting embedding parameters, we propose an anchor loss function to attain well-distributed embeddings and stabilize the training process.
In Section 3.2, we find that in the high dimensional embedding space, the insufficient noise results in a simple denoising task, which causes the degeneration of the model.
To tackle this challenge, we propose a novel framework named noise rescaling, which is orthogonal to the choice of the noise schedule and applicable to any existing schedules.
Based on the above discussion, we propose an integrated framework of Difformer, a denoising diffusion Transformer model.
On these benchmark datasets, Difformer outperforms diffusion-based and iteration-based non-autoregressive baselines and achieves state-of-the-art performance among embedding diffusion models.

Further experiments demonstrate the superiority of Difformer over baselines including LLMs in quality, diversity, and efficiency, emphasizing the potential of diffusion models for text generation in the era of LLMs.

Firstly, to tackle the challenge of embedding collapse and instability in training caused by the dynamic nature of embeddings, we introduce an anchor loss to regularize the embeddings and stabilize the training simultaneously.
Secondly, we derive a novel noise rescaling framework based on theoretical analysis, which notably alleviates the degeneration of the denoising model resulting from inadequate noise.
Finally, integrated with the aforementioned techniques, we present Difformer, a denoising diffusion model based on Transformer.
Difformer demonstrates superior performance on various benchmark text generation tasks, outperforming prior diffusion-based models as well as iterative non-autoregressive models.

mcq.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): In high-dimensional embedding spaces, what is the effect of conventional noise schedules on denoising model behavior and optimality as noise vanishes and dimension grows? [A] They provide insufficient corruption, pushing the model toward a condition-agnostic nearest-neighbor predictor, a degenerate solution that becomes globally optimal in the low-noise, high-dimensional limit. [B] They provide excessive corruption, enforcing condition dependence and making nearest-neighbor behavior globally suboptimal as noise vanishes and dimension grows. [C] They provide sufficient corruption to maintain condition dependence, so the optimal solution avoids nearest-neighbor behavior in high dimension and vanishing noise. [D] They are invariant to dimensionality and noise level, leaving the optimal predictor unchanged and not favoring nearest-neighbor behavior.

Choose the correct option (A–D): Which statement correctly characterizes the interplay of dimensionality, noise level, and conventional noise schedules for denoising in embedding spaces? [A] As noise increases and dimension decreases, insufficient corruption yields a condition-agnostic nearest-neighbor predictor that becomes globally optimal. [B] In high-dimensional embeddings, conventional schedules introduce enough corruption to prevent degeneration, so conditioning dominates and nearest-neighbor is never globally optimal as noise vanishes. [C] In high-dimensional embeddings, conventional schedules fail to corrupt sufficiently, driving denoising to a condition-agnostic nearest-neighbor rule that becomes globally optimal as noise goes to zero and dimension grows. [D] The optimal predictor remains condition-aware across dimensions and noise levels because schedules are tuned to avoid nearest-neighbor behavior.

assertion_reason.question: Assertion (A): In high-dimensional embedding spaces, conventional noise schedules provide insufficient corruption that drives the denoising model to a condition-agnostic nearest-neighbor predictor, with this degenerate solution becoming globally optimal as noise vanishes and dimension grows. paper_title: A model of checkpoint behavior for applications that have I/O
paper_claims: For MPI-based parallel applications that perform file I/O, the checkpoint file size is governed by workload, process and node counts, and collective I/O configuration (number of aggregators and aggregator buffer size), because the checkpoint image comprises application data, libraries, communication buffers, and an additional I/O buffer zone concentrated in aggregator processes whose magnitude depends on the MPI-IO implementation and its two-phase optimization.

verbatim_claims.text: In this paper, we present a model of checkpoint behavior for parallel applications that performs I/O; this depends on the application and on other factors such as the number of processes, the mapping of processes and the type of I/O used.

Our model describes the behavior of the checkpoint size based on the characteristics of the system and the type (or model) of I/O used, such as the number I/O aggregator processes, the buffering size utilized by the two-phase I/O optimization technique and components of collective file I/O operations.
The model can be useful when selecting what type of checkpoint configuration is more appropriate according to the applications’ characteristics and resources available.
In this paper, we present a model of checkpoint behavior for HPC parallel applications that uses message passing (MPI) which performs I/O.
The model is an extension of the previously published work, where applications didn’t perform I/O operations.

Our model describes the behavior of the checkpoint size based on the number of processes and nodes when, concurrently, there are I/O from application processes, the number I/O aggregator processes and buffering size utilized by the two-phase I/O optimization technique.

qa_by_claim.claim: For MPI-based parallel applications that perform file I/O, the checkpoint file size is governed by workload, process and node counts, and collective I/O configuration (number of aggregators and aggregator buffer size), because the checkpoint image comprises application data, libraries, communication buffers, and an additional I/O buffer zone concentrated in aggregator processes whose magnitude depends on the MPI-IO implementation and its two-phase optimization.

paper_title: Comparison of Three Job Mapping Algorithms for Supercomputer Resource Managers
paper_claims: A parallel composite mapping algorithm that seeds a genetic search with independently generated simulated-annealing solutions yields higher average mapping accuracy than either method alone, while parallel simulated annealing achieves the shortest runtimes with acceptable accuracy and is the only approach that consistently satisfies typical supercomputer resource-manager time limits for large graphs.

verbatim_claims.text: Three mapping algorithms were explored: parallel versions of simulated annealing, genetic and composite algorithms.

A set of experimental runs with different algorithms parameters was performed, comparison of mapping quality and runtime was made, an d suggestions on applicability of algorithms for resource managers were provided.
In this paper, we continue the study of algorithms for mapping the information graph of a parallel program onto the graph of a parallel computer.

We considered the ways to improve the solution [2] and studied the features of the composite parallel algorithm (imitation of annealing and genetic algorithm) for graphs of higher order corresponding to highly parallel computer.
The parallel combined algorithm always provides an average more accurate solution than the genetic and simulated annealing algorithms .
The simulated annealing algorithm has the minimum runtime.
The runtime of the composite algorithm is close to the runtime of the parallel genetic algorithm.
With a small number of processes the composite algorithm provides acceptable solutions, this reduces the amount of resources for mapping.
For high order graphs, the most accurate solutions are found by the parallel genetic algorithm and the parallel composite algorithm.
The parallel simulated annealing algorithm requires significantly less time, and it can be recommended for mapping the regular jobs in supercomputer resource managers.

qa_by_claim.claim: A parallel composite mapping algorithm that seeds a genetic search with independently generated simulated-annealing solutions yields higher average mapping accuracy than either method alone, while parallel simulated annealing achieves the shortest runtimes with acceptable accuracy and is the only approach that consistently satisfies typical supercomputer resource-manager time limits for large graphs.

[C] Parallel simulated annealing yields the highest average mapping accuracy and the composite achieves the shortest runtimes and consistent compliance with the time limits. [D] Neither the composite nor parallel simulated annealing improves over standalone methods, and both fail to satisfy typical time limits on large graphs.

[B] The composite and parallel simulated annealing have identical accuracy, and both fail to meet resource-manager time limits on large graphs. [C] The composite sacrifices accuracy for runtime, and parallel genetic search—not parallel simulated annealing—is the only approach that consistently satisfies resource-manager time limits. paper_title: Deep-Learning-Assisted IoT-Based RIS for Cooperative Communications
paper_claims: Training deep neural networks on channel state information to compute RIS element phase shifts and to classify received symbols enables IoT-coordinated, low-complexity multi-RIS cooperative communications without a direct path, with reliability governed by relay placement under Rayleigh fading and path loss.

verbatim_claims.text: In this paper, two new deep neural network (DNN)-assisted cooperative RIS models, namely, DNN 𝑅-CRIS and DNN 𝑅𝐷-CRIS, are pro- posed for cooperative communications.

In DNN 𝑅-CRIS model, the potential of RIS deployment as an IoT-based relay element in a next-generation cooperative network is investigated using deep learning (DL) techniques for RIS phase optimization.
In addition, to reduce the maximum likelihood (ML) complexity at 𝐷, a new DNN-based symbol detection method is presented with the DNN𝑅𝐷-CRIS model combined with DNN-assisted phase optimization.
It is revealed that the proposed DNN- based models show promising results in terms of BER, even in high-noise environments with low system complexity.

In this work, we investigate the possibility of a novel usage scenario: using an RIS as the sole relaying element in a cooperative network, where multiple DL-optimized RISs are utilized in a cooperative conﬁguration between 𝑆and 𝐷.

In our proposed deep learning-assisted cooperative RIS models (DNN 𝑅- CRIS and DNN 𝑅𝐷- CRIS), RISs are optimized through a deep neural network (DNN), which is trained with the channel state information (CSI) obtained for incident and reﬂected signals to estimate the phase adjustments required for optimal signal transmission of RISs.
Furthermore, in the DNN 𝑅𝐷- CRISmodel, instead of using a conventional maximum likelihood (ML) detector at 𝐷, we deploy another DNN to estimate the received symbols.

In the proposed DNN optimized model, all DNNs deployed on the RISs are considered to be embedded in the RIS con- troller hardware and interconnected through an IoT network to communicate and coordinate with other network components.
In the proposed DNN 𝑅- CRIS and DNN 𝑅𝐷- CRIS models, RISs deployed as IoT-based relay elements in a cooperative network are presented.
In both models, RIS conﬁgurations are optimized with DNNs.
A DNN is deployed for controlling each RIS in the system and to optimize the phase adjustments in real time.
In the DNN 𝑅𝐷- CRIS model, an additional DNN is deployed at the destination for symbol detection instead of an ML detector.
The distance from the relays to𝑆or𝐷in conjunction with fading channels has a critical impact on BER performance, and this is demonstrated extensively in our work.
An extensive evaluation of the results is also performed with a complexity analysis, comparing proposed DNN- assisted and conventional methods.

paper_title: Unsupervised Dense Retrieval Deserves Better Positive Pairs: Scalable Augmentation with Query Extraction and Generation
paper_claims: Unsupervised dense retrieval can be trained at scale by generating pseudo queries from documents via salient-span extraction and transferred summarization/keyword generation, yielding models that perform competitively across heterogeneous search tasks without human-annotated query–document pairs.

Using pretrained text generators to produce short topics or titles transfers beneficial inductive bias for retriever pretraining and is more effective than heuristic random cropping as a source of pseudo queries.

Momentum-encoder contrastive training is more robust to noisy pseudo queries and benefits from mixing random crops with higher-quality pseudo queries, whereas in-batch contrastive training depends more on higher-quality pseudo queries and gains less from such mixing.
In-domain pseudo topic generation for target collections enables effective unsupervised domain adaptation of dense retrievers without supervised queries, hard negatives, or rerankers.

verbatim_claims.text: We propose two approaches that enable annotation-free and scalable training by creating pseudo query-document pairs: query extraction and transferred query generation.
The query extraction method involves selecting salient spans from the original document to generate pseudo queries.
On the other hand, the transferred query generation method utilizes generation models trained for other NLP tasks, such as summarization, to produce pseudo queries.
Through extensive experimentation, we demonstrate that models trained using these augmentation methods can achieve comparable, if not better, performance than multiple strong dense baselines.

Moreover, combining these strategies leads to further improvements, resulting in superior performance of unsupervised dense retrieval, unsupervised domain adaptation and supervised fine-tuning, benchmarked on both BEIR and ODQA datasets1.
We introduce query extraction ( QE XT), a novel data augmentation method for training dense retrievers.

We propose transferred query generation ( TQG EN), where pseudo queries are generated using generation models trained for other NLP tasks, such as summarization, unlike previous studies that heavily rely on human annotated data for training query generation models.
To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study demonstrating that the inductive bias from other NLP tasks can be leveraged for training dense retrievers.
We contribute two datasets, namely AUGQ-W IKIandAUGQ-CC, which consist of 22.6M and 52.4M pseudo query-document pairs for unsupervised retrieval training.
Extensive experiments show that retrievers using QE XTand TQG EN, referred to as AUGTRIEVER , achieve superior performance and beat strong baselines on BEIR and open-domain QA benchmarks.
In this study, we propose a set of scalable augmentation methods to generate surrogate queries for training dense retrievers without the need for annotated query-document pairs.
Our approach achieves great performance on widely used benchmarks (BEIR and six ODQA tasks).

These results highlight the effectiveness of extracted andtransfer generated query-document pairs for training dense retrievers and prompt us to consider low-cost alternatives in place of expensive human annotations.

paper_title: Explainable depression detection with multi-aspect features using a hybrid deep learning model on social media
paper_claims: Integrating hierarchical attention-based representations of user tweets with multi-aspect behavioral features—including social interactions, posting-time patterns, emoji sentiment with valence–arousal–dominance signals, topic distributions, and domain-specific symptom and medication cues—yields superior and explainable depression detection on social media compared with using textual or behavioral signals alone.

verbatim_claims.text: In this work, we propose explainable Multi-Aspect Depression Detection with Hierarchical Atten- tion Network MDHAN , for automatic detection of depressed users on social media and explain the model prediction.

Specifically, we encode user posts using two levels of attention mecha- nisms applied at the tweet-level and word-level, calculate each tweet and words’ importance, and capture semantic sequence features from the user timelines (posts).
Our experiments show that MDHAN outperforms several popular and robust baseline methods, demonstrating the effectiveness of combining deep learning with multi- aspect features.
MDHAN achieves excellent performance and ensures adequate evidence to explain the prediction.

we propose a hybrid model, Multi-aspect Depression Detection Hierarchical Attention Network MDHAN to boost the classification of depressed users using multi-aspect features and word embed- ding features.
Our model is based on explainable depression detection, which can learn explainable information from a user’s tweets.
We further show when we combined the two attributes, our model led to better perfor- mance.
To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work of using multi-aspect of topical, temporaland semantic features jointly with word embeddings in deep learning for depressiondetection.

introducing the prospective of viewing explainability of model for depression detec- tion and building a pipeline aided with explainability based on hierarchical attention networks to explain the prediction of depression detection.
Extensive experiments are conducted on benchmark depression twitter dataset, which shows the superiority of our proposed method when compared to baseline methods.

paper_title: Is it Required? Ranking the Skills Required for a Job-Title
paper_claims: Important and relevant skills for a job title tend to appear more frequently across semantically similar job titles.
Skill importance for a job title can be inferred from the title alone by training a LaBSE-based model with weak labels defined as normalized skill frequencies within semantically similar titles.
Multiplying predicted skill importance by the inverse document frequency of each skill enhances the ranking of specialized skills by penalizing generic skills.
Fine-tuning a multilingual sentence encoder on English job-title data improves skill importance ranking in English while reducing performance on non-English titles.

verbatim_claims.text: In this paper, we describe our method for ranking the skills required for a given job title.
Our analysis shows that impor- tant/relevant skills appear more frequently in similar job ti- tles.
We train a Language-agnostic BERT Sentence Encoder (LaBSE) model to predict the importance of the skills using weak supervision.
We show the model can learn the impor- tance of skills and perform well in other languages.
Furthermore, we show how the Inverse Document Frequency factor of skill boosts the specialised skills.
To solve this, we frame the problem to learn the importances of skills given a job title.
Importantly, we propose a novel method that does not require manually annotating the importance of skills for dif- ferent job titles.
Further, we show how IDF factor of a skill can improve the rank of specialised skills.

paper_title: Real-time face alignment: evaluation methods, training strategies and implementation optimization
paper_claims: The laMSD, NJS-MSE, and NFDS-MSE metrics quantify inter-frame jitter and sensitivity to face detector initialization in face landmark tracking, enabling robustness assessment beyond standard point error metrics.

General Training eXtensions (GTX) that include geometric and photometric data augmentation, statistical normalization and noise injection, initialization variation, and teacher–student domain-specific annotation improve accuracy, robustness to illumination and extreme pose, and smoothness in tracking across different face alignment algorithms and devices.

Multicore-parallel C++ implementations and software optimization of cascaded regression face alignment algorithms provide real-time inference on embedded and desktop platforms without degrading landmark localization accuracy.
Leveraging temporal information through filtering converts still-image face landmark detectors into stable real-time trackers that reduce jitter and failures under rapid head pose changes.

verbatim_claims.text: Throughout this article, we propose a set of training strategies and implementations based on data augmentation, software optimization techniques that help in improving a large variety of models belonging to several real-time algorithms for face alignment.
We propose an extended set of evaluation metrics that allow novel evaluations to mitigate the typical problems found in real-time tracking contexts.

The experimental results show that the generated models using our proposed techniques are faster, smaller, more accurate, more robust in specific challenging conditions and smoother in tracking systems.
In addition, the training strategy shows to be applicable across different types of devices and algorithms, making them versatile in both academic and industrial uses.

We tackle the improvement of the accuracy of those models by proposing a set of training strategies, while opti- mizing the algorithms via parallelization strategies and the application of software engineering techniques, accelerating the inference of the models and making them applicable in systems that require both robust and fast inference of facial landmarks both in static images and video sequences.

First, we provide a set of original C++ implementations1 of state-of-the-art face alignment methods based on cas- caded regression, following the original publication [ 18] and its reference Matlab implementation.

We extend this implementation, initially thought for face landmark detection, and modify it to develop a facial landmark tracker, able to perform in video sequences and real-time video, by leveraging temporal information.
We propose three new evaluation metrics to assess the model performance.
We derive a set of rules for optimal training strategies, and we name them General Training eXtensions (GTX).
Derived from the proposed set of metrics, they include novel techniques such as initialization variations, or the addition of domain-specific data annotated using teacher- student architectures [19].
We propose a set of software optimization techniques to accelerate the inference of these models, both in embed - ded systems and desktop computers.

paper_title: Deep Learning Based Customer Preferences Analysis in Industry 4.0 Environment
paper_claims: A deep learning approach that partitions restaurant sales time series by day-of-week and holiday type, models ordinary days with stacked LSTMs, and uses MLPs to infer total holiday demand from pre-holiday signals and allocate it across holiday days effectively captures weekly seasonality and event-driven surges in nonlinear, intermittent demand while remaining computationally lightweight for real-time deployment.

verbatim_claims.text: In this paper, we introduce a throughput deep learning model for both short-term and long-term demands forecasting aimed at allowing catering businesses to be highly efficient and avoid wastage.

The results are compared with the best performing forecast methods Xgboost and autoregressive moving average model (ARMA), and they suggest that the proposed method significantly improves demand forecasting accuracy (up to 80%) for dishes demand along with reduction in associated costs and labor allocation.
1. Using deep learning, a real time sales demand forecasting model is proposed that can accurately predict demand based on past trends;
3. The trained model can achieve prediction rate accuracy higher than 80%, outperforming existing methods suchasXgboost (less than 60%) andARMA.
Based on real data provided by a well-known restaurant, this paper constructs a sales forecasting model based on deep learning.

We split the original time series into ordinary working day data and holiday data on the basis of date characteristics of the original sequence, and then we modelthem separately to form a complete forecast model of sales volume of dishes.
The experimental results show that our model occupies preferable predication performanceand stronger robustness, which can reflect the rules and characteristics of dish sales more comprehensively.

qa_by_claim.claim: A deep learning approach that partitions restaurant sales time series by day-of-week and holiday type, models ordinary days with stacked LSTMs, and uses MLPs to infer total holiday demand from pre-holiday signals and allocate it across holiday days effectively captures weekly seasonality and event-driven surges in nonlinear, intermittent demand while remaining computationally lightweight for real-time deployment.

[A] Partition by day-of-week and holiday type; stacked LSTMs for ordinary days; MLPs infer total holiday demand from pre-holiday signals and allocate across holiday days; lightweight for real-time and captures weekly seasonality and event-driven surges [B] Partition by week-of-month and promotion type; GRUs for all days; MLPs allocate post-holiday residuals; heavy computation unsuitable for real-time and weak seasonality capture [C] No partitioning; transformers handle all periods; MLPs model price effects; focuses on linear demand with limited weekly seasonality and high computational cost [D] Partition by day-of-week only; CNNs model ordinary days; rule-based holiday handling from pre- and post-holiday averages; moderate compute without reliable surge capture

paper_title: One-Stage Tree: end-to-end tree builder and pruner
paper_claims: A decision tree framework jointly optimizes splits and pruning in a single end-to-end bilevel formulation while keeping paths and architecture discrete via Gumbel-Softmax reparameterization and proximal iterations, enabling closed-form leaf values under mean squared error and cross-entropy losses, maintaining interpretability, reducing the training–testing performance discrepancy, and delivering improved predictive performance and stability over CART and prior soft trees.

verbatim_claims.text: In this paper, we propose One-Stage Tree to build and prune the decision tree jointly through a bilevel optimization problem.
Moreover, we leverage the reparameterization trick and proximal iterations to keep the tree discrete during end-to-end training.
As a result, One-Stage Tree reduces the performance gap between training and testing and maintains the advantage of interpretability.
Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed One-Stage Tree outperforms CART and the existing soft trees on classification and regression tasks.
In this work, we propose One-Stage Tree, which is, to our best knowledge, the first soft tree that maintains discretization during training.

In contrast to the two-stage methods (e.g., CART and End2End Tree) of building and then pruning, we first formalize the joint search for node and architecture parameters as a bilevel optimization problem.
Specifically, we directly sample leaves by the Gumbel Softmax to predict instances according to the path probability and propose an optimization strategy for discrete /u1D6FE via proximal iterations.
Benefiting from the discretization, we directly find the closed-form optimal solution of /u1D710 .
Moreover, we reduce the performance gap and maintain interpretability.
Extensive experimental results on both classification and regression tasks reveal the effectiveness of One-Stage Tree.
One-Stage Tree has a significant improvement over the most typical CART.
Compared with the existing soft trees, One-Stage Tree achieves better performance on most datasets.

qa_by_claim.claim: A decision tree framework jointly optimizes splits and pruning in a single end-to-end bilevel formulation while keeping paths and architecture discrete via Gumbel-Softmax reparameterization and proximal iterations, enabling closed-form leaf values under mean squared error and cross-entropy losses, maintaining interpretability, reducing the training–testing performance discrepancy, and delivering improved predictive performance and stability over CART and prior soft trees.
