paper_title: Online Support Vector Regression Based Adaptive NARMA-L2 Controller for Nonlinear Systems
paper_claims: An adaptive NARMA-L2 controller that directly identifies its subdynamics F and G via online support vector regression from streaming input–output data computes control actions using u[n]=(r[n+1]−F̂(Xn))/Ĝ(Xn) while updating two SVR regressors through convex, globally optimal margin-based parameter adjustments, thereby avoiding local minima of gradient-based identification and enabling real-time adaptation to nonlinear system changes.

verbatim_claims.text: In this paper, online SVR is utilized to obtain controlled plant’s subdynamics and consequently this information is used in the construction of NARMA-L2 controller.
Hence functionality of NARMA-L2 controllers and high generalization capability of SVR are combined.
Also, SVR formulates a convex optimization problem and therefore guarantees global optimum solution.

In this paper, a new method is proposed where online SVR is directly em- ployed to attain NARMA-L2 model of nonlinear systems, consequently this ob- tained model is adopted to construct a NARMA-L2 controller and compute the control input signal.
Here, main novelty is the direct identiﬁcation of NARMA- L2 subdynamics by utilizing the online SVR.
The main contribution of our proposed approach here is the direct identiﬁcation of SVR parameters without appealing to any intermediate formulation.
This leads to the straightfor- ward estimation of the subdynamical terms of the NARMA-L2 model which are consequently used to compute the control input.
Additionally, a detailed comparison with the previously published work in [17] is also accomplished and the superiority of the proposed adaptive NARMA-L2 controller is veriﬁed.

qa_by_claim.claim: An adaptive NARMA-L2 controller that directly identifies its subdynamics F and G via online support vector regression from streaming input–output data computes control actions using u[n]=(r[n+1]−F̂(Xn))/Ĝ(Xn) while updating two SVR regressors through convex, globally optimal margin-based parameter adjustments, thereby avoiding local minima of gradient-based identification and enabling real-time adaptation to nonlinear system changes.

mcq.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which description correctly states how an adaptive NARMA-L2 controller identifies F and G, computes the control input u[n], updates its models, and what properties this confers regarding local minima and real-time adaptation to nonlinear changes? [A] It identifies F and G online from streaming input-output data using support vector regression, computes u[n]=(r[n+1]-F_hat(X_n))/G_hat(X_n), and updates two SVR regressors via convex, globally optimal margin-based adjustments, thereby avoiding local minima of gradient-based identification and enabling real-time adaptation. [B] It identifies only F using batch least squares from stored data, computes u[n]=(r[n+1]-F_hat(X_n))*G_hat(X_n), and updates parameters by gradient descent that can be trapped in local minima, limiting real-time adaptation. [C] It tracks only G using an online Kalman filter, computes u[n]=r[n+1]-F_hat(X_n)+G_hat(X_n), and employs nonconvex, marginless updates that provide no global optimality or real-time adaptation guarantees. [D] It uses offline SVR with fixed models, computes u[n]=(r[n]-F_hat(X_n))/G_hat(X_n), and updates a single regressor with nonconvex training prone to local minima and slow adaptation.

[B] It uses online support vector regression on streaming input-output data to directly identify F and G, computes u[n]=(r[n+1]-F_hat(X_n))/G_hat(X_n), and updates two SVR regressors through convex, globally optimal margin-based parameter adjustments that avoid local minima of gradient-based identification and enable real-time adaptation to nonlinear system changes. [C] It employs recursive least squares to estimate a linearized F while keeping G constant, uses u[n]=(r[n+1]-F_hat(X_n)), and relies on nonconvex updates without margin guarantees, limiting adaptation. [D] It trains a single SVR offline to approximate F+G and computes u[n]=G_hat(X_n)/(r[n+1]-F_hat(X_n)), which does not avoid local minima or support real-time adaptation.

paper_claims: In wireless sensor networks with omnidirectional sensing and communication, modeling the region of interest as a circle and deterministically deploying sensors on concentric rings at radii n·√3 times the sensing range with 8n evenly spaced nodes per ring yields high coverage with reduced boundary holes relative to hexagonal tessellation and requires fewer nodes than strip-based deployments over square regions of equal area.

verbatim_claims.text: This paper proposes a novel coverage improved disc shape deployment strategy.
The simulation result shows that the circle shape coverage region of interest extremely reduces the required number of sensor node s.
The proposed deployment strategy provides desirable coverage, and it requires few more sensor nodes than hexagon shape deployment strategy.
To solve such an antithetical problem of square shape region, the structure of the coverage region of interest is taken as circular in the proposed strategy.
According to numerical and simulation analysis, when compar ed with equilateral triangle tessellation (hexagon shape) , the disc shape deployment strategy gives much better coverage.
The result manifests that, when compared with equilateral triangle tessellation (hexagon shape) and square shape deployment strategies, the uncovered area of disc shape deployment is lower.
The proposed probabilistic model for disc shape deployment strategy in the circular shape coverage region of interest gives better coverage performance.

paper_title: Enhancing spatial perception through sound: mapping human movements into MIDI
paper_claims: A two-layer grid that maps tracked body positions in video frames to MIDI note and control-change values enables real-time, expressive sonification of movement which, augmented by analog Theremin-based proximity modulation and transcribed into scientific pitch notation for multi-clef music sheets, improves users’ spatial perception and adaptation in an unfamiliar dark-room environment.

verbatim_claims.text: We present a novel algorithm for mapping human move- ments into MIDI music.

The algorithm has been implemented in a system that integrates a module for real-time tracking of movements through a sample based synthesizer using dif- ferent types of filters to modulate frequencies.

The system has been evaluated through a user study, in which several users have participated in a room experience, yielding significant results about their perceptions with respect to the environment they were immersed.

More specifically, we propose a novelalgorithm and a system for recognising human movements and translating them into MIDImusic, with a sample-based synthesiser, which uses different types of filters to modulatefrequencies.
The resulting frequency after the modulation step is used to associate a specific set of sounds to each movement captured in real-time.

In particular, to quickly map spatial coordinates of people in sound, we defined a layer-based module, which is able to uniquely identify MIDI notes and to manage their changes according to people’s movements.
Moreover, we have also integrated a new module to map MIDI notes into Scientific pitch notation, which permits to represent the notes by using literal notation.

qa_by_claim.claim: A two-layer grid that maps tracked body positions in video frames to MIDI note and control-change values enables real-time, expressive sonification of movement which, augmented by analog Theremin-based proximity modulation and transcribed into scientific pitch notation for multi-clef music sheets, improves users’ spatial perception and adaptation in an unfamiliar dark-room environment.

[B] It performs offline, non-expressive audio rendering using a single-layer grid and digital Theremin emulation, produces guitar tablature, and shows no effect on spatial perception in well-lit spaces. [C] It maps video pixels to audio amplitudes without MIDI, omits proximity modulation, avoids notation, and primarily addresses auditory memory rather than spatial adaptation. [D] It triggers fixed sound samples from skeletal joints with no control-change data, relies on light-based feedback instead of Theremin, exports only waveform plots, and degrades adaptation in dark rooms.

[B] A two-layer grid maps tracked body positions to MIDI notes only, with no control changes, with analog proximity modulation removed, and it produces unnotated soundscapes that do not alter spatial adaptation. [C] A two-layer grid maps tracked body positions to both MIDI note and control-change values to enable real-time, expressive sonification, augmented by analog Theremin-based proximity modulation, and the output is transcribed into scientific pitch notation for multi-clef sheets, leading to improved spatial perception and adaptation in an unfamiliar dark-room environment. [D] A grid-free pipeline converts depth maps to frequency spectra stored as spectrograms, where proximity reduces modulation, and users’ spatial perception declines in darkness.

paper_title: AUTOMAT[R]IX: learning simple matrix pipelines
paper_claims: A tree-based search over compositions of unary matrix primitives, guided by input–output dimensional typing constraints and a probabilistic ranking that combines function-usage priors with optional TF-IDF-based alignment of user text hints to documentation, can synthesize R code that performs a desired matrix transformation from a single partially specified example.

verbatim_claims.text: We present AUTOMAT[R]IX, a system that is able to induce R program snippets from a single (and possibly partial) matrix transformation example provided by the user.
Our learning algorithm is able to induce the correct matrix pipeline snippet by composing primitives from a library.

Because of the intractable search space—exponential on the size of the library and the number of primitives to be combined in the snippet, we speed up the process with (1) a typed system that excludes all combinations of primitives with inconsistent mapping between input and output matrix dimensions, and (2) a probabilistic model to estimate the probability of each sequence of primitives from their frequency of use and a text hint provided by the user.
We validate AUTOMAT[R]IX with a set of real programming queries involving matrices from Stack Overflow, showing that we can learn the transformations efficiently, from just one partial example.

In this paper, we present a system that is able to learn simple matrix pipelines from: (1) one input data matrix, (2) one partial output matrix filled by the user representing the desired transformation, and (3) optionally, a short description or hint in natural language of the desired transformation.
Because the combinatorics of primitives and operations is in the order of bd , we use several strategies to reduce the search space.
We use all this information for a tree-search procedure that re-estimates branching candidates dynamically.

The main contributions of this paper2 are: (1) the definition of the matrix transformation problem from one single example, and (2) a learning algorithm guided by a tree-based search to learn these transformations efficiently.

Besides, we also provide: (3) a collection of matrix pipeline transformations, matransf , some of them with textual hints, organised as a benchmark repository for the community, and (4) our system AUTOMAT[R]IX, validated with a battery of experiments on matransf .

paper_title: How to measure uncertainty in uncertainty sampling for active learning
paper_claims: Separating predictive uncertainty into reducible epistemic and irreducible aleatoric components enables uncertainty sampling rules that target epistemic uncertainty as the more informative criterion.

Feature-wise evidence-based uncertainty measures disregard class priors and model aggregation, do not capture model uncertainty, and can therefore behave inconsistently with predictive entropy under class imbalance and linear models.
Uncertainty sampling with Naïve Bayes induces sampling bias that distorts estimated class priors toward frequently queried uncertain instances and can yield suboptimal decisions.
Credal uncertainty derived from probability intervals correlates strongly with epistemic uncertainty and poorly with aleatoric uncertainty, effectively acting as a measure of epistemic uncertainty.

Weighting candidate models by relative likelihood provides normalized degrees of class support that decompose total uncertainty into separate epistemic and aleatoric parts and afford graded plausibility advantages over hard credal sets.

verbatim_claims.text: To this end, we instantiate uncertainty sampling with different measures, analyze the properties of the sampling strategies thus obtained, and compare them in an experimental study.
The goal of the current paper is to elaborate more broadly on the usefulness of different measures for uncertainty sampling, and to compare their performance in active learning.

qa_by_claim.claim: Separating predictive uncertainty into reducible epistemic and irreducible aleatoric components enables uncertainty sampling rules that target epistemic uncertainty as the more informative criterion.

[B] Predictive uncertainty is not separated, and sampling should target total uncertainty regardless of type. [C] Predictive uncertainty is separated into reducible aleatoric and irreducible epistemic components, and sampling should target aleatoric uncertainty as more informative. [D] Predictive uncertainty is separated into epistemic and aleatoric parts, and sampling prioritizes aleatoric uncertainty because it reflects inherent noise that can be reduced.

Choose the correct option (A–D): In guiding uncertainty sampling toward the most informative criterion, how should predictive uncertainty be conceptualized and which component should be prioritized? [A] Treat predictive uncertainty as a single undifferentiated quantity and prioritize large total variance. [B] Decompose predictive uncertainty into reducible epistemic and irreducible aleatoric components, and prioritize epistemic uncertainty. [C] Decompose predictive uncertainty into reducible epistemic and irreducible aleatoric components, and prioritize aleatoric uncertainty. [D] Decompose predictive uncertainty into components without regard to reducibility, and prioritize whichever is largest.

assertion_reason.question: Assertion (A): Separating predictive uncertainty into reducible epistemic and irreducible aleatoric components enables uncertainty sampling rules that target epistemic uncertainty as the more informative criterion. Reason (R): Epistemic uncertainty reflects reducible knowledge gaps that can be diminished by acquiring additional data, whereas aleatoric uncertainty stems from irreducible noise.

paper_title: Detection of agglomerate fog based on a shallow convolutional neural network
paper_claims: A shallow convolutional neural network with progressively smaller convolution kernels and pooling followed by two fully connected layers effectively detects agglomerate fog in images by capturing multi-scale global and local features while reducing overfitting and computational cost relative to deeper architectures.

Partitioning an image into sub-images and aggregating sub-classification decisions via voting improves robustness of agglomerate fog detection compared with whole-image classification by mitigating the influence of fog-free regions.
Applying dropout to the fully connected layers enhances generalization performance for agglomerate fog detection compared to training without dropout.
Training with synthetic fog generated using the atmospheric scattering model produces data sufficient to learn detectors that transfer to real-world images.

verbatim_claims.text:
This paper proposes a shal -
low convolutional neural network for agglomerate fog detection in images, including the 
framework of the network and the detailed design of each component.

Firstly, the image is 
divided into several sub-images; and then a shallow convolutional neural network is con -
structed and employed to identify the existence of fog for each of the sub-area images; 
lastly, the decision results of each sub-area images were integrated to determine whether 
the whole image contained agglomerate fog.
This paper proposes a shallow convolutional neural network model for agglomerate fog detection based on image.
Comparing with the existed methods, the network layer designed in this paper is shallow, which can save computing power.

At the same time, due to the block strategy and convolution layer size from large to small design ideas, taking into account the overall characteristics and local characteristics, it can achieve good detection results.
The algorithm has the characteristics of fast and accu-rate emergency response.
After an image was divided into sub-areas (blocks), the proposed network is used to fog detection on each block, and then combined the determination results of all sub-areas to obtain a final result.
The test results of simulation data and real data show that the image detection method pro-posed in this paper has high detection accuracy.

paper_title: ReliefE: feature ranking in high-dimensional spaces via manifold embeddings
paper_claims: ReliefE performs feature ranking by computing neighbor relations in low-dimensional manifold embeddings of the instance space and, for multi-label tasks, the label space, enabling scalable operation on high-dimensional data via sparse-matrix computation.

The intrinsic dimensionality of the embedding space is estimated from the empirical distribution of ratios between distances to the two nearest nonidentical neighbors, eliminating manual selection of embedding dimension.
The number of neighbors used in the update step is selected adaptively per instance by identifying the largest gap in the sorted distance distribution, obviating a fixed k hyperparameter.
Updating feature weights against the average representation of the selected neighbors improves robustness to outliers compared to averaging per-neighbor updates.
Probabilistic matrix sparsification and representative subspace training reduce memory and time costs of embedding while maintaining the quality of the resulting feature rankings.

verbatim_claims.text: This paper explores how the Relief branch of algorithms can be adapted to benefit from (Riemann- ian) manifold-based embeddings of instance and target spaces, where a given embedding’s dimensionality is intrinsic to the dimensionality of the considered data set.
The developed ReliefE algorithm is faster and can result in better feature rankings, as shown by our evalu- ation on 20 real-life data sets for multi-class and multi-label classification tasks.
The util- ity of ReliefE for high-dimensional data sets is ensured by its implementation that utilizes sparse matrix algebraic operations.

In this work we propose ReliefE, an embedding-based feature ranking algorithm built on the ideas of the original Relief and ReliefF (Robnik-Šikonja and Kononenko 2003), as well as their extensions to a multi-label classification setting (Petković et al. 2018).
ReliefE does not compute feature importances based on the original, high-dimensional feature space, but via low-dimensional embeddings of the input and/or output spaces.

• We present ReliefE, an algorithm for feature ranking implemented using sparse matrix algebraic computation of distances between low-dimensional manifold embeddings of both instances and targets (when considering multi-label classifica- tion).
• The latent dimension of the space, in which the distances are computed, is inferred automatically in an efficient manner.
• We show that the number of neighbors to be considered can be automatically inferred based on the distribution of distances to the considered instances, rather than hard-coded as part of the input.
• Theoretically grounded sparsification of the input was considered as a preprocessing step, potentially decreasing the execution time.
• We offer evidence that ReliefE performs significantly faster than many state-of-the- art methods, especially in high-dimensional input (and output) spaces.
• Theoretical properties of ReliefE, including the properties of the embedding spaces, their relations and computational complexity analysis are studied.

qa_by_claim.claim: ReliefE performs feature ranking by computing neighbor relations in low-dimensional manifold embeddings of the instance space and, for multi-label tasks, the label space, enabling scalable operation on high-dimensional data via sparse-matrix computation.

mcq.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which option accurately describes ReliefE's overall approach to feature ranking and scalability across high-dimensional, including multi-label, datasets? [B] It ranks features by measuring global feature–label correlations in the original high-dimensional space and scales by using dense-matrix computation. [C] It ranks features by clustering instances in their original space and, for multi-label tasks, ignores inter-label relations, relying on kernel methods for scalability. [D] It ranks features by constructing decision trees over labels only and scales by dimensionality reduction that discards neighbor information.

[C] Neighbor relations computed within low-dimensional manifold embeddings of the instance space and, in multi-label problems, the label space; scalability achieved through sparse-matrix computation when handling high-dimensional data. [D] Nearest-centroid distances in compressed label embeddings only; scalability via approximate nearest neighbors in the original feature space.

paper_title: RoleSim*: Scaling axiomatic role-based similarity ranking on large graphs
paper_claims: RoleSim* defines role-based similarity as a convex combination of average similarities over maximum bipartite matchings of in-neighbors and over the remaining in-neighbor pairs, thereby preserving automorphic equivalence while incorporating non-matching neighbor information.
The difference between the k-th iterative RoleSim* score and its exact value is bounded by an exponentially decaying term in β, enabling a priori iteration bounds for a desired accuracy.
Threshold-based pruning and single-source retrieval variants of RoleSim* reduce computational time and memory while providing provable accuracy guarantees and scalability on large graphs.

verbatim_claims.text: We propose a novel similarity model, namely RoleSim*, which accurately evaluates pair- wise role similarities in a more comprehensive manner.

RoleSim* not only guarantees theautomorphic equivalence that SimRank lacks, but also takes into account the neighboring similarity information outside the automorphically equivalent sets that are overlooked by RoleSim.
We prove the existence and uniqueness of the RoleSim* solution, and show its three axiomatic properties (i.e., symmetry, boundedness, and non-increasing monotonicity).
We provide a concise bound for iteratively computing RoleSim* formula, and estimatethe number of iterations required to attain a desired accuracy.
We induce a distance metric based on RoleSim* similarity, and show that the RoleSim* metric fulfills the trian- gular inequality, which implies the sum-transitivity of its similarity scores.
We present a threshold-based RoleSim* model that reduces the computational time further with provable accuracy guarantee.
We propose a single-source RoleSim* model, which scales well for sizable graphs.
Our experimental results on real datasets demonstrate that RoleSim* achieves higher accuracy than its competitors while scaling well on sizable graphs with billions of edges.
We first propose a novel similarity model, RoleSim*, which accurately evaluates pairwise role similarities in a more comprehensive fashion.

Compared with the exist- ing well-known similarity models ( e.g., SimRank and RoleSim), RoleSim* not only guarantees the automorphic equivalence that SimRank lacks, but also takes into con-sideration the pairwise similarities outside the automorphically equivalent sets that areoverlooked by RoleSim. (Section 3.1)
We show three key properties of RoleSim*, i.e., symmetry, boundedness, and non- increasing monotonicity of its iterative similarity scores.
We derive an iterative formula for computing RoleSim* similarities.

To substantially accelerate the computation of RoleSim*, we also devise a threshold- based RoleSim* model based on two pruning strategies, and provide provable guaran- tees on accuracy which is controlled by a user-specified threshold parameter δtrading between speed and accuracy. (Section 4)

To scale RoleSim* similarity search well on large graphs with billions of edges, we pro- pose a scalable algorithm for single-source RoleSim* retrieval, which avoids spending unnecessary time on repeated RoleSim* computations while caching important pairs through an unordered hash map. (Section 5)
We induce a distance metric based on our RoleSim* measure, and rigorously show that the RoleSim* distance metric fulfills the triangular inequality which other measures (e.g., cosine distance) lack.
Our empirical results show that RoleSim* achieves higher accuracy than the existing competitors (e.g., RoleSim and SimRank) while entailing comparable computational complexity bounds of RoleSim.

qa_by_claim.claim: RoleSim* defines role-based similarity as a convex combination of average similarities over maximum bipartite matchings of in-neighbors and over the remaining in-neighbor pairs, thereby preserving automorphic equivalence while incorporating non-matching neighbor information.

mcq.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): A role-based similarity is defined as a convex combination of average similarities over maximum bipartite matchings of in-neighbors and over the remaining in-neighbor pairs, thereby preserving automorphic equivalence while incorporating non-matching neighbor information; which option correctly restates this characterization? [A] It blends averages from maximum matched in-neighbor pairs with averages over unmatched in-neighbor pairs via a convex combination, ensuring automorphic equivalence and using information from non-matching neighbors. [B] It sums similarities from maximum matched out-neighbor pairs only, which ignores unmatched neighbors and does not guarantee automorphic equivalence. [C] It takes a convex combination of similarities over all neighbor pairs without matching, focusing on out-neighbors and thus discarding automorphic equivalence. [D] It selects the maximum single-pair similarity among in-neighbors and sets others to zero to preserve structural equivalence but not automorphic equivalence.

Choose the correct option (A–D): Which option accurately characterizes a role-based similarity that both preserves automorphic equivalence and incorporates non-matching neighbor information by combining two averages? [A] It uses a harmonic mean over all neighbor-pair similarities without any matching, which may not preserve automorphic equivalence. [B] It forms a weighted sum over maximum matchings of out-neighbors and discards all non-matching neighbor pairs. [C] It is a convex combination of average similarities from maximum bipartite matchings of in-neighbors and from the remaining in-neighbor pairs, thereby preserving automorphic equivalence and including non-matching neighbor information. [D] It takes only the maximum similarity among matched in-neighbor pairs and ignores the rest to approximate automorphic equivalence.

fill_blank.question: Role-based similarity is defined as a _________(convex combination/simple sum) of average similarities over maximum bipartite matchings of in-neighbors and over the remaining in-neighbor pairs, preserving automorphic equivalence while incorporating non-matching neighbor information.

By combining average similarities from maximum bipartite matchings of _________(in-neighbors/out-neighbors) and from the remaining in-neighbor pairs, the role-based similarity preserves automorphic equivalence while incorporating non-matching neighbor information.

assertion_reason.question: Assertion (A): Role-based similarity is defined as a convex combination of average similarities over maximum bipartite matchings of in-neighbors and over the remaining in-neighbor pairs, thereby preserving automorphic equivalence while incorporating non-matching neighbor information. Reason (R): Combining the match-based average with an average over unmatched in-neighbor pairs keeps automorphically equivalent vertices indistinguishable and allows information from non-matching neighbors to contribute to the similarity.

paper_title: pfd-parallel, a Singular/GPI-Space package for massively parallel multivariate partial fractioning
paper_claims: A modular, open-source framework for multivariate partial fraction decomposition combines an improved Leinartas method—with a preliminary numerator division and an algebraic-dependence step using annihilating polynomials chosen to ensure termination under graded reverse lexicographic order—with the MultivariateApart algorithm, and realizes the computation as Petri-net workflows in GPI-Space/Singular that provide intertwined parallelism across coefficients and within termwise decompositions via fork/join with early cancellations and size-aware scheduling, producing sums whose denominators are algebraically independent with common zeros and whose numerators are reduced with respect to the denominator ideal.

verbatim_claims.text: We develop a large scale parallel framework for mul- tivariate partial fractioning, which implements and combines an improved version of Leinartas' algorithm and the MultivariateApart algorithm.
Our approach relies only on open source software.
The implementation is based on the Singular /GPI-Space framework for massively parallel computer algebra, which formulates par- allel algorithms in terms of Petri nets.
The modular nature of this approach allows for easy incorporation of future algorithmic developments into our package.
We demonstrate the performance of our framework by simplifying expressions arising from current multiloop scattering amplitude problems.
In this paper, we present a large-scale parallel implemen- tation of the two algorithms in [3] and [4], based on the Singular /GPI- Space framework1introduced in [15].

It can be conveniently installed via the widely-used su- percomputing package manager Spack [16], and is usable on homogeneous and heterogenous computing environments from a personal computer to a high-performance cluster.
By formulating the algorithm in GPI-Space in the language of Petri nets leads to automated parallelization.

The package and the realization of the parallelism are de- signed with a modular structure in mind, allowing for easy integration of improvements and algorithms which might become available in the future.

paper_title: ModelPred: A Framework for Predicting Trained Model from Training Data
paper_claims: A permutation-invariant neural network that ingests the contents of a training subset can predict the parameters of the model produced by a specified learning algorithm on that subset, generalizing to subsets with unseen points and enabling downstream tasks such as data valuation, data selection, memorization assessment, and prediction calibration.

Augmenting the predictor’s objective with a global utility-consistency regularizer and a local Karush–Kuhn–Tucker optimality regularizer constrains predicted models to be high-utility and near-stationary, thereby mitigating overfitting.

For learning problems with strongly convex or smooth objectives and bounded derivatives, the dataset-to-parameters mapping has bounded sensitivity and is efficiently approximable by ReLU neural networks.

verbatim_claims.text: Speciﬁcally, ModelPred learns a parameterized function that takes a dataset Sas the input and predicts the model obtained by training on S.
Our work diﬀers from the recent work of Datamodels [Ilyas et al., 2022] as we aim for predicting the trained model parameters directly instead of the trained model behaviors.
We demonstrate that a neural network-based set function class is capable of learning the complex relationships between the training data and model parameters.

We introduce novel global and local regularization techniques to prevent overﬁtting and we rigorously characterize the expressive power of neural networks (NN) in approximating the end-to-end training process.

Through extensive empirical investigations, we show that ModelPred enables a variety of applications that boost the interpretability and accountability of machine learning (ML), such as data valuation, data selection, memorization quantiﬁcation, and model calibration.
In this paper, we propose a framework, ModelPred , to analyze the dependence of the trained models on training data by building an explicit model for the trained models in terms of training data.

Compared to [Ilyas et al., 2022], ModelPred estimates the trained model parameters instead of the prediction performance at a certain test point, thereby providing ﬂexibility to switch to diﬀerent test points for evaluation.

At the same time, the test performance estimates based on ModelPred are more accurate than those from [Ilyas et al., 2022] because ModelPred eﬀectively leverages the knowledge of how test performance is calculated from a trained model.
ModelPred introduces a cheap-to-evaluate parametric model bA.
We treat the search for the best bAas a supervised learning problem.
In this paper, we use a neural-network-based set function to approximateA, which takes in the actual contents of a subset and predicts the trained model.
This design enables the learned bAto generalize to subsets involving unseen points.

To mitigate overﬁtting, we propose two novel regularization techniques: the global regularizer encourages that the models predicted by the ﬁtted bApredict the labels in the held-out set as eﬀectively as those obtained from the original A; and the local one ensures that the gradient of the classiﬁcation or regression learning objective function with respect to these models is close to zero.
This paper presents the ﬁrstformal study of the approximability of a learning process with neural networks.
In particular, our results suggest that learning processes associated with strongly convex or smooth problems can be eﬃciently approximated by neural networks with ReLU units.

ModelPred functions successfully predict trained models from the training data
(Table 2 and 3).

ModelPred functions enable the discovery of the connection between data and
model parameters (Figure 4).

ModelPredfunctionssuccessfullyboosttheaccuracyofdatavaluation—measuring
the contribution of individual points to learning (Table 5).

ModelPred functions lead to improved data selection in the presence of bad data
(Figure 6).

ModelPred functions lead to more eﬃcient and accurate quantiﬁcation of model
memorization (Figure 7).
ModelPred functions improve the accuracy of prediction conﬁdence (Table 6).

qa_by_claim.claim: A permutation-invariant neural network that ingests the contents of a training subset can predict the parameters of the model produced by a specified learning algorithm on that subset, generalizing to subsets with unseen points and enabling downstream tasks such as data valuation, data selection, memorization assessment, and prediction calibration.

mcq.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which statement accurately captures the described capability: a permutation-invariant neural network ingests the contents of a training subset and predicts the parameters of the model produced by a specified learning algorithm on that subset, generalizes to subsets with unseen points, and enables downstream tasks such as data valuation, data selection, memorization assessment, and prediction calibration? [A] It takes a training subset as an unordered set to predict the specified learning algorithm’s resulting model parameters for that subset, generalizes to subsets with unseen points, and supports downstream tasks including data valuation, data selection, memorization assessment, and prediction calibration. [B] It processes a fixed-order sequence of the entire training set to output labels for test points, cannot generalize to subsets with unseen points, and is not intended for tasks like data valuation, data selection, memorization assessment, or prediction calibration. [C] It ingests a training subset but predicts only hyperparameters of an unspecified algorithm; any generalization to new subsets is not supported, and the primary downstream use is hyperparameter tuning. [D] It is permutation-invariant and predicts model parameters, but only when all points were seen during training; downstream tasks are limited to model compression.

Choose the correct option (A–D): Which option best summarizes the function and utility of the system: [A] A sequence-sensitive network that consumes the entire dataset to predict labels for individual points, with no claims about generalizing to unseen points within subsets or enabling data-centric downstream tasks. [B] A permutation-invariant network that, given a training subset, estimates loss values for each example without modeling the learning algorithm’s parameters, and is primarily used for data augmentation. [C] A permutation-invariant network maps a given training subset to the parameters of the model that a specified learning algorithm would produce on that subset, generalizes to subsets containing unseen points, and enables downstream tasks including data valuation, data selection, memorization assessment, and prediction calibration. [D] A network that predicts hyperparameters for any learning algorithm but requires the subset’s examples to be in a fixed order and does not support tasks like data valuation or prediction calibration.

paper_title: A MECS redistribution algorithm for SDN-enable MEC using response time and transmission overhead
paper_claims: In SDN-enabled MEC with multiple controllers, modeling controller response time as M/G/1 sojourn plus hop-based propagation and transmission overhead as hop-weighted request load, and solving the resulting bi-objective MECS-to-controller assignment via a decomposition-based evolutionary algorithm with coordinate-transformation selection, jointly reduces control-plane response time and data-plane PACKET_IN transmission overhead while balancing controller load under capacity and limited-migration constraints.

Capping the maximum number of concurrent MECS redistributions per step reduces search space and prevents transient controller resource spikes, enabling convergence to high-quality assignments within effective time.

Increasing the number of controllers lowers average response delay and transmission overhead only up to a topology- and load-dependent saturation point, after which additional controllers provide no benefit and may degrade performance in heterogeneous (scale-free) networks if topology is not considered.

verbatim_claims.text: In this paper, a multi-objective optimization MECS redistribution algorithm (MOSRA) is proposed to decrease the response time and overhead.
A controller response time model and link transmit overhead model areintroduced as basis of an evolutionary algorithm which is proposed to optimize MECS redistribution.
The proposed algorithm aims to select an available sub-optimizes individual by using a strategy based coordination transformation from Pareto Front.

Finally, the simulation results demonstrate that the MOSRA can solve the redistribution problem in different network load levels and different network sizes within the effective time, and has a lower control plane response time, while making the edgenetwork plane transmission overhead lower, compared with other algorithms.

In order to solve the multi-objective optimization problem, we use the decom-position based multi-objective evolutionary algorithm (MOEA/D) framework to decompose the target problem into multiple scalar sub problems.
In this paper, we comprehensively consider response delaytransmission overhead and propose a switch redistributionmethod based on delay and overhead optimization.
We design MOSRA in MOEA/D algorithm framework.
It is able to solve the MECS redistributionproblem in effective time, and it can converge to the suboptimal solution which has a small gap with the optimal solution.
In this paper, we introduce a MOEA algorithm to redis-tribute the master controller of MECS for improving net-work performance in SDN-enable MEC scenarios.
The proposed algorithm uses MOEA to obtain Pareto optimal solution set and a coordinate transformation strategy toselect an available solution from the set.

It can be seen from the simulation results, in the SFN, increasing the number of controllers without considering the topological characteristics between the controller and MECS may even lead to a decrease in network performance.

Based on the test bed, we simulated the MOSRA and evaluated its performance indifferent topologies, rate of PACKET_IN and number of controllers, which demonstrated the feasibility and advan-tage of the proposed algorithm.

paper_title: MATTER: A tool for generating end-to-end IoT test scripts
paper_claims: A model-based approach uses UML state machine and class diagrams of an IoT system to automatically derive executable end-to-end test scripts by generating all-transitions-covering test paths, making them feasible with a branch-distance-guided executability heuristic, and minimising redundancy in the resulting suite.

The produced test scripts exercise the system through its user interface via standard automation frameworks while stimulating and observing sensors and actuators through virtualized components or adapters for physical devices.
Arbitrary environmental inputs are incorporated by equivalence partitioning, encoding each partition as distinct operations in the model to enable automated path generation.

On representative IoT systems evaluated with mutation testing, the generated test suites effectively detect faults, and the suite minimisation step reduces execution time without compromising fault detection capability.

verbatim_claims.text: In this paper, we extended and improved our previous approach for semi-automated model-based generation of executable test scripts.
Our proposal is oriented to system-level acceptance testing of IoT systems.

We have implemented a prototype tool taking in input a UML model of the system under test and some additional artefacts, and producing in output a test suite that checks if the system’s behaviour is compliant with such a model.
First, our prototype tool generates a set of test paths covering at least once all the transitions of the State machine.
Second, if some test paths are infeasible, it transforms them in feasible paths exploiting the notion of branch distance (McMinn, 2011).
Third, it transforms test paths into test scripts.

Produced test scripts are directly executable on the SUT because they contain testing framework commands (e.g. Selenium or Appium) able to drive a smartphone or a Web application as a real user (e.g. inserting values in fields of a form or clicking some buttons retrieved using specific locators (Leotta et al., 2021)).

qa_by_claim.claim: A model-based approach uses UML state machine and class diagrams of an IoT system to automatically derive executable end-to-end test scripts by generating all-transitions-covering test paths, making them feasible with a branch-distance-guided executability heuristic, and minimising redundancy in the resulting suite.

mcq.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which statement accurately characterizes a model-based approach for IoT systems that uses UML artifacts to produce executable end-to-end tests, including its coverage criterion, executability strategy, and suite optimization? [A] It uses UML state machine and class diagrams of an IoT system to automatically derive executable end-to-end test scripts by generating all-transitions-covering test paths, making them feasible via a branch-distance-guided executability heuristic, and minimizing redundancy in the resulting suite. [B] It uses only UML state machine diagrams to manually write integration tests by sampling random paths, accepting infeasible paths, and eliminating redundancy only by removing identical scripts. [C] It uses sequence and component diagrams of any software to generate unit tests with all-states coverage and no executability guidance, retaining all generated tests. [D] It uses UML class diagrams of an IoT system to translate requirements into high-level test ideas, relying on constraint solving for path feasibility and maximizing test diversity rather than minimizing redundancy.

Choose the correct option (A–D): Which combination of model inputs, path coverage, executability mechanism, and suite curation aligns with an approach that automatically derives executable end-to-end test scripts for IoT systems? [A] UML state machine only; all-states coverage; random search for executability; retain redundant tests to maximize count. [B] UML class diagrams only; boundary-based path selection; symbolic execution for feasibility; no redundancy minimization. [C] UML state machine and class diagrams; all-transitions-covering paths; branch-distance-guided executability heuristic; redundancy minimized in the suite. [D] UML activity and deployment diagrams; pairwise transition coverage; SAT-based feasibility; prioritize rare transitions without removing redundancy.

true_false.question: A model-based approach uses UML state machine and class diagrams of an IoT system to automatically derive executable end-to-end test scripts by generating all-transitions-covering test paths, employing a branch-distance-guided executability heuristic to make them feasible, and minimizing redundancy in the resulting suite.

A model-based approach uses UML activity diagrams of an IoT system to automatically derive exploratory test cases by generating all-states-covering paths without executability guidance and by retaining redundant scripts in the resulting suite.

Reason (R): All-transitions coverage ensures each distinct transition is exercised at least once, and branch-distance guidance drives execution toward satisfying path predicates, which together yield feasible end-to-end scripts and justify pruning tests that add no additional transition coverage.

paper_title: CORL: Compositional Representation Learning for Few-Shot Classification
paper_claims: Few-shot image classification can be performed by representing objects as compositions of a shared dictionary of component embeddings and a shared dictionary of prototypical component spatial activation patterns, learned end-to-end with clustering and sparsity regularization and modulated by an attention mechanism that reweights class-discriminative components, enabling efficient transfer to novel classes by recombining these reusable elements.
During meta-testing, unseen classes can be recognized by training only a simple classifier on the learned compositional features while keeping the backbone and dictionaries fixed.

verbatim_claims.text: Inspired by the compositional representation of ob- jects in humans, we train a neural network architecture that explicitly represents objects as a dictionary of shared com- ponents and their spatial composition.

In particular, dur- ing meta-learning, we train a knowledge base that consists of a dictionary of component representations and a dictio- nary of component activation maps that encode common spatial activation patterns of components.
During meta-testing, the representation of unseen classes is learned using the component representations and the com- ponent activation maps from the knowledge base.

We demon- strate the value of our interpretable compositional learn- ing framework for a few-shot classiﬁcation using miniIm- ageNet, tieredImageNet, CIFAR-FS, and FC100, where we achieve comparable performance.
We introduce CORL, a novel neural architecture for few-shot classiﬁcation that implements the inductive prior of compositional representation sharing.
It learns a knowledge base with component representations and their common spatial activation patterns, and re-uses this knowledge to learn efﬁciently about novel classes.
During meta-testing, our model learns representations of objects by composing them from the components and component activation maps of the knowledge base.
During meta-testing, we observed that it is sufﬁcient to train the classiﬁcation head only, while freezing the learned back- bone and knowledge base.

This is different from the major- ity of other meta-learning methods and highlights the strong generalization performance induced by integrating compo- sitional representation sharing into neural networks.
To the best of our knowledge, we are the ﬁrst to study and demonstrate the effectiveness and interpretability of compositional representation learning on few-shot classiﬁcation.
We achieve comparable performance on several stan- dard benchmarks, outperforming many recent complex optimization methods.
Inspired by the compositional representation of objects in humans, we introduce CORL, a novel neural architecture for few-shot classiﬁcation that learns through compositional representation sharing.

In particular, CORL learns a knowledge base that contains a dictionary of com- ponent representations and a dictionary of component acti- vation maps that encode frequent spatial activation patterns of components.
During meta-testing, this knowledge is reused to learn about unseen classes from very few samples.
Our extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our method, which achieves comparable performance on four popular few-shot classiﬁcation benchmarks.

qa_by_claim.claim: Few-shot image classification can be performed by representing objects as compositions of a shared dictionary of component embeddings and a shared dictionary of prototypical component spatial activation patterns, learned end-to-end with clustering and sparsity regularization and modulated by an attention mechanism that reweights class-discriminative components, enabling efficient transfer to novel classes by recombining these reusable elements.

[A] Use two shared dictionaries (component embeddings and prototypical component spatial activation patterns) learned end-to-end with clustering and sparsity, plus attention that reweights discriminative components to enable recombination-based transfer. [B] Use per-class, non-shared component dictionaries trained without clustering or sparsity and without attention, with transfer achieved only by full fine-tuning rather than recombination. [C] Use a single dictionary of holistic image prototypes without spatial activation patterns and without attention, with transfer not based on recombining reusable elements. [D] Use two shared dictionaries learned in separate stages without clustering or sparsity, no attention reweighting, and transfer that does not rely on recombination.

[A] Class-specific templates trained without clustering or sparsity, with attention suppressing discriminative components and transfer via full fine-tuning. [B] A single shared dictionary of spatial activation patterns only, with attention reweighting background components, and transfer not achieved through recombination. [C] Two shared dictionaries—component embeddings and prototypical component spatial activation patterns—learned end-to-end with clustering and sparsity, with attention reweighting class-discriminative components to enable recombination-based transfer to novel classes. [D] Many-shot image classification using per-class embeddings not learned end-to-end, without clustering or sparsity and without attention, with transfer requiring retraining rather than recombination.

paper_title: Event Distortion-Based Clustering Algorithm for Energy Harvesting Wireless Sensor Networks
paper_claims: Distortion-aware K-means clustering in RF energy-harvesting wireless sensor networks with spatially correlated observations, using MMSE-based single-hop and two-hop distortion models, selects only essential reporting nodes and designates the rest as relay heads while meeting a target reconstruction distortion.

Two-hop clustered relaying reduces energy consumption and extends network lifetime compared with single-hop direct transmission, at the cost of higher reconstruction distortion arising from the additional relay channel noise.

Exploiting ambient RF energy harvesting within the distortion-aware clustering framework increases the number of alive nodes and prolongs operation duration as harvested power increases without violating distortion constraints.

verbatim_claims.text: In this paper, the event distortion-based clustering (EDC) algorithm is proposed for the spatially correlated sensor nodes.
The EDC algorithm allows the energy-harvesting sensor nodes to select and eliminate nonessential nodes while maintain an acceptable level of distortion at the BS.
As a result, EDC algorithm can successfully exploit both the spatial correlation and energy harvesting to improve the energy efficiency while preserving an acceptable level of distortion.
Furthermore, the performance comparisons reveal that the two-hop communication model outperforms the single-hop model in terms of the distortion and energy-efficiency.
The operations of the EDC algorithm are given for both single-hop and two-hop communication models.
For each of those models, a different distortion function is derived and employed within the EDC algorithm, to verify the reliability performance level.
The results show that the two-hop communication model outperforms the single-hop communication model in terms of network lifetime.
The two-hop model is simply having two links (i.e., two channels between the CMs and BS), and hence, higher distortion level is observed than one-hop model.
Furthermore, it is shown that the EDC algorithm capability successfully utilize the energy harvesting such that the network lifetime can be improved as the power of the source (cell tower) increases.

paper_title: A machine learning based sentient multimedia framework to increase safety at work
paper_claims: An integrated sentient multimedia architecture that combines edge machine-learning wearable personal devices, fixed area devices, and a cloud-based safety coordination platform enables real-time workplace safety by fusing multi-source sensor data, classifying hazardous events such as falls from accelerometer and gyroscope signals using low-pass filtering with statistical features, and issuing coordinated alarms and guidance through predictive and prescriptive analytics.

verbatim_claims.text: In this paper, we propose a framework that uses both Sentient Multimedia Systems and Machine Learning to support safety in the workplace.
As for this application scenario, we describe a Machine Learning based wearable device for fall detection that we designed, built and tested.
Moreover, we illustrate a safety coordination platform for monitoring the work environment, activating alarms in case of falls, and sending appropriate advices to help workers involved in falls.
The two technologies mentioned above, i.e. Sentient Multimedia Systems and Machine Learning, represent the foundations of the framework for safety in the workplace that we propose in this paper.
The design of the framework proposed in this paper is done at an abstraction level that allows it to be used in any working context and to address any safety issue.
Our framework adopts a new, very advanced wearable device, based on Machine Learning, which we designed, built and tested and that we describe in detail in this paper.
Finally, it adopts an appropriate chain of Machine Learning based modules for the management of the Safety Coordination Platform.

Instead, the definition of the framework, as well as the description of its specialization to fall detection, as far as Area Devices and Safety Coordination Platform are concerned, are specific to this paper.

qa_by_claim.claim: An integrated sentient multimedia architecture that combines edge machine-learning wearable personal devices, fixed area devices, and a cloud-based safety coordination platform enables real-time workplace safety by fusing multi-source sensor data, classifying hazardous events such as falls from accelerometer and gyroscope signals using low-pass filtering with statistical features, and issuing coordinated alarms and guidance through predictive and prescriptive analytics.

[A] An integrated architecture that combines edge ML wearables and fixed area devices with a cloud safety coordination platform, fuses heterogeneous sensor streams, detects hazardous events (such as falls) from accelerometer and gyroscope data via low-pass filtering and statistical features, and delivers coordinated alarms and guidance using predictive and prescriptive analytics in real time. [B] A cloud-only setup that analyzes a single sensor stream with high-pass filtering, flags general activities offline, and sends uncoordinated notifications without guidance. [C] A wearable-only approach without fixed area devices or cloud coordination that reads only accelerometer data, thresholds raw signals without features, and generates delayed reports. [D] An integrated device network that provides descriptive dashboards, emphasizes routine activity classification instead of hazardous event detection, and issues independent device alerts without coordinated guidance.

Choose the correct option (A–D): Which description aligns with a sentient multimedia architecture that enables real-time workplace safety by combining edge machine-learning wearables, fixed area devices, and a cloud-based safety coordination platform to fuse multi-source sensor data, classify hazardous events such as falls from accelerometer and gyroscope signals using low-pass filtering with statistical features, and issue coordinated alarms and guidance via predictive and prescriptive analytics? [C] Fixed area devices without edge wearables, high-pass filtering with heuristic rules to identify general motion patterns, and periodic summaries instead of real-time guidance. [D] All three tiers present but no data fusion, omission of hazardous event classification, and exclusive use of descriptive analytics to provide dashboards without coordinated alarms.

paper_title: Social Influence Under Uncertainty in Interaction with Peers, Robots and Computers
paper_claims: In perceptual decision-making without accuracy feedback, prior beliefs about an agent’s nature bias reliance on its judgments, with social robots initially exerting stronger influence than humans or computers despite identical estimate distributions, and this bias weakens with repeated exposure to the partner’s responses.

In the absence of overt socially relevant signals, susceptibility to influence in human–human interaction is shaped by social normative dynamics beyond perceived competence, whereas susceptibility in human–robot interaction is governed primarily by informational assessments of the robot’s competence.

verbatim_claims.text: Results show participants’ belief about the nature of their partner biased their compliance with its judgments: participants were more inﬂuenced by the social robotthan human and computer partners.

This difference emerged strongly at the very beginning of the task and decreased with repeated exposure to empirical feedback on the partner’s responses, disclosing the role of prior beliefs in social inﬂuence under uncertainty.

Furthermore, the results of our functional task suggest an important difference between human–human and human–robot interaction in the absence of overt socially relevant signal from the partner: the former is modulated by socialnormative mechanisms, whereas the latter is guided by purely informational mechanisms linked to the perceived competence of the partner.
The present study aims at investigating how humans perceive and use information on other agents’ behavior inthe presence of uncertainty about their performance.
In this regard, no study to our knowledge compared directly our willingness to follow the opinions of human peers and social robots in judgment under uncertainty.
Importantly, our study will provide novel insights on how robots, when compared to humans, can be perceived and trusted in the absence of precise feedback about their reliabilityand performance.

paper_title: Image classification-based brain tumour tissue segmentation
paper_claims: Combining CNN-derived features from a transfer-learned CIFAR architecture with a per-voxel histogram-based texture descriptor that encodes local neighborhood dependencies and normalized intensities, followed by decision tree classification, improves voxel-wise brain tumor tissue segmentation accuracy and boundary delineation in multi-modal MRI relative to using CNN features alone while maintaining low model complexity.

verbatim_claims.text: The CIFAR network is modified to extract CNN-based features, and histogram-based texture features are fused to compensate the limitation in the CIFAR network.
These features together with the pixel intensities of the original MRI images are sent to a decision tree for classifying the MRI image voxels into different types of tumour tissues.
Experiments show that the proposed method produces promising segmentation results.
1. A novel histogram-based texture descriptor is developed for each pixel of interest within each MRI modality, which is based on Otsu’s image thresholding method.

2. A new method is proposed to address the limitation in CIFAR network and increase the performance of brain tumour segmentation by combining the hand-designed features with the machine-learned features that are extracted from the last fully connected layer of the CIFAR architecture.
3. A decision tree-based method is introduced for accurate brain tumour segmentation based on the combined features together with the image intensities.
To overcome these drawbacks, we have combined the hand-crafted features (the histogram-based texture features) and the machine-learned features to compensate the limitation in the local dependencies.

Overall, the combination of the histogram-based texture features as hand-crafted features technique with the machine-learned features can produce the best possible result masks that close to the expert’s delineation across all the glioma grades, leading to faster brain tumour diagnosis and treatment plan for patients.

paper_title: AI-based smart prediction of clinical disease using random forest classifier and Naive Bayes
paper_claims: Across Pima diabetes, Framingham coronary heart disease, and Wisconsin breast cancer datasets, random forest classification applied after missing-value handling and correlation-based feature pruning achieves higher predictive accuracy than Naive Bayes and clustering baselines (K-means and DBSCAN), consistent with random forest’s robustness to high-dimensional features and feature interactions and its mitigation of single-tree overfitting.

verbatim_claims.text: The artificial intelligence has been used with Naive Bayes classification and random forest classification algorithm to classify many disease datasets like diabetes, heart disease, and cancer to check whether the patient is affected by that disease or not.
The results of the simulations show the effectiveness of the classification techniques on a dataset, as well as the nature and complexity of the dataset used.

It has been trained the Naive Bayes and random forest classifier model with three different disease datasets namely—diabetes, coronary heart disease and cancer datasets and performance of each model are calculated.
Random forest algorithm provides better prediction accuracy compared with the Naïve Bayes algorithm.
In addition, it has been applied few sample test data of the three diseases to those classified models to show whether the patient data in sample test are suffering from that disease or not [6 ].
The accuracy outcome of Random forest model for the three diseases is greater than the accuracy values of Naïve Bayes classifier.

Finally, the proposed algorithms are compared against K-means clustering algorithm and DBSCAN algorithm for identifying the effectiveness, and the result graph shows that the random forest algorithm works well compared with other two algorithms.
When performing classification in the trained model by applying sample test data of each disease, the random forest model gives accurate results.

paper_title: Analysis of the evolution and collaboration networks of citizen science scientific publications
paper_claims: Citizen science publications indexed in Web of Science have expanded rapidly across disciplines, and their co‑authorship network is highly modular and clustered with a relatively small connected core, indicating many medium‑sized, largely country‑homogeneous communities linked by a few central cross‑cutting researchers.

verbatim_claims.text: Thus, we address the study of the citizen science expansion and evolution in terms of the properties of the graphs which encode relations between scientists by studying co-authorship and the consequent networks of collaboration.

This approach - not used until now in research on citizen science, as far as we know- allows us to analyze the properties of these networks through graph theory, and complement the existing quantitative research.
The aim of this article is to analyze the evolution and collaboration networks of citizen science publications, specifically those published in WoS journals.

As it has been demonstrated in previous studies (see Follett and Strezov 2015; Kullenberg and Kasperowski 2016; Bautista-Puig et al. 2019)—and will also be proved in this paper—the expansion of citizen science, as a scientific methodology, is reflected particularly in the exponential growth of citizen science publications in indexed journals for the last two decades.
With these tools we are able to define an empirical growth law for the total scientific production of citizen science groups.

This approach - not used until now in research on citi- zen science, as far as we know - allows us to analyse the properties of the corresponding graphs, completing the existing quantitative research.

paper_title: Cited text span identification for scientific summarisation using pre-trained encoders
paper_claims: Fine-tuning pre-trained transformer encoders on in-domain unlabelled text or augmented citing–cited sentence pairs improves cited text span identification compared with using generic pre-trained encoders without domain adaptation.

Providing only the abstract and identified cited text spans of a reference article as input to a BERT-based extractive summariser can match or surpass the summary quality obtained from the full text while reducing input length.

verbatim_claims.text: We present our approach for the identification of cited text spans in scientific literature, using pre-trained encoders (BERT) in combination with different neural networks.
We show that identifying and fine-tuning the language models on unlabelled or augmented domain specific data can improve the performance of cited text span identification models.
For the scientific summarisation we implement an extractive summarisation model adapted from BERT.
We observe that in certain experiments, by using only the cited text-spans we can achieve better performance, while minimising the input size needed.
The results show that in many configurations, performance is comparable between two scenarios on ROUGE-1 and ROUGE-L scores but using cited text spans consistently yields better ROUGE-2 scores.

paper_title: Hierarchical sparse Cholesky decomposition with applications to high-dimensional spatio-temporal filtering
paper_claims: A hierarchical Vecchia approximation defined by conditioning each hierarchical group on its nested ancestors yields a Gaussian-process model whose covariance and precision matrices have Cholesky factors that are both sparse, share the same sparsity pattern, and satisfy that the precision factor equals the inverse transpose of the covariance factor.

Under independent Gaussian observation noise, the posterior covariance and precision Cholesky factors induced by the hierarchical Vecchia prior retain exactly the same sparsity patterns as the prior factors.

The covariance Cholesky factor implied by the hierarchical Vecchia approximation equals the incomplete Cholesky factorization of the exact covariance restricted to the specified sparsity pattern, enabling inference that scales as O(n N^2) time and O(n N) memory when conditioning sets have size at most N.

Combining the hierarchical Vecchia structure with a Laplace approximation for exponential-family likelihoods yields a Newton–Raphson-based algorithm that exploits the same sparsity and converges under concavity to a Gaussian approximation at the posterior mode.

Embedding the hierarchical Vecchia structure into linear and linearized spatiotemporal state-space models produces Kalman–Vecchia–Laplace filters that preserve sparsity through forecast and update steps and scale approximately linearly with state dimension given sparse temporal evolution.

verbatim_claims.text: We propose a hierarchical V ecchia approximation, whose conditional-independence assumptions imply sparsity in the Cholesky factors of both the precision and the covariance matrix.
This remarkable property is crucial for applications to high-dimensional spatiotemporalﬁltering.

We present a fast and simple algorithm to compute our hierarchical V ecchia approximation, and we provide extensions to nonlinear data assimilation with non-Gaussian data based on the Laplace approximation.
In several numerical comparisons, including a ﬁltering analysis of satellite data, our methods strongly outperformed alternative approaches.

We show that these assumptions are simultaneously satis-ﬁed for a particular Gaussian-process approximation that we call hierarchical V ecchia (HV), which is a special case of the general V ecchia approximation (Katzfuss and Guin-ness 2021 ) based on hierarchical domain partitioning (e.g., Katzfuss 2017 ; Katzfuss and Gong 2020 ).
We show that the HV approximation can be computed using a simple and fast incomplete Cholesky decomposition.
We provide a scalable HV-based ﬁlter for linear Gaussian spatiotemporal state-space models, which is related to the multi-resolution ﬁlter of Jurek and Katzfuss ( 2021 ).
Further, by combining HV with a Laplace approximation (cf. Zilber and Katzfuss 2021 ), our method can be used for the analy- sis of non-Gaussian data.
For a given formulation of HV , the computational cost of all of our algorithms scales linearly in the state dimension, assumingsufﬁciently sparse temporal evolution.

paper_title: Ensemble Kalman filter based sequential Monte Carlo sampler for sequential Bayesian inference
paper_claims: In sequential Bayesian parameter estimation with additive Gaussian observation noise, defining the SMCS forward kernel via the Ensemble Kalman filter as a Gaussian centered at the previous particle plus a Kalman-gain–weighted innovation with covariance given by propagated observation noise (with small jitter), and pairing it with a Gaussian approximation to the optimal backward kernel fitted from the previous ensemble, yields a tractable sampler whose proposal marginal approximates the current posterior whenever the previous proposal approximates the previous posterior.

An adaptive weight refinement scheme that uses inexpensive approximate weights based on Gaussian surrogates and triggers exact weight computation via an effective-sample-size threshold reduces forward model evaluations while preserving exact importance weights at refinement steps.

verbatim_claims.text: In this work, we present a method that constructs the kernels of SMCS using an EnKF formulation, and we demonstrate the performance of the method with numerical examples.
Building upon similar ideas, we propose in this work to construct the kernels in SMCS by using an EnKF framework.
Specifically, the forward kernel is obtained directly from an EnKF approximation, and the backward kernel is derived by making a Gaussian approximation of the optimal backward kernel.
In this work, we propose a sampling method to compute the posterior distribution that arises in sequential Bayesian inference problems.

The method is based on SMCS, which seeks to generate weighted samples from the posterior in a sequential manner, and specifically, we propose to construct the forward kernel in SMCS using an EnKF framework and also derive a backward kernel associated with it.
With numerical examples, we demonstrate that the EnKF-SMCS method can often yield more accurate estimations than the direct use of either SMCS or EnKF for a class of problems.

qa_by_claim.claim: In sequential Bayesian parameter estimation with additive Gaussian observation noise, defining the SMCS forward kernel via the Ensemble Kalman filter as a Gaussian centered at the previous particle plus a Kalman-gain–weighted innovation with covariance given by propagated observation noise (with small jitter), and pairing it with a Gaussian approximation to the optimal backward kernel fitted from the previous ensemble, yields a tractable sampler whose proposal marginal approximates the current posterior whenever the previous proposal approximates the previous posterior.

mcq.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): In sequential Bayesian parameter estimation with additive Gaussian observation noise, a tractable sampler is obtained by defining the SMCS forward kernel via the Ensemble Kalman filter as a Gaussian centered at the previous particle plus a Kalman-gain–weighted innovation with covariance given by propagated observation noise with small jitter and pairing it with a Gaussian approximation to the optimal backward kernel fitted from the previous ensemble, which combination below reflects this construction and its consequence that the proposal marginal approximates the current posterior whenever the previous proposal approximates the previous posterior? [A] Forward kernel: EnKF-based Gaussian with Kalman-gain–weighted innovation and propagated observation-noise covariance plus small jitter; backward kernel: Gaussian approximation to the optimal fitted from the previous ensemble; consequence: proposal marginal approximates the current posterior conditional on the previous proposal approximating the previous posterior. [B] Forward kernel: Laplace distribution centered at zero with fixed covariance; backward kernel: none; consequence: proposal marginal approximates the current posterior regardless of previous-stage quality. [C] Forward kernel: bootstrap particle filter transition with resampling only; backward kernel: uniform; consequence: proposal marginal matches the current posterior only asymptotically in infinite ensembles. [D] Forward kernel: EnKF-based Gaussian using prior covariance and no jitter; backward kernel: exact optimal kernel assumed known; consequence: proposal marginal equals the current posterior in all cases.

Choose the correct option (A–D): In sequential Bayesian parameter estimation with additive Gaussian observation noise, when the SMCS forward kernel is defined via the Ensemble Kalman filter as a Gaussian centered at the previous particle plus a Kalman-gain–weighted innovation with covariance given by propagated observation noise with small jitter and is paired with a Gaussian approximation to the optimal backward kernel fitted from the previous ensemble, under what condition does the resulting tractable sampler have a proposal marginal that approximates the current posterior? [A] Always, regardless of the quality of the previous proposal. [B] Only when the observation noise is heavy-tailed and non-Gaussian. [C] Whenever the previous proposal approximates the previous posterior. [D] Only in exactly linear-Gaussian models with an exact Kalman filter.

paper_title: Equilibrium: an elasticity controller for parallel tree search in the cloud
paper_claims: Maintaining a user-defined target efficiency for elastic parallel tree search in the cloud is achieved by monitoring workload efficiency—a runtime measure of the fraction of essential computation that approximates elastic efficiency—and dynamically scaling processing units to meet this target, which thereby enforces a constant cost-per-problem-size ratio across runs.

verbatim_claims.text: We show that their characteristics render static resource provisioning inappropriate and the capability of elastic scaling desirable.

Moreover, we discuss how to construct an elasticity controller that reasons about the scaling behavior of a parallel system at runtime and dynamically adapts the number of processing units according to user-defined cost and efficiency thresholds.
Our experimental results show that, by means of elastic scaling, the performance can be controlled according to user-defined thresholds, which cannot be achieved with static resource provisioning.

•  We discuss how to construct an elasticity controller for parallel tree search applications that dynamically adapts the number of processing units according to user-defined cost and efficiency thresholds.
Based on our findings, we present Equilibrium—an elasticity controller that enables efficiency-based and cost-based elasticity control for parallel tree search applications in Sect. 5.

paper_title: Using autoencoders for session-based job recommendations
paper_claims: Learning latent representations of anonymous job-browsing sessions with autoencoder architectures (classical, denoising, and variational) from interaction data alone or combined with content features and using these embeddings for k-nearest-neighbor retrieval yields session-based job recommendations that maintain high coverage, achieve competitive accuracy, and increase system- and session-level novelty relative to established baselines.

verbatim_claims.text: We propose a recom-mendation approach, which uses different autoencoder architectures to encode ses-sions from the job domain.
The inferred latent session representations are then used in a k-nearest neighbor manner to recommend jobs within a session.

Our results show that autoencoders provide relevant job recommendations as well as maintain a high coverage and, at the same time, can outperform state-of-the-art session-based recommendation techniques in terms of system-based and session-based novelty.
Our results show that variational autoencod-ers provide competitive job recommendations in terms of accuracy compared to the state-of-the-art session-based recommendation algorithms.
We find that autoencoders can produce more novel and surpris-ing recommendations compared to the baselines and, at the same time, provide relevant jobs for the user while maintaining a high coverage.
