paper_title: Human segmentation in surveillance video with deep learning
paper_claims: A deep encoder–decoder convolutional network trained on composited images of green-screen-captured human shapes over site-specific background frames can automatically segment people in surveillance videos without requiring a static camera, constrained subject motion, or auxiliary annotations such as pose or contours.

Applying a bilateral filter to synthetic training composites to attenuate high-frequency foreground–background edge discrepancies improves generalization to real indoor and outdoor images by better matching natural boundary transitions.
Inverse-frequency weighting of classes in the cross-entropy loss effectively mitigates foreground–background imbalance and enhances foreground pixel classification.

An architecture with a VGG-16–initialized encoder and a decoder that upsamples via unpooling using stored pooling indices enables memory-efficient dense pixel classification suitable for video surveillance streams.

verbatim_claims.text: This paper proposes an alternative approach for human segmentation in videos through the use of a deep convolutional neural network.

Two specific datasets were created to train our network, using the shapes of 35 different moving actors arranged on background images related to the area where the camera is located, allowing the network to take advantage of the entire site chosen for video surveillance.

The results show that the main benefit of our method is the possibility to automatically recognize and segment people in videos without constraints on camera and people movements in the scene (Video, code and datasets are available at http://graphics.unibas.it/www/HumanSegmentation/index.md.html ).
In this paper, we propose an alternative approach for human segmentation in videos through the use of a deep convolutional neural network (CNN).
To train our network, two specific datasets were created and combined with the background captured from the monitored area of the surveillance camera.
Furthermore, our network output can also be used for other applications, for example, to quickly generate an accurate trimap on real images with people in the foreground.

paper_title: A deep-learning model for urban traffic flow prediction with traffic events mined from twitter
paper_claims: Data-level fusion of traffic measurements, weather variables, and geo-specific Twitter signals about traffic events—filtered to reliable road-traffic organization accounts—within a deep bidirectional LSTM stacked autoencoder improves multi-step short-term urban traffic flow prediction accuracy relative to traffic-only approaches, particularly under non-recurring disruptions.

verbatim_claims.text: This paper proposes a deep learning urban traffic prediction model that combines information extracted from tweet messages with traffic and weather information.

The findings from extensive empirical analysis using real-world data demonstrate the effectiveness of the approach in improving prediction accuracy when compared to other classical/statistical and machine learning (ML) state-of-the-art models.

The contributions of this research are two-fold: (1) assessing empirically if the inclusion of tweets in addition to weather and traffic datasets improve urban traffic flow prediction, and (2)an end-to-end deep bi-directional LSTM autoencoder traffic flow prediction model implemen-tation that uses tweet, weather, and traffic datasets for model training.
The findings from the study showed asignificant improvement in the model prediction accuracy when information for Twitter feedswas included.

qa_by_claim.claim: Data-level fusion of traffic measurements, weather variables, and geo-specific Twitter signals about traffic events—filtered to reliable road-traffic organization accounts—within a deep bidirectional LSTM stacked autoencoder improves multi-step short-term urban traffic flow prediction accuracy relative to traffic-only approaches, particularly under non-recurring disruptions.

paper_title: Fast and Accurate Edge Computing Energy Modeling and DVFS Implementation in GEM5 Using System Call Emulation Mode
paper_claims: An OS-independent DVFS capability in GEM5’s system call emulation mode, triggered by application-issued pseudo-instructions that switch among predefined CPU clock domains, combined with an architecture-agnostic energy model that decomposes dynamic and leakage power and parameterizes effective capacitance by workload class calibrated from hardware measurements—while accounting for DVFS transition energy, sleep modes, and systematic offset corrections—enables fast, accurate energy estimation for microcontroller-class edge systems using only GEM5 instruction-mix and activity statistics.

verbatim_claims.text:
In this paper, we propose a non-intrusive application controlled DVFS management implementation in the
GEM5 simulator, used with GEM5’s system call emulation mode.
We also propose a novel architecture independent energy model based on categorization of different measurable workload classes.
Together with the GEM5 output statistics, the model accurately estimates the total energy consumption of our simulated system.
The results from our modified GEM5 simulator are validated with representative signal processing applications.
Our contributions in this paper can easily be tailored to other processor models in GEM5 and to future versions of GEM5.
It will therefore enable system architects to explore new techniques and compare the improvements relative to existing architectures.

As illustrated in Fig. 1, the main contribution of this paper is 1) a non-intrusive application controlled DVFS management implementation in GEM5 SE mode, and 2) an architecture independent energy model based on classification of different measurable workload classes.
The energy model is parametrized by running a calibration application once on real hardware.
The contributions enable efficient use of CA simulators in design exploration for energy optimization of ultra-low power edge computing systems.
To our knowledge, no published DVFS implementations exist in GEM5 SE mode or for application controlled DVFS in GEM5.
In our model, we separate different types of workloads into measurable classes, which are parametrized in our power formulas.
The only information needed by the energy model script from the GEM5 simulator are the workload related statistics.
Together this makes the setup fully reusable in a user-friendly manner for other processor platforms.

qa_by_claim.claim: An OS-independent DVFS capability in GEM5’s system call emulation mode, triggered by application-issued pseudo-instructions that switch among predefined CPU clock domains, combined with an architecture-agnostic energy model that decomposes dynamic and leakage power and parameterizes effective capacitance by workload class calibrated from hardware measurements—while accounting for DVFS transition energy, sleep modes, and systematic offset corrections—enables fast, accurate energy estimation for microcontroller-class edge systems using only GEM5 instruction-mix and activity statistics.

mcq.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): An OS-independent DVFS capability in GEM5’s system call emulation mode, triggered by application-issued pseudo-instructions that switch among predefined CPU clock domains, combined with an architecture-agnostic energy model that decomposes dynamic and leakage power, parameterizes effective capacitance by workload class calibrated from hardware measurements, and accounts for DVFS transition energy, sleep modes, and systematic offset corrections, enables fast, accurate energy estimation for microcontroller-class edge systems using only GEM5 instruction-mix and activity statistics. Which option correctly matches this description? [A] OS-independent DVFS in GEM5 SE mode via pseudo-instructions switching predefined CPU clock domains plus an architecture-agnostic model decomposing dynamic/leakage with workload-class calibrated capacitance that accounts for transition energy, sleep modes, and systematic offsets, enabling fast, accurate energy estimation for microcontroller-class edge systems using only GEM5 instruction-mix and activity statistics [B] OS-dependent DVFS in GEM5 full-system mode via OS governors switching unspecified domains plus an architecture-specific model considering only dynamic power without calibration or transition/sleep/offset handling, requiring RTL traces and targeting server-class CPUs [C] OS-independent DVFS in GEM5 SE mode via pseudo-instructions switching memory voltage domains plus an architecture-agnostic model that ignores leakage and transition energy and requires per-ISA capacitance constants, producing estimates for GPU accelerators using simulator microarchitectural events and OS traces [D] OS-independent DVFS in GEM5 SE mode via pseudo-instructions with an architecture-agnostic power model, but needing OS kernel power models and not considering sleep modes or offsets, yielding only rough latency estimates rather than energy for embedded systems

Choose the correct option (A–D): Fast, accurate energy estimation for microcontroller-class edge systems is achieved by pairing OS-independent DVFS in GEM5’s system call emulation mode—triggered by application-issued pseudo-instructions that switch among predefined CPU clock domains—with an architecture-agnostic energy model that decomposes dynamic and leakage power, parameterizes effective capacitance by workload class calibrated from hardware measurements, and accounts for DVFS transition energy, sleep modes, and systematic offset corrections, using only GEM5 instruction-mix and activity statistics. paper_title: A machine learning approach to univariate time series forecasting of quarterly earnings
paper_claims: An ε-support vector regression–based univariate forecasting model that derives features from original, first-differenced, and seasonally differenced quarterly earnings and is trained across multiple firms attains significantly higher predictive accuracy than the BR ARIMA model, particularly for short forecast horizons and when only limited historical data are available.

verbatim_claims.text: We propose our quarterly earnings prediction  (QEPSVR) model, which is based on epsi- lon support vector regression (ε-SVR), as a new univariate model for quarterly earnings forecasting.
Our results show that the predictive accuracy of the  QEPSVR model signifi- cantly exceeds that of the BR ARIMA model under 24 out of the 28 tested experiment con-ditions.
Furthermore, significance is achieved under all conditions considering short fore- cast horizons or limited availability of historic data.
Our  QEPSVR model retains the univariate character of the BR ARIMA model, mean- ing all predictive features are derivable from historic quarterly earnings series.
Unlike the BR ARIMA model however, the  QEP SVR model is fitted using the historic quarterly earn- ings of multiple firms.
Empirical evidence shows that under 24 out of 28 tested conditions, the predictive accuracy of the  QEPSVR model is significantly higher than that of the state-of-the-art BR ARIMA model.

Furthermore, the significant conditions include all those considering one and two-step-ahead pre- dictions ( PS∈{1, 2} ), as well as those for which only limited historic data is available ( ASL∈{6, 7, 8 } ).

paper_title: A combination of CSP-based method with soft margin SVM classifier and generalized RBF kernel for imagery-based brain computer interface applications
paper_claims: In motor imagery EEG classification, a pipeline using discriminative filter bank common spatial pattern features weighted by discriminative sensitive learning vector quantization and classified with a soft-margin support vector machine employing a generalized radial basis function kernel—optionally preceded by GRBF-based kernel linear discriminant analysis—achieves superior accuracy and robustness compared with Gaussian RBF SVMs, KPCA-based pipelines, k-nearest neighbor, and neural networks across datasets.

verbatim_claims.text: A discriminative filter bank of CSP method using a discriminative sensitive learning vector quantization (DFBCSP-DSLVQ) system is implemented.

Four algorithms are then combined to form three methods for improving the efficiency of the DFBCSP-DSLVQ method, namely the kernel linear discriminant analysis (KLDA), the kernel principal component analysis (KPCA), the soft margin support vector machine (SSVM) classifier and the generalized radial bases functions (GRBF) kernel.
The GRBF is used as a kernel for the KLDA, the KPCA feature selection algorithms and the SSVM classifier.
It is concluded that the algorithms, which are based on the SSVM-GRBF classifier and the KLDA with the SSVM-GRBF classifiers give sufficient accuracy and reliable results.

The procedure of this study is: recording nine EEG data based on the BCI competition III, dataset IVa (the designed task is the same as the BCI Competition III dataset IVa task); implementing the existed DFBCSP-DSLVQ method; applying a frequency sub-band selection method to find the best sub-bands; using the KLDA and KPCA algorithms, in which the kernel is GRBF, to select the features; and finally utilizing different combinations to classify the selected features, namely the SVM with the radial basis function (RBF) kernel (SVM-RBF), SVM with the GRBF kernel (SVM-GRBF), soft margin support vector machine (SSVM) with the RBF kernel (SSVM-RBF), SSVM with the GRBF kernel (SSVM-GRBF), the NN, and the K-nearest neighbor (K-NN).

paper_title: Bayesian estimation and likelihood-based comparison of agent-based volatility models
paper_claims: Bayesian estimation via Hamiltonian Monte Carlo of continuous-state approximations to agent-based financial market models enables rigorous parameter inference and predictive likelihood-based comparison, with mechanisms allowing persistent mispricing delivering predictive performance comparable to stochastic volatility benchmarks while exhibiting complex posterior geometries and limited parameter identifiability.

verbatim_claims.text: Here, we propose Hamiltonian Monte Carlo , an efﬁcient and scalable Markov chain Monte Carlo algorithm, as a general methodfor Bayesian inference of agent-based models.
In particular, we implement several models by Vikram and Sinha, Franke and Westerhoff and Alfarano, Lux and Wagner in Stan, an accessible probabilistic programming language for Bayesian modeling.
We also compare the performance of these models with standard econometric models of the GARCH and stochastic volatility families.

We ﬁnd that the best agent-basedmodels are on par with stochastic volatility models in terms of predictive likelihood, yet exhibit challenging posterior geometries requiring care in model comparison and sophisticated sampling algorithms.
In particular, this includes the model by Vikram & Sinha, which had not been ﬁtted before, as well as a novel moving average speciﬁcation for the model of Franke & Westerhoff.
Secondly, we imple-ment all models in Stan , a modern probabilistic programming language for Bayesian modeling.
Thirdly, we provide a detailed pairwise comparison of all models based on cross-validated predictive likelihoods.
In particular, we ﬁnd that the best agent- based models are competitive with standard econometric models.

While this has been observed previously for the Franke & Westerhoff model (Barde 2016 ), we provide evidence that other herding dynamics give comparable results provided that the model allows for persistent mispricing between fundamental and observed prices.

qa_by_claim.claim: Bayesian estimation via Hamiltonian Monte Carlo of continuous-state approximations to agent-based financial market models enables rigorous parameter inference and predictive likelihood-based comparison, with mechanisms allowing persistent mispricing delivering predictive performance comparable to stochastic volatility benchmarks while exhibiting complex posterior geometries and limited parameter identifiability.

mcq.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): When applying Bayesian estimation via Hamiltonian Monte Carlo to continuous-state approximations of agent-based financial market models, which statement is accurate regarding parameter inference, predictive likelihood-based model comparison, the predictive performance of mechanisms allowing persistent mispricing relative to stochastic volatility benchmarks, and posterior geometry and identifiability? [A] It enables rigorous parameter inference and predictive likelihood-based comparison; mechanisms allowing persistent mispricing deliver predictive performance comparable to stochastic volatility benchmarks; and the posteriors exhibit complex geometries with limited parameter identifiability. [B] It fails to support predictive likelihood-based comparison, mispricing mechanisms perform worse than stochastic volatility benchmarks, and posteriors are simple with well-identified parameters. [C] It relies on variational inference rather than Hamiltonian Monte Carlo, mispricing must be eliminated to match stochastic volatility benchmarks, and posterior geometry is trivial with strong identifiability. [D] It only applies to discrete-state agent-based models, delivers descriptive rather than rigorous parameter inference, and achieves superior predictive performance to stochastic volatility benchmarks with unimodal, well-identified posteriors.

Choose the correct option (A–D): Which combination best characterizes outcomes when using Bayesian Hamiltonian Monte Carlo on continuous-state approximations to agent-based financial market models, considering inference rigor, model comparison, mispricing mechanisms, and posterior/identifiability properties? [A] Non-Bayesian estimation, no predictive likelihood-based comparison, superior predictive performance to stochastic volatility benchmarks, and simple, well-identified posteriors. [B] Bayesian HMC with continuous-state approximations, rigorous parameter inference and predictive likelihood-based comparison, mispricing mechanisms with predictive performance comparable to stochastic volatility benchmarks, and complex posteriors with limited parameter identifiability. [C] Approximate Bayesian computation without predictive likelihoods, no reliable parameter inference, mispricing mechanisms lagging stochastic volatility benchmarks, and clear identifiability with simple posteriors. [D] Variational Bayes on discrete-state representations, comparable performance only without mispricing, and convex posteriors with uniquely identified parameters.

Bayesian estimation via Hamiltonian Monte Carlo of continuous-state approximations to agent-based financial market models does not enable predictive likelihood-based comparison, and mechanisms allowing persistent mispricing deliver inferior predictive performance to stochastic volatility benchmarks while yielding simple posterior geometries with strong parameter identifiability.

paper_title: Distributed deep learning platform for pedestrian detection on IT convergence environment
paper_claims: Combining data parallelism with parameter parallelism and using asynchronous parameter updates in a parameter-server architecture reduces synchronization overhead and accelerates distributed training for pedestrian detection relative to synchronous approaches.
Deploying distributed deep learning workloads in OS-level containers (such as LXD) yields higher resource utilization efficiency than hypervisor-based virtualization.

Co-locating a parameter server and a slave worker within the same VM on each physical node while avoiding multiple slaves per VM minimizes remote communication and improves overall training performance.

Training performance does not monotonically improve with additional parameter servers or slave workers, exhibiting a configuration-dependent optimum determined by model parameter size, data distribution, and network bandwidth.

verbatim_claims.text: Therefore, in this paper we shall present a distributed deep learning platform which can easily build a cluster, and execute deep learning process in the distributed cloud environment, while achieving performance improvement in various ways.
Our platform provides a convenient interface for easily and efficiently executing the deep learning process in a distributed environment by providing a multilayered system architecture.

Our system builds and utilizes computing power in easy and efficient way by leveraging container technique, so-called OS-level virtualization, rather than traditional hyper - visor-based virtualization.

In our system, we improve the whole performance by exploiting both of data and parameter parallelisms at once and reduce the synchroni-zation overhead by exploiting asynchronous communication for parameter updates.
Also, we propose an efficient resource allocation scheme for parameter servers and slaves which can improve the performance from the experiment.

In this paper we shall present a distributed deep learning platform which can easily build a cluster, and execute deep learning process fast in the distributed cloud environment while achieving performance improvements.

Our system utilizes computing power in easy and efficient way by providing interface for managing distributed resources and by leveraging con-tainer technique, so-called OS-level virtualization, rather than traditional hyper - visor-based virtualization.
First, we improve the whole performance by exploiting both of data and parameter parallelisms at once.
Next, we reduce the synchronization overhead by exploiting asynchronous com-munication for parameter updates.
Also, we propose an efficient resource alloca-tion scheme for parameter servers and slaves which can improve the performance from the experiment.
Therefore, we can reduce the network overhead by placing the parameter server and slave in each VM in one physical node.
Our system can easily build clustering and run distributed processing in a distributed environment using one simple command.
Our system utilizes computing power in easy and efficient way by leveraging container technique, so-called OS-level virtualization, rather than traditional hypervisor-based virtualization.
First, we have improved the whole per - formance by exploiting both of data and parameter parallelisms at once.
Next, we have reduced the synchronization overhead by exploiting asynchronous communica- tion for parameter updates.
We have suggested a way to improve performance by reducing the frequency of remote communication by placing parameter servers and slaves on the same physical node.
Experimental results have shown that our scheme for distributed deep learning provides higher performance on processing time by adopting asynchronous communication method.
In both experiments, asynchronous communication method has shown better perfor - mance than synchronous communication method.
In addition, the strategy of plac- ing parameter servers and slaves on each VM of a single physical node is able to boost performance.

paper_title: Graph matching beyond perfectly-overlapping Erdős–Rényi random graphs
paper_claims: A partially-overlapping correlated Bernoulli networks model with overlap parameter s and correlation parameter ρ generalizes correlated Erdős–Rényi settings by allowing missing vertices while preserving identical marginal distributions and controlled cross-graph edge correlations.

Matching vertices by comparing 1‑Wasserstein distances between degree profile distributions and connecting each vertex to its top‑d candidates accommodates partial overlap without enforcing a bijection and produces candidate match sets.

Seeding with high‑degree vertices and refining via iterative linear assignment that maximizes common neighbors converts candidate sets into one‑to‑one alignments and provides per‑vertex convergence diagnostics.
Combining spectral community detection based on ratios of eigenvectors with within‑community degree‑profile matching and post‑processing enables graph matching under stochastic block models.

Degree‑profile‑based matching operates in polynomial time with distance computation O(n^3ρ + n^2) and subsequent steps O(n^2) and remains effective in sparse graphs where spectral methods typically fail.

verbatim_claims.text: In this paper, we exploit the degree information, which was previously used only in noiseless graphs and perfectly-overlapping Erd˝ os–Rényi random graphs matching.
We propose the edge exploited degree proﬁle graph matching method and two reﬁned variations.
Our methods areproved to be numerically superior than the state-of-the-art methods.
We formally describe a partially-overlapping correlated Bernoulli networks model in Deﬁnition 1.
Further, we explore the degree proﬁle graph matching method for thenewly deﬁned partially-overlapping Erd˝ os–Rényi ran- dom graphs and also the stochastic block random graphs.
To the best of our knowledge, this is the ﬁrst work toexploit the degree proﬁle-type method in stochastic blockmodel graph matching problems.
We propose the edge exploited (EE) degree proﬁle graph matching method.
In addition, we propose reﬁned EE algorithms, including pre-processing and post-processing steps.
These proposed methods are demonstrated to out- perform the state-of-the-art methods when the graphs arepartially overlapping.
The degree proﬁle core of our methods enable us to con- duct graph matching for graphs with sparse connections,where the spectral-based methods usually fail.

As explained in Remark 3 in Ding et al. ( 2018 ), the time complexity of calculating Wis of order O(n3ρ+n2), where nandρrepresent the magnitude of the network sizes and the upper bound on the connectivity probabilities in bothnetworks, respectively.
The matrix W∈R nA×nBis stored once constructed, and the consequential storage cost is of order n2.
Algorithm 3is the main algorithm proposed in this paper.
If we do not care about the order of the top dmatches, then the time complexity of Algorithm 3is of order O(n2), otherwise, O(n2+ndlog(d)), using Introselect algorithms.
Algorithms 4and 5are two reﬁnement algorithms based on Algorithm 3, both of which have the most time-consuming step being the calculation of the matrix W.

paper_title: Math-word embedding in math search and semantic extraction
paper_claims: Math-specific word embeddings trained on mathematical corpora capture semantic relationships among mathematical terms and expressions and can support tasks such as term similarity, analogy search, concept modeling, and query expansion.

Representing mathematical expressions as streams of tokens enables learning of similarities among mathematical objects but is inadequate for reliably linking identifiers to textual definiens due to ambiguity and context dependence.

The lack of precise, canonical semantic specifications for identifiers and functions in MathML/OpenMath and the absence of standardized datasets and benchmarks are major obstacles to effective machine learning for mathematical information retrieval and semantic extraction.

Embedding entire equations as single tokens facilitates comparison of equations, whereas recovering the semantics of individual identifiers requires structural grouping and canonicalization beyond current embedding techniques.
Word2vec-style embeddings trained on math texts can exhibit non-symmetric similarity behavior, limiting reliability for automated end-to-end retrieval scenarios.

verbatim_claims.text: As math text consists of natural text, as well as math expressions that similarly exhibit linear correlation and contextual characteristics, word embedding techniques can also be applied to math documents.

In this paper, we explore math embedding by testing it on several different scenarios, namely, (1) math-term similarity, (2) analogy, (3) numerical concept-modeling based on the centroid of the key - words that characterize a concept, (4) math search using query expansions, and (5) seman-tic extraction, i.e., extracting descriptive phrases for math expressions.
Our results show that math embedding holds much promise for similarity, analogy, and search tasks.
However, we also observed the need for more robust math embedding approaches.

As math text consists of natural text as well as math expressions that exhibit linear and contextual correlation characteristics that are very similar to those of natural sen- tences, word embedding applies to math text much as it does to natural text.

Still, math expressions and math writing styles are different from natural text to the point that NLP techniques have to undergo significant adapta- tions and modifications to work well in math contexts.
We will show that the DLMF trained model is appro-priate to discover mathematical term similarities and term analogies, and to generate query expansions.
We examine the possible reasons why the word embedding models, trained on the arXiv dataset, does not present valuable results for this task.

[B] They are trained on general news corpora, capture only syntactic adjacency, and support sentiment analysis and headline classification. [C] They are trained on mixed-domain corpora, capture only frequency statistics, and support keyword matching and deduplication. [D] They are hand-crafted symbolic rules that do not model semantics and cannot support analogy search or query expansion.

paper_claims: Mapping visual features to congruent olfactory and auditory cues with derived haptic feedback in mulsemedia steers visual attention toward the corresponding visual attributes and produces higher physiological arousal alongside improved perceived quality of experience compared to video-only presentation.

verbatim_claims.text:
Accordingly, the eye gaze and heart rate results
showed significant influence of the cross-modall y mapped multisensorial effects on the users ’
QoE.

Our results highlight that when the olfact ory content is crossmodally congruent with the
visual content, the visual attention of the users seems shifted towards the correspondent visual
feature.

Crosmodally matched media is also shown to result in an enhanced QoE compared to
a video only condition.

mapped multisensorial effects (olfaction, so und, and auto-generated haptic) from visual
features of videos enhance the users ’QoE.

paper_claims: Cross-modally matched odours do not significantly enhance overall Quality of Experience compared with a single control scent in mulsemedia experiences that combine audiovisual, olfactory, and haptic stimuli.

verbatim_claims.text: Our results re veal that whilst users had a satisfyin g overall mulsemedia experience the specific use of cross modally matched odours did not result in significantly higher QoE levels than when a control scent (rosemary) was employed.
However, aspects of QoE are impacted upon by all individual differences dimensions considered in our study.

Accordingly, in this paper we are looking at multisensory interaction beyond the audio- visual and have added haptic and olfactory stimuli to the user experience, exploring how individual differences mediate this.

paper_title: PenQuest: a gamified attacker/defender meta model for cyber security assessment and education
paper_claims: A gamified attacker/defender meta model integrates service, information, and event layers with standardized knowledge bases (STIX, CAPEC, CVE/CWE) and NIST SP 800-53 controls to map concrete attack patterns and observable events to corresponding countermeasures for cyber risk assessment and education.

The rule system represents adversarial campaigns as a dynamic, asymmetric, non-cooperative, imperfect-information game with Bayesian elements, in which time-enabled actions affect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability status of interdependent services.

Parametric action definitions that include actor attributes, equipment effects, requirements, and success and detection chances, linked to attack patterns and event sequences, enable semantic correlation of monitoring data with attacker behavior and systematic selection of mitigating controls.

verbatim_claims.text: In this article, we introduce PenQuest, a meta model designed to present a complete view on information system attacks and their mitigation while providing a tool for both semantic data enrichment and security education.

PenQuest simulates time-enabled attacker/defender behavior as part of a dynamic, imperfect information multi-player game that derives signiﬁcant parts of its ruleset from established information security sources such as STIX, CAPEC, CVE/CWE and NIST SP 800-53.
Attack patterns, vulnerabilities, and mitigating controls are mapped to counterpart strategies and concrete actions through practical, data-centric mechanisms.
We implemented PenQuest as a physical serious game prototype and successfully tested it in a higher education environment.
Players can utilize the RPG to learn in an entertaining way about digital threats and how theymay affect different infrastructures.

PenQuest also provides an abstraction layer that helps assess the risk of targeted attacks on IT systems by providing an extensible frameworkfor simulating attacker and defender behavior in an adversar- ial setting.

Analysts and IT/security experts are provided with the means to depictconcrete hacks through attack patterns and cyber-observables and link them to the output of intrusion detection systems currently in place at their organization.

paper_title: Quality attribute trade-offs in the embedded systems industry: an exploratory case study
paper_claims: Trade-offs between run-time and design-time qualities are common in embedded systems projects and are often implicit because teams lack adequate monitoring tools and practices.
Embedded systems practitioners manage quality attribute trade-offs primarily through lightweight, ad-hoc practices rather than formal processes or dedicated tooling to minimize overhead.
Continuous automated monitoring of relevant quality attributes enables explicit and prudent trade-off decisions and reduces the risk of accumulating technical debt in embedded systems.

Available tools in the embedded systems domain focus on individual quality attributes and provide little integrated support for managing trade-offs across attributes, creating demand for dedicated trade-off management tooling.

verbatim_claims.text: The interviewed subjects showed a preference for run-time over design-time qualities.
Trade-offs between design-time and run-time qualities are very common, but they are often implicit, due to the lack of adequate monitoring tools and practices.
Practitioners prefer to deal with trade-offs in the most lightweight way possible, by applying ad-hoc prac- tices, thus avoiding any overhead incurred.

Although it is notoriously difficult to deal with trade-offs, constantly monitoring the quality attributes of interest with automated tools is key in making explicit and prudent trade-offs and mitigating the risk of incurring technical debt.

We begin to address this problem through an exploratory case study investigating how embedded systems engineers manage trade-offs between run-time and design-time quality attributes and what kind of support they require.
Our findings shed light on which qualities are prioritised in the studied domain, what kind of trade-offs occur, how these trade-offs take place in practice, and how they should ideally take place.

paper_title: Deep Insights into Convolutional Networks for Video Recognition
paper_claims: Cross-stream fusion in two-stream convolutional architectures induces units that integrate appearance and motion into coherent spatiotemporal features rather than independent cues.
The learned representation comprises both highly class-specific local detectors and generic units shared across classes, yielding distributed codes for action recognition.

With increasing network depth, feature selectivity becomes more abstract and increasingly invariant to nuisance factors such as motion speed, with motion units tuned to direction while tolerating a range of speeds and accelerations.

Early motion-stream layers implement spatiotemporal derivative-like filtering with temporally low-pass characteristics, whereas later layers capture higher-frequency temporal dynamics and exhibit stronger dependence on spatiotemporal regularization.

During supervised training, early-layer spatial filter structure remains stable while temporal selectivity progressively emerges, whereas higher-layer spatiotemporal filters undergo substantial structural changes.

verbatim_claims.text: We visualize multiple two-stream architectures to show that local detectors for appearance and motion objects arise to form distributed representations for recognizing human actions.
First, cross-stream fusion enables the learning of true spatiotemporal features rather than simply separate appearance and motion features.
Second,the networks can learn local representations that are highly class speciﬁc, but also generic representations that can serve a range of classes.

Third, throughout the hierarchy of the network, features become more abstract and show increasing invariance to aspects of the data that are unimportant to desired distinctions (e.g. motion patterns across various speeds).
Fourth, visualizations can be used not only to shed light on learned representations, but also to reveal idiosyncrasies of training data and to explain failure cases of the system.
In this paper we shed light on deep spatiotemporal networks by visualizing what excites the learned models using activation maximization by backpropagating on the input.

Our visual explanations are highly intuitive and pro- vide qualitative support for the beneﬁts of separating into two pathways of appearance (RGB image) and motion (optical-ﬂow) information when processing spatiotemporalinformation—a principle that has also been found in nature where numerous studies suggest a corresponding separation into ventral and dorsal pathways of the brain (Mishkin et al.1983 ; Goodale and Milner 1992 ; Felleman and van Essen 1991 ) as well as the existence of cross-pathway connections (Saleem et al. 2000 ; Kourtzi and Kanwisher 2000 ).

First, we augment our earlier activation maximization analysis of what the networks learnwith response histograms across test data associated with training labels to lend additional insight into what has been learned by units within the networks.
Second, we showexperiments on the evolution of ﬁlters throughout training.

Third, we investigate several additional architectures, Spa- tiotemporal Residual Networks (Feichtenhofer et al. 2016a ) using ResNet50 (He et al. 2016 ) streams, Temporal Seg- ment Networks (Wang et al. 2016 ) using BN-Inception (Ioffe and Szegedy 2015 ) streams, and Two-Stream net- works (Simonyan and Zisserman 2014 ) using Inception_v3 (Szegedy et al. 2015 ) streams, as well as the originally studied VGG16 two-stream fusion model (Feichtenhofer et al. 2016b ).

Fourth, we investigate the impact of train- ing on additional datasets, UCF101 (Soomro et al. 2012 ), HMDB51(Kuehne et al. 2011 ) and Kinetics (Carreira and Zisserman 2017 ) to discuss our analysis in broader context, compared to the single architecture and dataset used in theearlier conference publication (Feichtenhofer et al. 2018 ).

paper_title: The dynamics of collaboration networks and the history of general relativity, 1925–1970
paper_claims: Integrating co-authorship, non-publishing collaborations, mentorship/influence ties, and institutional co-presence into a multilayer collaboration network reveals the emergence of a giant connected component in the general relativity community in the late 1950s–early 1960s, a transition that precedes 1960s astrophysical discoveries and is not reducible to postwar socio-economic changes.

verbatim_claims.text: This paper presents a novel methodology for defining and analyzing the dynamics of the collaboration networks of scientists working on general relativity from the mid-1920s–1970.
We constructed a multilayer network, in which each layer rep-resents a different kind of collaboration.

This analysis provides robust quantita-tive evidence that a shift in the structure of the relativity collaboration network occurred between the late 1950s and the early 1960s, when a giant component started forming.

It shows that this phenomenon was not a consequence of astrophysical discoveries in the 1960s, nor was it a simple by-product of socio-economic transformations in the physics landscape after World War II.
The method we propose is to consider a range of collaboration relations in addition to co-authorship relations retrieved in online repositories.
We will argue that this methodology allows for a robust quantitative interpretation of the renaissance of general relativity as a shift in the structure of a properly defined collaboration network.

qa_by_claim.claim: Integrating co-authorship, non-publishing collaborations, mentorship/influence ties, and institutional co-presence into a multilayer collaboration network reveals the emergence of a giant connected component in the general relativity community in the late 1950s–early 1960s, a transition that precedes 1960s astrophysical discoveries and is not reducible to postwar socio-economic changes.

[A] The emergence of a giant connected component in the late 1950s–early 1960s that precedes 1960s astrophysical discoveries and is not reducible to postwar socio-economic changes [B] The emergence of a giant connected component only after the 1960s astrophysical discoveries and fully explained by postwar socio-economic changes [C] A fragmentation into smaller components during the late 1950s–early 1960s that coincides with astrophysical discoveries and is driven by socio-economic factors [D] No meaningful change in connectivity across the period, with any shifts attributable solely to postwar socio-economic changes

Integrating co-authorship, non-publishing collaborations, mentorship/influence ties, and institutional co-presence into a multilayer collaboration network indicates that the general relativity community’s giant connected component formed only after the 1960s astrophysical discoveries and is fully explained by postwar socio-economic changes.

paper_title: Simulating a rich ride-share mobility service using agent-based models
paper_claims: Coupling an agent-based demand model (MATSim) with an operational fleet simulator (IMSim) via co-simulation enables realistic, convergent assessment of autonomous ride-share mobility services by integrating personalized utility-based mode choice with endogenously computed waiting and detour times, multi-tier customer-segmented vehicle allocation, and refuel/recharge constraints to produce operator-, traveler-, and city-level performance measures.

verbatim_claims.text: This paper reports on the novel integration, through co-simulation of two independent agent-based simulators: MATSim and IMSim.
We show how the sim-ulation tools complement each other to deliver a superior Autonomous Mobility on Demand (AMoD) modelling capability.

By combining the two, we can evaluate the impact of diverse AMoD scenarios from different standpoints: from a traveller’s perspective (e.g. satisfaction, service level, etc.); from an operator’s perspectives (e.g. cost, revenue, etc.); and from a city’s perspective (e.g. congestion, significant shifts between transport modes, etc.).
This research presents a comprehensive simulation approach to study the deployment of a ride-share AMoD service.
Our approach couples a demand simulation model (built in MATSim) with a fully featured fleet simulation engine: IMSim, the Intelligent Mobility Simulator.
It allows both commercial operators and city authorities to evaluate the impact that AMoD will have on the mobility ecosystem (with or without ride-sharing).

qa_by_claim.claim: Coupling an agent-based demand model (MATSim) with an operational fleet simulator (IMSim) via co-simulation enables realistic, convergent assessment of autonomous ride-share mobility services by integrating personalized utility-based mode choice with endogenously computed waiting and detour times, multi-tier customer-segmented vehicle allocation, and refuel/recharge constraints to produce operator-, traveler-, and city-level performance measures.

Choose the correct option (A–D): What does the co-simulation coupling of an agent-based demand model (MATSim) with an operational fleet simulator (IMSim) accomplish for autonomous ride-share services, and what elements are explicitly included? paper_title: A Multi-grained Log Auditing Scheme for Cloud Data Confidentiality
paper_claims: By aggregating data block accesses with Merkle Hash Trees and auditing an ordered AccessList of pseudonymous user identifiers across single blocks, multiple blocks, and entire files, a file-oriented cloud logging scheme achieves privacy-preserving confidentiality auditing and leakage investigation with reduced log volume and auditing overhead while maintaining forward-secure, append-only log integrity.

verbatim_claims.text: This paper proposes a multi-grained log auditing scheme to make logs volume smaller and log auditing more efficient.
We design a logging mechanism to support multi-grained data access with Merkle Hash Tree structure.
Based on multi-grained log, we present a log auditing approach to achieve data confidentiality auditing and leakage investigation by making an Access List.
In this paper, we propose a file-oriented multi-grained log auditing scheme to solve the problem aforementioned.
Besides, to support multi-grained auditing on logs, we propose a refined AccessList structure together with two auditing algorithms to investigate data leakage with accuracy.
In our scheme, the multi-grained logs and the multi-grained auditing target coordinate to achieve high efficiency in log auditing.
We build a data operation log structure which enables multi-grained data operation and improves information validity of logs.

In consideration of that existing log auditing schemes are inefficiency, a multi-grained log auditing scheme is pro- posed in this paper to monitor cloud data confidentiality status and investigate data leakage.
The scheme introduces Merkle Hash Tree structure to support multiple data granularity in logs.
Based on our multi-grained log, we present a multi-grained data confidentiality auditing.
Through our log auditing algorithms based on AccessList, we achieve sig- nificant efficiency amelioration than existing fine-grained logs based auditing.

qa_by_claim.claim: By aggregating data block accesses with Merkle Hash Trees and auditing an ordered AccessList of pseudonymous user identifiers across single blocks, multiple blocks, and entire files, a file-oriented cloud logging scheme achieves privacy-preserving confidentiality auditing and leakage investigation with reduced log volume and auditing overhead while maintaining forward-secure, append-only log integrity.

mcq.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which description correctly states how a file-oriented cloud logging scheme achieves privacy-preserving confidentiality auditing and leakage investigation with reduced log volume and auditing overhead while maintaining forward-secure, append-only log integrity? [A] It aggregates data block accesses with Merkle Hash Trees and audits an ordered AccessList of pseudonymous user identifiers across single blocks, multiple blocks, and entire files. [B] It aggregates access events using Bloom filters and audits only per-user logs, focusing on availability checks with increased log volume and no forward security. [C] It uses Merkle Hash Trees but audits an unordered list only at the per-file level, achieving no leakage investigation and no reductions in volume or overhead. [D] It records each access individually with plaintext identities and relies on per-request signatures, offering confidentiality auditing without append-only integrity or overhead reductions.

Choose the correct option (A–D): Identify the combination of mechanisms, identity handling, audit scope, and resulting properties that matches the described file-oriented cloud logging scheme. [A] Merkle Hash Trees; plaintext identities; per-file only; confidentiality auditing only; increased log volume and overhead; mutable logs. [B] Counting sketches; pseudonymous users; across blocks and files; privacy-preserving auditing and leakage investigation; reduced overhead; forward-secure append-only integrity. [C] Merkle Hash Trees; pseudonymous user identifiers in an ordered AccessList; across single blocks, multiple blocks, and entire files; privacy-preserving confidentiality auditing and leakage investigation; reduced log volume and auditing overhead; forward-secure, append-only log integrity. [D] Merkle DAGs; anonymous tokens; across sessions; availability monitoring; unchanged log size; rewritable logs.

paper_title: Deep Transfer Learning for Image Emotion Analysis: Reducing Marginal and Joint Distribution Discrepancies Together
paper_claims: Minimizing both marginal distribution discrepancies at fully connected layers using maximum mean discrepancy and joint discrepancies across fully connected and softmax layers using joint maximum mean discrepancy within a shared convolutional neural network reduces domain shift and yields more transferable features for cross-domain image emotion classification in label-scarce target domains.

verbatim_claims.text: In this paper, we propose a deep transfer learning method for image emotion analysis.
The method can leverage rich emotion knowledge from a source domain to the target domain.
Our method reduces both marginal and joint domain distribution discrepancies at fully-connected layers.
Through this way, we can effectively extract more transferable features and advance the performance of CNNs on poor-label emotion-image domains.
In order to generalize CNNs to different emotion-image domains, in this paper, we design a novel deep transfer learning method to promote CNN-based emotion classiﬁers on small-scale image domains.
Our method requires two domains share the same CNN.
We have both considered marginal distribution discrepancy at the same layers [ 14] and joint distribution discrepancy of different layers [ 16].
In this paper, we propose a deep transfer learning method into image emotion analysis.
During the transferring process, we decrease the marginal and the joint distribution discrepancies together.
The experimental results demonstrate the promise of our method for discriminating image emotions.

qa_by_claim.claim: Minimizing both marginal distribution discrepancies at fully connected layers using maximum mean discrepancy and joint discrepancies across fully connected and softmax layers using joint maximum mean discrepancy within a shared convolutional neural network reduces domain shift and yields more transferable features for cross-domain image emotion classification in label-scarce target domains.

Reason (R): Aligning both marginal and joint distributions across representation and prediction layers promotes source–target invariance, enhancing transferability when labeled target data are scarce.

paper_title: Gameful Experience Questionnaire (GAMEFULQUEST): an instrument for measuring the perceived gamefulness of system use
paper_claims: GAMEFULQUEST is a psychometrically validated questionnaire that models and measures perceived gamefulness of system use as a multidimensional, co-created experience distinct from traditional game experience and general affect or enjoyment, and is applicable across diverse gamified services.

verbatim_claims.text: In this paper, we present the development and validation of an instrument for meas-uring users’ gameful experience while using a service.

As a result of this work, we devised GAMEFULQUEST, an instru-ment that can be used to model and measure an individual user’s gameful experience in systems and services, which can be used for user-adapted gamification and for informing user-modeling research within a gamification context.
As a result of this research, we developed the instrument, called GAMEFULQUEST, which can be used to measure and model the individual user’s gameful experience of systems and services.

qa_by_claim.claim: GAMEFULQUEST is a psychometrically validated questionnaire that models and measures perceived gamefulness of system use as a multidimensional, co-created experience distinct from traditional game experience and general affect or enjoyment, and is applicable across diverse gamified services.

mcq.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which statement best describes GAMEFULQUEST in terms of validation, what it models and measures, how it conceptualizes perceived gamefulness, how it differs from other constructs, and where it can be applied? [A] A psychometrically validated questionnaire that models and measures perceived gamefulness of system use as a multidimensional, co-created experience, distinct from traditional game experience and general affect or enjoyment, and applicable across diverse gamified services. [B] An exploratory checklist for assessing general enjoyment in traditional games, assumed to be unidimensional and identical to general affect, intended for a narrow class of entertainment titles. [C] An unvalidated questionnaire measuring game addiction within gaming communities, focusing on single-user experiences and not meant for non-game systems. [D] A validated scale exclusively for video games that conflates perceived gamefulness with fun and does not generalize to other gamified services.

Choose the correct option (A–D): Select the statement that accurately captures GAMEFULQUEST’s conceptualization of perceived gamefulness, its distinction from other experiences, its psychometric status, and its applicability. [A] An unvalidated tool focusing on general affect in traditional games, limited to entertainment contexts. [B] A validated measure of general enjoyment that does not distinguish gamefulness from game experience and applies only to a few gamified services. [C] A psychometrically validated questionnaire that models and measures perceived gamefulness of system use as a multidimensional, co-created experience distinct from traditional game experience and general affect or enjoyment, and is applicable across diverse gamified services. [D] A domain-specific survey of gamification mechanics that equates perceived gamefulness with fun and assumes a single dimension.

paper_title: Correction of Scanning Electron Microscope Imaging Artifacts in a Novel Digital Image Correlation Framework
paper_claims: A unified integrated digital image correlation framework using a hierarchical composition of motion and imaging mappings simultaneously identifies and corrects SEM spatial distortion, drift distortion, and scan-line shifts while recovering mechanical displacement fields by modeling drift as a smooth time function projected onto scan times and separating it from mechanics with paired images per load step, calibrating time-invariant spatial distortion via rigid-body motions using spline or polynomial bases, representing random line shifts with localized error-function profiles, and performing time-integrated correlation with the average of all back-deformed images as the reference, thereby eliminating separate image-correction steps.

verbatim_claims.text: The current study proposes a unified generalframework to correct for the three dominant types of SEM artifacts, i.e. spatial distortion, drift distortion and scan lineshifts.
The artifact fields are measured alongside the mechanical deformations to minimize the artifact induced errors inthe latter.
To this purpose, Integrated DIC (IDIC) is extended with a series of hierarchical mapping functions that describethe interaction of the imaging process with the mechanics.
A new IDIC formulation based on these mapping functions isderived and the potential of the framework is tested by a number of virtual experiments.
A validation on real SEM images at three different magnifications confirms that all three distortion fields are accurately captured.
The results of all virtual and real experiments demonstrate the accuracy of the methodologyproposed, as well as its robustness in terms of convergence.
In this paper, a novel framework for correcting all three categories of SEM imaging artifacts is presented in a unifiedand systematic way.
The method is fully integrated in a DIC framework and thus yields, at the same time, artifact-corrected images and accurate mechanical deformationfields.
Based on this fact, in the currentstudy, Integrated Digital Image Correlation (IDIC) is usedto measure these artifact fields alongside the mechanical displacements in a separate manner.
Such a composition of mapping functions enables to uniquely capture the mechanics andartifacts independently.

The general framework is notrestricted to SEM images and can be equally applied for correcting imaging artifacts for other microscopy methods, whereby the hierarchy of mapping functions needs to beadapted to the specific microscopy technique.
The DIC problem is then solved in a time-integrated man-ner, correlating all deformation and distortion fields of allimages at once [ 33].

Finally, since all images (including the first one) contain distortions, the existence of an unde-formed reference image must be abandoned; therefore, a more general definition of the reference image is intro-duced, based on the average of all back-deformed images.

qa_by_claim.claim: A unified integrated digital image correlation framework using a hierarchical composition of motion and imaging mappings simultaneously identifies and corrects SEM spatial distortion, drift distortion, and scan-line shifts while recovering mechanical displacement fields by modeling drift as a smooth time function projected onto scan times and separating it from mechanics with paired images per load step, calibrating time-invariant spatial distortion via rigid-body motions using spline or polynomial bases, representing random line shifts with localized error-function profiles, and performing time-integrated correlation with the average of all back-deformed images as the reference, thereby eliminating separate image-correction steps.

mcq.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which description correctly specifies how a unified integrated digital image correlation framework using a hierarchical composition of motion and imaging mappings simultaneously identifies and corrects SEM spatial distortion, drift distortion, and scan-line shifts while recovering mechanical displacement fields and eliminating separate image-correction steps? [A] It models drift as a smooth time function projected onto scan times and separates it from mechanics using paired images per load step; calibrates time-invariant spatial distortion via rigid-body motions with spline or polynomial bases; represents random scan-line shifts using localized error-function profiles; and performs time-integrated correlation using the average of all back-deformed images as the reference. [B] It treats drift as piecewise-constant per frame and separates it from mechanics using single images; calibrates spatial distortion via nonrigid motion with Fourier bases; models line shifts as a global affine offset; and correlates against a single raw image, requiring pre-correction. [C] It sequentially corrects drift, spatial distortion, and line shifts using independent modules; estimates mechanical fields afterward; and references the first acquired image without time integration. [D] It relies on external calibration grids to remove all imaging distortions, assumes drift is negligible, estimates mechanics only, and averages raw images to define the reference.

Choose the correct option (A–D): Which combination of modeling choices and reference selection characterizes a unified integrated DIC framework that, via a hierarchical composition of motion and imaging mappings, simultaneously corrects SEM spatial distortion, drift distortion, and scan-line shifts while recovering mechanical displacement fields and removing the need for separate image-correction steps? [A] Drift as white noise per line; spatial distortion from elastic deformations in Fourier bases; line shifts as global affine; reference is a single raw image. [B] Drift as a smooth time function projected onto scan times and separated from mechanics with paired images per load step; spatial distortion calibrated via rigid-body motions using spline or polynomial bases; random line shifts with localized error-function profiles; reference is the average of all back-deformed images in time-integrated correlation. [C] Drift ignored; spatial distortion estimated per load step; line shifts corrected by median filtering; reference is the first deformed image. [D] Drift modeled by a step function per frame; spatial distortion corrected by external fiducials only; line shifts assumed periodic; reference is the median of raw images without back-deformation.

paper_title: A hybrid model for EEG-based gender recognition
paper_claims: Entropy-based features of resting-state EEG exhibit systematic gender differences across multiple scalp sites, providing discriminative information for gender recognition.

A hybrid classifier that encodes Random Forest leaf indices as sparse features for Logistic Regression yields superior EEG-based gender recognition compared with either Random Forest or Logistic Regression alone.
Fuzzy entropy features from EEG provide the strongest gender-discriminative power among the tested entropy measures and can match or exceed the performance of combined entropy feature sets.

Classification performance increases with the number of EEG electrodes, and the hybrid model shows greater robustness to variations in training data size and male–female class ratio than individual classifiers.

verbatim_claims.text: In this contribution, we present an automated gender recognition system by a hybrid model based on EEG data of resting statefrom twenty-eight subjects.

For achieving a good performance and a strong robustness, the system develops a hybrid model of combining random forest and logistic regression, and employs four common entropy measures to analyze the non-stationary EEGsignals.
In this study, we explore the application ofRF?LR in gender recognition, and determine if it is possible to further improve the recognition performance, and if there is a certain robustness.

In this paper, we present an EEG-based biometric authentication system to effectively access and analyze personal gender by applying a hybrid model combining RF over LR based on four common entropy measures, i.e., FE, SE, AE and PE.
According to the inﬂuence of the numberof electrodes and the fraction of training data, we evaluated and analyzed this hybrid model by using thirty electrodes to improve the authentication results.

In addition, thisapproach was founded on a nested tenfold cross-validation loop that embedded an inner tenfold cross validation for determining parameters that would yield the best classiﬁ-cation performance in detector design.
The EEG-based biometric authentication system is of potential beneﬁt for medical diagnosis and informa-tion security in relevant areas and may have a comple-mentary role in existing methods.

The strategy of using a hybrid model via combining ran-dom forest and logistic regression classiﬁers based on four common entropy factors can remarkably improve therecognition quality of a predictor for person’s gender, as indicated by the nested cross-validation tests in which a wider pool of participants was examined.

In this paper, an objective approach based on entropy feature sets and various classiﬁers was proposed to rec-ognize gender in EEG-based systems and the results demonstrated its promise as a method to identify gender by achieving higher success rates.

Compared with other existing EEG-based methods, the proposed method for gender authentication achieved an improved performance and a more robust detector, for studying personal gender conve-niently and effectively.
It also shows a signiﬁcant potential applicability of the proposed approach and is capable of identifying personal gender in an EEG-based biological recognition system.

paper_title: Detecting bursty terms in computer science research
paper_claims: Applying Moving Average Convergence Divergence to normalized time series of free-text term prevalence in time-ordered text corpora identifies bursty terms and, using MACD-derived features in a supervised model, predicts whether their prevalence will rise or fall over subsequent years.

verbatim_claims.text: In this paper, we take a trend detection algorithm from stock market analysis and apply it to over 30 years of computer science research abstracts, treating the prevalence of each term in the dataset like the price of a stock.
Unlike previous work in this domain, we use the free text of abstracts and titles, resulting in a finer-grained analysis.
The proposed methodology can be applied to any time-ordered collection of text to yield past and present bursty terms and predict their probable fate.
In this paper, we explore a burst detection methodology that requires little tuning and can be used on a large dataset.
To our knowledge, this is the first use of this method on free text rather than a controlled vocabulary of keywords.
We have explored a stock market-inspired burst detection algorithm, and used it to find bursty terms in over thirty years of computer science abstracts.

Most interestingly though, we find that many of our terms display a characteristic life cycle in their popularity over time, and note that it shares some similarities with the famous Gartner hype cycle (Fenn and Raskino 2008).

qa_by_claim.claim: Applying Moving Average Convergence Divergence to normalized time series of free-text term prevalence in time-ordered text corpora identifies bursty terms and, using MACD-derived features in a supervised model, predicts whether their prevalence will rise or fall over subsequent years.

[A] It identifies bursty terms and, using MACD-derived features in a supervised model, predicts whether their prevalence will rise or fall over subsequent years. [B] It identifies rare terms and, with unsupervised clustering, forecasts topic shifts within months. [C] It normalizes corpora and estimates absolute frequency counts without prediction. [D] It detects sentiment and predicts next-day spikes using raw counts.

Choose the correct option (A–D): When applied to time-ordered text corpora, what does applying MACD to normalized time series of free-text term prevalence accomplish and how are the outputs used? [A] Detects topic drift and ranks terms by tf-idf with no predictive component. [B] Identifies bursty terms, and MACD-derived features feed a supervised model to classify whether term prevalence will rise or fall over the following years. [C] Smooths term frequencies and uses unsupervised co-occurrence clustering to forecast long-term cycles. [D] Predicts exact future counts using linear regression on raw, unnormalized term frequencies.

true_false.question: Applying MACD to normalized time series of free-text term prevalence in time-ordered text corpora identifies bursty terms and, using MACD-derived features in a supervised model, predicts whether their prevalence will rise or fall over subsequent years.

Applying MACD to raw, unnormalized term counts in unordered text corpora identifies bursty terms and, without any supervised model, predicts exact future prevalence values rather than rise-or-fall direction.

assertion_reason.question: Assertion (A): Applying MACD to normalized time series of free-text term prevalence in time-ordered text corpora identifies bursty terms and, with MACD-derived features in a supervised model, predicts whether their prevalence will rise or fall over subsequent years. Reason (R): MACD yields features that capture changes in term prevalence over time, which a supervised classifier can use to label future direction.

paper_title: XOR-ed visual secret sharing scheme with robust and meaningful shadows based on QR codes
paper_claims: Embedding XOR-ed random-grid visual secret sharing shadow bits into the padding codewords of QR codes preserves the codes’ decodability and error correction, producing meaningful shadows that enable (k,n)-threshold recovery of binary secret images with lossless reconstruction when all n shadows are combined and robustness to common image attacks.

verbatim_claims.text: In this paper, we propose a robust (k,n) -threshold XOR-ed VSS (XVSS) scheme based on a QR code with the error correction ability.

Since the standard QR encoder does not check if the padding codewords are correct during the encoding phase, we replace padding codewords by initial shadows shared from the secret image using XVSS to generate QR code shadows.
As a result, the shadows can be decoded normally, and their error correction abilities are preserved.
Once all the shad- ows have been collected, the secret image can be recovered losslessly.

More importantly, if some conventional image attacks, including rotation, JPEG compression, Gaussian noise, salt-and-pepper noise, cropping, resizing, and even the addition of camera and screen noises are performed on the shadows, the secret image can still be recovered.
Therefore, in this paper, we propose a XVSS scheme with robust and meaningful shadows based on QR codes.
We use XVSS to share a secret image and generate noise-like shadows with higher visual quality and no pixel expansion.
As a result, the error correction ability of QR code shadows is as strong as that of cover QR codes.

Thus, the shadows are robust to conventional image attacks, such as rotation, JPEG compression, Gaussian noise, salt-and- pepper noise, cropping, resizing, and even the addition of camera and screen noise.
The decoding results of QR code shadows are identical to cover QR codes; thus, they will not arouse the suspicions of attackers.
We can recover the secret image from at least k shadows.
When k is equal to n, the secret can be recovered without loss.
Our scheme is robust and is characterized by perfect security and recovery.
The proposed approach is robust because the error correction capacity of QR codes is preserved.

As demonstrated in the experiments, the (k,n) -threshold XVSS can be implemented using QR codes; QR code shadows are meaningful because they can be decoded by a QR reader, and the proposed scheme is more robust than the existing related schemes.

The robustness test indicates that our scheme is robust to conventional image attacks, including JPEG compression, Gaussian noise, salt- and-pepper noise, cropping, rotation, resizing, and even the addition of camera and screen noises.

qa_by_claim.claim: Embedding XOR-ed random-grid visual secret sharing shadow bits into the padding codewords of QR codes preserves the codes’ decodability and error correction, producing meaningful shadows that enable (k,n)-threshold recovery of binary secret images with lossless reconstruction when all n shadows are combined and robustness to common image attacks.

[B] It modifies data codewords and degrades error correction, producing unintelligible shadows that are fragile to common image attacks and do not support lossless (k,n)-threshold recovery. [C] It preserves decodability but requires more than n shadows for recovery, yielding lossy reconstruction and limited robustness to attacks. [D] It makes QR codes undecodable unless all shadows are present and supports only approximate recovery of grayscale secrets.

Choose the correct option (A–D): Which statement accurately characterizes embedding XOR-ed random-grid visual secret sharing shadow bits into QR codes? [A] Embedding into finder and alignment patterns enhances payload capacity but sacrifices decodability and error correction, with shadows that are not meaningful. [B] Embedding yields robustness only for color images and reconstruction is lossy even if all n shadows are combined. [C] Embedding into padding codewords maintains QR decodability and error correction, yields meaningful shadows, supports (k,n)-threshold recovery of binary secret images with lossless reconstruction when all n shadows are combined, and remains robust to common image attacks. [D] Embedding requires exactly k shadows and fails if all n are combined, although error correction improves.

assertion_reason.question: Assertion (A): Embedding XOR-ed random-grid visual secret sharing shadow bits into the padding codewords of QR codes preserves decodability and error correction, produces meaningful shadows, and enables (k,n)-threshold recovery of binary secret images with lossless reconstruction when all n shadows are combined and robustness to common image attacks. Reason (R): Modifying only padding codewords leaves the encoded payload and error correction intact while XOR-ed random-grid shares can be combined to perfectly reconstruct the binary secret and tolerate common image degradations.

paper_title: Temporally Coherent General Dynamic Scene Reconstruction
paper_claims: An unsupervised multi-view framework reconstructs temporally coherent 4D models of general dynamic scenes captured by static or moving wide-baseline cameras without prior scene or background knowledge by automatically initializing coarse object segmentation and geometry from sparse 3D features, propagating sparse-to-dense temporal correspondences to detect and track dynamic regions, and jointly refining per-view depth and multi-view segmentation under a geodesic star-convexity shape constraint to obtain surfaces with consistent labels and correspondence across time.

verbatim_claims.text: This paper introduces a generalapproach to obtain a 4D representation of complex dynamic scenes from multi-view wide-baseline static or moving cameraswithout prior knowledge of the scene structure, appearance, or illumination.
Comparison with state-of-the-art approaches on a variety of complex indoor and outdoor scenes, demonstrates improved accuracy in both multi- view segmentation and dense reconstruction.

This paper demonstrates unsupervised reconstruction of complete temporallycoherent 4D scene models with improved non-rigid object segmentation and shape reconstruction and its application to various applications such as free-view rendering and virtual reality.

This paper addresses the limitations of existing approaches by introducing a methodology for unsupervised 4D tempo-rally coherent dynamic scene reconstruction from multiple wide-baseline static or moving camera views without prior knowledge of the scene structure or background appear- ance.

The scene is automatically decomposed into a setof spatio-temporally coherent objects as shown in Fig. 1 where the resulting 4D scene reconstruction has temporally coherent labels and surface correspondence for each object.
This temporally coherent dynamic scene reconstruction isdemonstrated to work in applications for immersive content production such as free-viewpoint video (FVV) and virtual reality (VR).
In particular the approach estimates a 4D surface model with full correspondence overtime.

qa_by_claim.claim: An unsupervised multi-view framework reconstructs temporally coherent 4D models of general dynamic scenes captured by static or moving wide-baseline cameras without prior scene or background knowledge by automatically initializing coarse object segmentation and geometry from sparse 3D features, propagating sparse-to-dense temporal correspondences to detect and track dynamic regions, and jointly refining per-view depth and multi-view segmentation under a geodesic star-convexity shape constraint to obtain surfaces with consistent labels and correspondence across time.

mcq.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which statement accurately and completely describes a framework that reconstructs temporally coherent 4D models of general dynamic scenes? [A] An unsupervised multi-view framework uses static or moving wide-baseline cameras without prior scene or background knowledge, automatically initializes coarse object segmentation and geometry from sparse 3D features, propagates sparse-to-dense temporal correspondences to detect and track dynamic regions, and jointly refines per-view depth and multi-view segmentation under a geodesic star-convexity shape constraint to yield surfaces with consistent labels and correspondences across time. [B] A supervised multi-view framework relies on pre-labeled backgrounds, assumes narrow-baseline static cameras, initializes from dense optical flow, and performs independent per-view depth optimization without shape constraints, producing per-frame surfaces without temporal correspondences. [C] An unsupervised single-view framework requires a prior scene model, tracks motion by keyframe selection only, and refines segmentation alone without imposing any shape constraint, yielding temporally incoherent reconstructions. [D] A semi-supervised multi-view pipeline reconstructs only static scenes by aggregating per-frame meshes from stereo pairs and post-hoc label smoothing, assuming background subtraction is given.

Choose the correct option (A–D): Which option best summarizes how a multi-view approach achieves temporally coherent 4D reconstruction of general dynamic scenes? [A] It trains a supervised network with background models, uses narrow-baseline cameras, and performs dense-to-sparse matching to segment motion without a shape prior. [B] It requires only a single moving camera and prior scene templates, initializes from dense optical flow, and refines segmentation without depth estimation. [C] It is unsupervised, handles static or moving wide-baseline cameras without prior scene or background knowledge, initializes coarse segmentation and geometry from sparse 3D features, propagates sparse-to-dense temporal correspondences to detect and track dynamic regions, and jointly refines per-view depth and multi-view segmentation under a geodesic star-convexity constraint to obtain surfaces with consistent labels and correspondence across time. [D] It reconstructs static 3D scenes by independent per-view depth maps with no temporal linkage and no multi-view segmentation constraints.
