paper_title: An automated methodology to select functional co-simulation configurations
paper_claims: Energy-residual monitoring across power bonds, evaluated via short predefined input tests and judged by the approximate linear dependence of accumulated residuals on the communication step-size, enables automated selection of stable and computationally efficient configurations for non-iterative Jacobi-scheme co-simulation with fixed-step multi-rate subsystems.

verbatim_claims.text: We put forward a methodology to automatically determine a stable and computationally efﬁ- cient conﬁguration for Jacobi-scheme co-simulation.
The method uses energy residuals to gain insight into co-simulation stability.

The relation between energy residual and commu- nication step-size is exploited to monitor co-simulation accuracy during a series of tests in which the external inputs are replaced with predetermined input functions.
Results indicate that the proposed method can be used to ﬁnd stable and accurate conﬁgurations for co-simulation applications.

In this paper we put forward a methodology to determine a stable and efﬁcient conﬁgura- tion for non-iterative Jacobi scheme co-simulation setups, especially aimed at multiphysics problems in the context of simulation of machinery.
The method described in this paper relies on the evaluation of energy residuals at the co- simulation interface, which are used as indicators of the stability of the numerical integra- tion.
Subsystem inputs are replaced during the initialization phase with a series of standard test functions and the communication step-size His gradually increased until the stability limit is reached.
However, the proposed method provides in- formation to determine a stable co-simulation conﬁguration in a simple manner with little additional effort for the user.
The viability of the algorithm was veriﬁed in the co-simulation of benchmark problems composed of mechanical and hydraulic subsystems.

paper_title: Refractive Two-View Reconstruction for Underwater 3D Vision
paper_claims: Refraction at a flat interface renders the single-viewpoint approximation invalid and induces depth-dependent geometric bias in underwater imaging when using pinhole-plus-distortion models.

In the case of a pinhole camera observing through a known thin, flat refractive interface, the two-view geometry admits a refractive fundamental matrix that depends on the interface’s position and orientation and the relative camera motion.

Using the refractive fundamental constraint together with refractive reprojection constraints in a two-step estimation improves outlier rejection and yields more accurate and robust underwater relative pose and quasi-dense 3D reconstruction than standard two-view Structure-from-Motion.

verbatim_claims.text: Our method applies to the case of underwaterimaging systems whose entrance lens is in direct contact with the external medium.

By adopting the refractive camera model, we provide a succinct derivation and expression for the refractive fundamental matrix and use this as the basis for a novel two- view reconstruction method for underwater imaging.
We demonstrate our approach outperforms classic two-view Structure- from-Motion method relying on the pinhole-plus-distortion camera model.

We propose a novel stereo 3D-reconstruction method for thecase of a pinhole camera looking through a thin refractiveinterface, where the position and orientation of this interface are known (see Fig. 2).
Our approach is particularly ﬂexi- ble and can be applied to various applications of underwaterimaging and vision behind a thin refractive interface.

To demonstrate our contribution to the refractive geometry of underwater vision, we evaluate the effectiveness and improvements on numerical stability of our approach on synthetic and real data over classic two-view Structure-from-Motion (2View SfM) relying on the pinhole-plus-distortion per- spective camera model.

To highlight a particular appli- cation for two-view Refractive Structure-from-Motion (2View RSfM), we show results for fetoscopy, where an endoscope is used to guide vessel photo-coagulation on the placental surface within the amniotic ﬂuid.

paper_title: Disentangling Geometry and Appearance with Regularised Geometry-Aware Generative Adversarial Networks
paper_claims: Conditioning a generative adversarial network on a statistical shape model and enforcing the prior by differentiably warping outputs to a canonical frame disentangles shape from appearance, provides explicit control over object geometry while maintaining realistic texture, constrains the search space to enable effective training on small datasets, and serves as a modular augmentation applicable to diverse GAN architectures.

Training the discriminator with (α,β) regularization to accept slight shape perturbations and reject large ones under the warping transform mitigates label and covariate shift and produces reliable image–shape alignment scores that enable detection of facial landmark tracking or detection errors.

verbatim_claims.text: In this work, we propose a regularized Geometry-Aware Generative AdversarialNetwork (GAGAN) which disentangles appearance and shape in the latent space.
This regularized GAGAN enables the generation of images with both realistic texture and shape.
Speciﬁcally, we condition the generator on a statistical shape prior.
The prior is enforced through mapping the generated images onto a canonical coordinate frame using a differentiablegeometric transformation.
We show that our approach is versatile, able to generalise across domains (faces, sketches, hands and cats) and sample sizes (from as little as ∼200−30,000 to more than 200 ,000).
We demonstrate superior performance through extensive quantitative and qualitative experiments in a variety of tasks and settings.
Finally, we leverage our modelto automatically and accurately detect errors or drifting in facial landmarks detection and tracking in-the-wild.
In particular, we introduce Geometry-Aware GAN (GAGAN) which disentangles thelatent space corresponding to shape and texture by employ- ing a statistical shape model.
By mapping the output of the generator to the coordinate frame of a canonical shape through a differentiable geomet-ric transformation, we strongly enforce the geometry of the objects.
We propose a novel method to address the issue of dataset shift, speciﬁcally label shift and covariate shift(Quionero-Candela et al. 2009 ).
We extend GAGAN to generate entire images, including the actual visual object and the background.
By employing a ﬂexible differentiable transformation, GAGAN can be seen as a meta-algorithm and used to augment any existing GAN architecture.
By constraining the search space using a shape model built in a strongly supervised way, GAGAN works wellon very small datasets unlike existing approaches.

The experimental results indicate that GAGAN produces superior results with respect to the visual quality of the images produced by existing state of the art GAN-based methods as well respecting a givengeometrical prior.
In addition, by sampling from the sta- tistical shape model we can generate faces with arbitrary facial attributes such as facial expression, pose and morphol- ogy.

paper_title: Robust optimization and uncertainty quantification in the nonlinear mechanics of an elevator brake system
paper_claims: In a mechanical equilibrium model of a friction elevator safety gear, the braking force is an explicit function of geometry, friction coefficients and loads, and in realistic operating ranges it is weakly sensitive to cam angle while varying approximately affinely with the spring reaction force, making spring force uncertainty the dominant contributor to braking force variability.

A robust optimization that maximizes a convex combination of worst-case, best-case, mean and inverse standard deviation of braking force under a probabilistic minimum-force constraint produces geometric designs that are less sensitive to parameter uncertainties than those obtained by deterministic optimization.

verbatim_claims.text: A deterministic model that relates the braking force with uncertain parameters is deduced from mechanicalequilibrium conditions.
In order to take into account parameters variabilities, a parametric probabilistic approach is employed.
In this stochastic formalism, the uncertain parameters are modeled as random variables, with distributions speciﬁed by the maxi-mum entropy principle.
The uncertainties arepropagated by the Monte Carlo method, which provides a detailed statistical characterization of the response.
This work still considers the optimum design of the brake system, formulating and solvingnonlinear optimization problems, with and without the uncertainties effects.

Thus, seeking to ﬁll this gap, the present work aims to study the inﬂuence of some operating conditions on the efﬁciency of an elevator brake device, by analyz- ing how the operating parameters underlying uncer-tainties propagate through the mechanical system.

In addition to quantifying theeffects of uncertainties in operating conditions, this study also aims to achieve a robust design of a brake system by solving a nonlinear optimization problem, considering (or not) the uncertainty effects.
The paper starts from an original constructionof a safety gear for the brake, for which a mechanical- mathematical model is constructed.

Studies involving the quantiﬁcation of the braking force uncertaintiesdue to the variability in the brake cam angle and the spring reaction force are presented, showing that spring force uncertainties are more signiﬁcant.

The paper also focuses on the optimal design of an elevator brake system, showing through the solution of a robust optimization problem that operating conditions uncer-tainties can signiﬁcantly inﬂuence its efﬁciency.

qa_by_claim.claim: In a mechanical equilibrium model of a friction elevator safety gear, the braking force is an explicit function of geometry, friction coefficients and loads, and in realistic operating ranges it is weakly sensitive to cam angle while varying approximately affinely with the spring reaction force, making spring force uncertainty the dominant contributor to braking force variability.

[B] The braking force is an implicit function determined numerically; in realistic ranges it is highly sensitive to cam angle, varies nonlinearly with the spring reaction force, and cam angle uncertainty dominates variability. [C] The braking force depends only on loads; it is insensitive to cam angle and independent of the spring reaction force, so friction coefficient uncertainty dominates variability. [B] Explicit dependence on geometry and loads only; strong sensitivity to cam angle; approximately affine variation with spring reaction force; and dominance of cam angle uncertainty in variability. [C] Implicit dependence on geometry, friction coefficients, and loads; weak sensitivity to cam angle; nonlinear dependence on spring reaction force; and dominance of friction coefficient uncertainty in variability. [D] Explicit dependence on geometry, friction coefficients, and loads; strong sensitivity to cam angle; nonlinear dependence on spring reaction force; and dominance of geometric uncertainty in variability.

Reason (R): Because the force-balance equations yield a closed-form dependence on geometry, friction, and loads, and within realistic ranges the cam angle contributes only minor (higher-order) effects whereas the spring reaction enters nearly linearly, so uncertainty in the spring force propagates most strongly to the braking force.

paper_title: Disk Cluster Allocation Behavior in Windows and NTFS
paper_claims: NTFS on Windows 7 and Windows 10 generally follows a best-fit free-space allocation strategy for block-written files but exhibits systematic deviations where allocations are taken from large areas despite smaller better-fitting holes and fragments are placed leaving small residual gaps.

On fragmented volumes, block-written file fragments decrease in size both within each file and across successive files, whereas stream-written files begin with very small fragments and progressively increase fragment sizes as writing proceeds.

verbatim_claims.text: We have therefore studied the NTFS allocation algorithm and its behavior empirically.
Our results show that files written as one large block are allocated areas of decreasing size when the files are fragmented.
We also found that a file written as a stream gets the opposite allocation behavior, i. e. its fragments are increasing in size as the file is written.
The first allocated unit of a stream written file is always very small and hence easy to identify.
Our work is based on an empirical evaluation of the behavior of the allocation algorithm of Windows NTFS, using the hypothesis that a best fit allocation strategy is used.
The data source is based on writing files of different sizes to disk using different writing strategies (writing the file as one large block at once or as a continuous stream of data).

qa_by_claim.claim: NTFS on Windows 7 and Windows 10 generally follows a best-fit free-space allocation strategy for block-written files but exhibits systematic deviations where allocations are taken from large areas despite smaller better-fitting holes and fragments are placed leaving small residual gaps.

mcq.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): On Windows 7 and Windows 10, NTFS free-space allocation for block-written files generally follows which pattern, including how it deviates in practice? [A] A generally best-fit allocation with systematic deviations: allocations are sometimes taken from large areas despite smaller better-fitting holes, and fragments are placed leaving small residual gaps. [B] A strict best-fit allocation with no deviations: the smallest fitting hole is always chosen and residual gaps are never left. [C] A primarily first-fit allocation with deviations that favor smaller holes over larger areas, eliminating small gaps. paper_title: A new design for cubic spatially processed wideband arrays
paper_claims: A cubic volumetric array with a single real multiplier per element can steer a fully spatial beam throughout full azimuth and elevation hemispheres while maintaining frequency-invariant beampatterns over a wide fractional bandwidth.

Real-valued element weights are determined by representing the array response as a sum of L symmetric steering terms centered at selected focal points in a bounded three-dimensional frequency space and solving a reduced L-by-L linear system, where even L and symmetric point placement ensure real weights and unit gain in the desired direction.

The design requires solving a linear system of dimension L that can be chosen much smaller than the total number of array elements, thereby substantially reducing computational complexity relative to direct weight computation.

With inter-element spacings not exceeding half the minimum wavelength, the transformed frequency variables are confined to a cube (−0.5, 0.5) in each dimension, supporting frequency-invariant array responses within the operational band.
Increasing the number of symmetric focal points narrows the main lobe and suppresses sidelobes without compromising steering capability or frequency invariance.

verbatim_claims.text:
This paper presents a simple and novel formulation for the design and analysis of a genericcubic array with a single real coefﬁcient for each antenna element, to steer the developedbeam toward a certain desired angle in both azimuth (totally 360
◦) and elevation (totally 180◦)
directions.

This conﬁguration demonstrates frequency invariance of the directional patternsof the array within a relatively large fractional bandwidth which makes this array a potentialcandidate for wideband and ultra wideband applications.
The required real coefﬁcients arecalculated based on matrix manipulation methods.
However, the dimension of the matricesand vectors used in the computations are much less than the total number of antenna elements.
In this paper a new algorithm is proposed to calculate the real multipliers of a linear cubic array.
The main advantage of this method is the simplicity of the computation which does not involve two dimensional Fourier transform normally used in wideband array calculations.
Moreover, it covers 360◦of azimuth and 180◦of elevation angles with a single structure.

We also notice that the proposed method is superior in terms of overall size and frequency independence compared to the distributed technique presented in Overturf et al. ( 2017 ) which is using both temporal and spatial signal processing methodology.
The fully spatial beamformer can steer the beam toward both azimuth and elevation directions covering the whole three dimensional space.

The conﬁguration beneﬁts from the frequency invariance of the patterns within a relatively large fractional bandwidth of about 30%, which may be used in the whole UWB spectrum for different applications.
However, the dimension of the matrices and vectors used in the computations can be much lower than the total number of antenna elements.
Moreover, a comparison was made with the Fourier transform based and adaptive LMS algorithm methods in terms of the directivity and sidelobe levels to show the advantages of the proposed algorithm.

paper_title: Local dimension reduction of summary statistics for likelihood-free inference
paper_claims: A localization strategy for projection-based dimension reduction in likelihood-free inference, where transformations are fitted in a neighborhood of the observed data and localization and transformation parameters are optimized using validation datasets, yields informative low-dimensional summaries that improve posterior estimation accuracy and stability and mitigate failure of transformed summaries outside the local region across diverse models.

verbatim_claims.text: We introduce a localization strategy for any projection-based dimension reduction method, in which the transformation is estimated in the neighborhood of the observed data instead of the whole space.
In our localization approach the transformation is validated and optimized over validation datasets, ensuring reliable performance.
We demonstrate the improvement in the estimation accuracy for localized versions of linear regression and partial least squares, for three different models of varying complexity.
Here we introduce an algorithm for reliable localiza- tion of any projection-based dimension reduction technique.
By evaluating the performance of the local transformation on the validation datasets globally, the method is able to overcome the issue of poorly performing summaries outside the local neighborhood.
We show improvement over global dimension reduction in different models of varying complexity for both linear regression and partial least squares.
Compared to the previously published localization approaches, the optimization of the local transformation results in higher accuracy and improved stability of the transformed summary statistics.

qa_by_claim.claim: A localization strategy for projection-based dimension reduction in likelihood-free inference, where transformations are fitted in a neighborhood of the observed data and localization and transformation parameters are optimized using validation datasets, yields informative low-dimensional summaries that improve posterior estimation accuracy and stability and mitigate failure of transformed summaries outside the local region across diverse models.

[B] It yields low-dimensional summaries but does not improve posterior accuracy or stability and has no effect on failures outside the local region. [C] It relies on globally fitted transformations without validation-based optimization, providing general improvements regardless of model diversity. [D] It fits transformations far from the observed data and tunes only transformation parameters using training data, leading to less informative summaries and model-specific instability.

Choose the correct option (A–D): Which configuration and outcome best aligns with a localization approach for projection-based dimension reduction in likelihood-free inference across diverse models? [A] Fitting transformations in the observed-data neighborhood and optimizing localization and transformation parameters with validation datasets, producing informative low-dimensional summaries that enhance posterior accuracy and stability while reducing failure of transformed summaries outside the local region. [B] Fitting transformations globally and fixing parameters a priori, producing summaries that marginally compress data but often degrade posterior accuracy and exacerbate out-of-region failures. [C] Fitting transformations near observed data but optimizing only transformation parameters on training data, yielding stable posteriors yet not informative summaries and no mitigation outside the local region. [D] Using no localization but cross-validated feature selection, which ensures improvements only within a single model and may increase instability.

paper_title: GPU acceleration of splitting schemes applied to differential matrix equations
paper_claims: For differential Lyapunov equations, approximating the integral representation with high-order quadrature using matrix exponential actions delivers superior accuracy and efficiency compared to Strang splitting.

For differential Riccati equations, splitting into the minimal number of subproblems—grouping the linear and constant terms and separating the nonlinear term—generally minimizes splitting error, whereas additionally splitting the constant term becomes advantageous when that term is sufficiently small.

In generalized equations with multiplicative terms of the form S P S^T, treating that subproblem by the midpoint rule within Strang splitting preserves second-order convergence and admits efficient low-rank updates through block augmentation and column compression.

verbatim_claims.text: According to our numerical experiments, large speed-ups are often observed for sufficiently large matrices.

We also provide a comparison between dif-
ferent splitting strategies, demonstrating that splitting the equations into a moderate
number of subproblems is generally optimal.
Our results already show a remarkable improvement in efficiency, and this can only increase with further optimizations and the use of more advanced techniques tailored to specific problems.
Our results in this direction demonstrate that it is usually beneficial to use the smallest number of splits.

How- ever, when Qis sufficiently small it is beneficial to split it too, since the extra error is similarly small and the subproblems ˙P=ATP+PAand˙P=Qare very cheap to compute compared to ˙P=ATP+PA+Q.

paper_title: Robust Bayesian model selection for variable clustering with the Gaussian graphical model
paper_claims: The maximum a posteriori estimation for this model admits a strictly convex reformulation in the precision parameters that is solvable to global optimality using a three-block ADMM algorithm with convergence guaranteed for any positive penalty parameter.

Classical BIC and Laplace-type approximations are inappropriate for this model because the maximum-likelihood fit and parameter count do not depend on the clustering, yielding identical scores across clusterings.

Restricting the hypothesis space to candidate clusterings produced by spectral clustering on Graphical Lasso precision estimates yields a tractable set that often contains the true or near-true clustering, enabling practical Bayesian model selection.

verbatim_claims.text: In this work, we try to address this issue by proposing a Bayesian model that accounts for negligible small, but not necessarily zero,partial correlations.
Based on our model, we propose to evaluate a variable clustering result using the marginal likelihood.
To address the intractable calculation of the marginal likelihood, we propose two solutions: one based on a variational approximation and another based on MCMC.
Experiments on simulated data show that the proposed method is similarlyaccurate as BIC in the no noise setting, but considerably more accurate when there are noisy partial correlations.
Furthermore,on real data the proposed method provides clustering results that are intuitively sensible, which is not always the case when using BIC or its extensions.
As an alternative, we propose to cluster variables, such that the partial correlation between any two variables in different clusters is negligibly small, but not necessarily zero.
As a solution, we pro- pose here a Bayesian model that explicitly accounts for smallpartial correlations between variables in different clusters.

Under our proposed model, the marginal likelihood of the data can then be used to identify the correct (if there is a ground truth in theory), or at least a meaningful clustering(in practice) that helps analysis.
Since the marginal likelihood of our model does not have an analytic solution, we provide two approximations: the ﬁrst is a variational approximation,and the second is based on MCMC.

The proposed method also compares favorable to two previously proposed methods for variable clustering and model selection, namely the Clustered Graphical Lasso(CGL) (Tan et al. 2015 ) and the Dirichlet Process V ariable Clustering (DPVC) (Palla et al. 2012 ) method.

paper_title: Enabling connectivity for tactical networks in mountainous areas by aerial relays
paper_claims: Integrating high-resolution 3D terrain, slope-dependent mobility traces, antenna radiation patterns, and irregular-terrain propagation modeling indicates that in mountainous tactical deployments a single tethered low-altitude balloon relay equipped with a tilted helical antenna can maintain line-of-sight star connectivity among platoon cluster heads, with reliable dissemination achieved via time-shared unicast using amplify/decode-and-forward relaying and go-back-N retransmissions.

verbatim_claims.text: The framework is easily extensible, and can be eventually automated.
It can be also used to generate data for other network simulators.
The framework utilizes the freely downloadable high resolution 3D terrain data to deﬁne time dependent trajectories of network nodes.
The node speeds and directions are linked to the terrain proﬁle which extends the previously proposed mobility models.
The path-loss analysis along the node trajectories revealed the need for aerial relays to enable full network connectivity at all times.
The relay location and its antenna height are optimized to achieve the line-of-sight connectivity over the whole mission duration.
The antenna radiation pattern at the relay is incorporated in the analysis.
Several relaying schemes including the amplify-and-forward and the decode-and-forward relaying are studied together with the go-back-N retransmissions to achieve the reliable data transfer.
It is shown that a single relay with balloonmounted antennas can provide sufﬁcient LOS coverage for all the squadron platoons during the whole mission duration.
The generated received signal strength (RSS) traces can be used as realistic inputs to other protocol-oriented network simulators such asNS2 [ 13].
– We used a realistic digital elevation model (DEM) with 30 m resolution to construct the 3D terrain proﬁle of a selected geographical area.
– We used a free software for realistic prediction of radio propagation and radio coverage in order to optimize theballoon relay height above the ground.
– We found that only star topology with the balloon relay at the center is available at all times while direct connectionsbetween mobile units cannot be guaranteed.
– We designed a simple unicast protocol for sharing information packets from each mobile unit to all other mobile units.

paper_title: Person Re-identification in Identity Regression Space
paper_claims: Formulating person re-identification as regression into an identity regression space where each training identity is assigned an orthogonal unit target vector yields an embedding that collapses intra-class variance and maximizes inter-class separateness.

The regression-based re-identification model supports online incremental learning by updating the projection with Sherman–Morrison–Woodbury identities using only newly labelled samples, eliminating retraining and past-data storage.

Active learning that jointly exploits exploration of appearance diversity and unexplained cross-view matching discrepancy prioritizes informative samples for annotation, improving label efficiency during incremental updates.

Encoding class targets as orthogonal one-hot vectors strengthens global between-class discrimination while retaining extreme intra-class compactness compared to null-space Fisher and FDA-style embeddings.

verbatim_claims.text: Speciﬁcally, we propose to construct an identity regression space (IRS) based on embedding different training per- son identities (classes) and formulate re-id as a regression problem solved by identity regression in the IRS.
The IRS approach is characterised by a closed-form solution with high learning efﬁciency and an inherent incremental learning capability with human-in-the-loop.

Extensive experiments on four benchmarking datasets (VIPeR, CUHK01, CUHK03 and Market-1501) show that the IRS model not only outperforms state-of-the-art re-id methods, but also is more scalable to large re-id population size by rapidly updating model and actively selecting informative samples with reduced human labelling effort.
We propose the concept of an identity regression space (IRS) by formulat- ing re-id as a regression problem for tackling the inherent small sample size (SSS) challenge.
The IRS model is featured by an efﬁcient closed-form feature embedding solution without the need for solving anexpensive eigen-system and alternative optimisation.
W e introduce an incremental learning algorithm for efﬁcient on- line IRS model update.
We develop an active learning algorithm for more cost-effective IRS model update with human-in-the-loop, an under-studied aspect in exist- ing re-id methods.

paper_title: A diachronic perspective on peer disagreement in veritistic social epistemology
paper_claims: In a Bayesian, veritistic evaluation of peer disagreement that updates both credences and inter-agent trust, the conciliatory response of lowering confidence and trust upon unexpected disagreement increases expected truth-approximation only when agents continue to recheck the target proposition over time, whereas without such ongoing inquiry it confers no veritistic advantage over steadfastness.

verbatim_claims.text: Douven and Kelp’s favored simulation framework cannot naturally handle Christensen’s famous Mental Math example.
As a remedy, I introduce an alternative (Bayesian) simulation framework, Laputa, inspired by Alvin Goldman’s seminal work on veritistic social epistemology.

I show that Christensen’s conciliatory response, reasonably reconstructed and supplemented, gives rise to an increase in epistemic (veritistic) value only if the peers continue to recheck their mental math; else the peers might as well be steadfast.
On a meta-level, the study illustrates the power of Goldman’s approach when combined with simulation techniques for handling the computational issues involved.
However, their simulation framework cannot convincingly handle Christensen’s famous Mental Math example.

As a remedy, I introduce an alternative simulation framework, Laputa, which relies on Alvin Goldman’s seminal work on veritistic value (Goldman 1999 ), and I show how the example can be diachronically addressed in this other setting in line with Christensen’s conciliatory recommendation (with some steadfast qualiﬁ- cations).
Alas, we saw that the Hegelmann-Krause model upon which Douven and Kelp’s study relies cannot convincingly handle Christensen’s Mental Math example.
Moreover, we found Christensen’s own recommendation for how to proceed lacking in detail, synchronically as well as diachronically.

I showed how the Mental Math example can be diachronically addressed in line with Christensen’s recommendation, supplemented with a minor steadfast element concerning the way in which trust was handled.
In this study it was crucial that Laputa is both synchronically and diachronically complete which makes it particularly suitable for a diachronic assessment of Christensen’s example.

What comes out of the simulation study conducted in this paper is that Christensen’s approach, as reconstructed here, gives rise to an average gain in veritistic value only if the peers continue to recheck their results; else they might as well be (completely) steadfast.
On a meta-level, the present study illustrates, I hope, the power of the veritistic approach to social epistemology when combined with simulation techniques.

paper_title: Face Mask Extraction in Video Sequence
paper_claims: A ConvLSTM-augmented fully convolutional network that processes video sequences learns temporal dependencies alongside spatial features to perform end-to-end dense segmentation of facial components in video.

A segmentation loss derived from the intersection-over-union objective that assigns separate per-class weights to positive and negative samples directly optimizes mean IoU and mitigates class imbalance more effectively than cross-entropy and naive IoU losses.
A coarse-to-fine pipeline that combines a primary whole-face model with zoomed-in eye and mouth models yields more accurate component masks than a single-scale model alone.

verbatim_claims.text: Inspired by the recent development of deep network-based methods in semantic image segmentation, we introduce an end-to-end trainable model for face mask extraction in video sequence.
Comparing to landmark-based sparse face shape representation, our method can produce the segmentation masks of individual facial components, which can better reﬂect their detailed shape variations.

By integrating convolutional LSTM (ConvLSTM) algorithm with fully convolutional networks (FCN), our new ConvLSTM-FCN model works on a per-sequence basis and takes advantage of the temporal correlation in video clips.
In addition, we also propose a novel loss function, called segmentation loss, to directly optimise the intersection over union (IoU) performances.
In practice, to further increase segmentation accuracy, one primary model and two additional models were trained to focus on the face, eyes, and mouth regions, respectively.
To the best of our knowledge, this is the ﬁrst exploration of face mask extraction in video sequence with an end-to-end trainable deep-learning model.
In this paper, we propose an end-to-end trainable model which could exploit the temporal information in a more directand natural way.

The key idea is the application of convolutional long short term memory (ConvLSTM) layer (Xingjian et al. 2015 ) in FCN models, which enable the FCNs to learn the temporal connections while retaining the ability to learnspatial correlations.

In this work, we introduce the ConvLSTM-FCN model that combines FCN and ConvLSTM by converting a certain convolutional layer in the FCN model into a ConvLSTM layer, thus adding the ability to model temporal dependen-cies within the input video sequence.
The ConvLSTM-FCN model accepts video sequence as input and outputs the predictions of the same size, and the temporal information is learnt together with the spatial connections.

To be able to optimise the model toward higher accuracy in terms of mean intersection over union (mIoU), which isa typical performance metric for segmentation problems, we also propose a new loss function, called segmentation loss.
Unlike the IoU loss in Rahman and Wang ( 2016 ), segmentation loss is more ﬂexible and carries more practical meaning in image space.
In comparison to the frequently-used cross-entropy loss and the IoU loss by Rahman and Wang ( 2016 ), higher mIOU can be achieved when segmentation loss is used as the loss function during training.

paper_title: Reducing false wake-up in contention-based wake-up control of wireless LANs
paper_claims: In wake-up radio assisted contention-based transmitter wake-up for WLANs, the WLAN module’s wake-up latency induces multiple false activations that dominate energy overhead relative to collisions, and jointly applying backoff counter resetting to emulate freezing during wake-up, early sleep upon inferred false activation, and contention window tuning reduces false-wake probability and duration while improving the throughput–energy tradeoff.

False wake-up probability increases with the WLAN module wake-up latency, and for sufficiently large contention windows the ratio of false wake-ups to collisions approaches the wake-up period expressed in slots.

Selecting the contention window by maximizing the product of spectral energy efficiency and channel efficiency, using wake-up radio observations of idle intervals to inform contention levels, yields higher overall efficiency than configurations based solely on throughput or collision energy.

verbatim_claims.text: In this model, a WuR monitors the channel, performs carrier sense, and activates its co-located WLAN module when the channel becomes ready for transmission.

This is called a false wake-up problem and it is solved from three aspects in this work: (i) resetting backoff counter of each node in a way as if it is frozen in a wake-up period, (ii) reducing false wake-up time by immediately putting a WLAN module into sleep once a false wake-up is inferred, and (iii) reducing false wake-up probability by adjusting contention window.
Analysis shows that false wake-ups, instead of collisions, become the dominant energy overhead.

To the best of our knowledge, this is the first paper that investigates and analyzes the impact of wake-up latency, considering both the power consumption of WLAN modules in the wake-up period and the power waste due to false wake-upsFootnote 1 of non-relevant WLAN modules.
Specifically, we show that false wake-up probability increases with wake-up latency.

Then, we find the optimal CW to reduce false wake-ups and improve throughput, and suggest a heuristic method to adjust CW, which exploits a WuR to monitor channel contention in an energy efficient way.
Extensive simulations lead to the same findings and confirm that the proposed method achieves a better tradeoff between throughput and energy consumption than state-of-the-arts.

qa_by_claim.claim: In wake-up radio assisted contention-based transmitter wake-up for WLANs, the WLAN module’s wake-up latency induces multiple false activations that dominate energy overhead relative to collisions, and jointly applying backoff counter resetting to emulate freezing during wake-up, early sleep upon inferred false activation, and contention window tuning reduces false-wake probability and duration while improving the throughput–energy tradeoff.

mcq.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): In wake-up radio-assisted contention-based transmitter wake-up for WLANs, which statement correctly describes the dominant source of energy overhead and the effect of a specific joint mitigation? [A] The WLAN module’s wake-up latency induces multiple false activations that dominate energy overhead relative to collisions, and jointly applying backoff counter resetting to emulate freezing during wake-up, early sleep upon inferred false activation, and contention window tuning reduces false-wake probability and duration while improving the throughput–energy tradeoff. [B] Wake-up latency has negligible impact on activation patterns, collisions dominate energy overhead, and enabling RTS/CTS with larger payloads eliminates false wakes but degrades the throughput–energy tradeoff. paper_title: Probabilistic Approach Versus Machine Learning for One-Shot Quad-Tree Prediction in an Intra HEVC Encoder
paper_claims: For HEVC intra coding, per-depth thresholds on CU luminance variance derived from a learning frame and modeled as a linear function of the mean and standard deviation of the variance distribution enable one-shot prediction of CTU quad-tree partitioning largely independent of quantization parameter.

Lightweight decision-tree classifiers using local variance-based features of CUs, sub-CUs, neighboring CUs, and QP accurately predict per-depth split/merge decisions, enabling one-shot quad-tree partitioning without exhaustive RDO.

Constraining rate–distortion optimization to the interval between an initial variance-threshold-based depth map and a refined depth map drastically reduces quad-tree search complexity while preserving compression performance within a controlled trade-off.

verbatim_claims.text: For the purpose of reducing the energy consumption of a real-time H EVC Intra encoder, this paper proposes and compares two methods that predict the quad-tree partitioning in “one-shot”, i.e. without iterating.
These methods drastically limitthe computational cost of the recursive Rate-Distortion Optimization (RDO) process.
The first proposed method uses aProbabilistic approach whereas the second method is based on Machine Learning approach.
We propose in this paper two energy reduction methods for H EVC Intra encoders based on a CTU partitioning prediction technique.
We then compare these methodsthat drastically limit the recursive RDO process.
The first method exploits the correlation between CTU partitioning and the variance of the CTU luminance samples to predictthe quad-tree decomposition in one-shot.
The secondmethod uses a Machine Learning approach to perform the same prediction.

qa_by_claim.claim: For HEVC intra coding, per-depth thresholds on CU luminance variance derived from a learning frame and modeled as a linear function of the mean and standard deviation of the variance distribution enable one-shot prediction of CTU quad-tree partitioning largely independent of quantization parameter.

[B] Global thresholds on CU chroma variance, derived from each test frame and modeled as a nonlinear function of the variance distribution's median and range. [C] Per-CTU thresholds on CU luminance variance, learned without a learning frame and fixed across all depths, modeled as constants. [D] Per-depth thresholds on CU edge density, derived from a learning frame and modeled as a quadratic function of the mean and standard deviation, tuned for each quantization parameter.

Choose the correct option (A–D): Which statement correctly characterizes the conditions under which one-shot prediction of CTU quad-tree partitioning is achieved? [A] For HEVC intra coding, per-depth CU luminance variance thresholds from a learning frame modeled as a linear function of the variance distribution's mean and standard deviation, with decisions largely independent of quantization parameter. [B] For HEVC inter coding, per-depth CU luminance variance thresholds from a learning frame modeled as a linear function of the variance distribution's mean and standard deviation, with decisions highly dependent on quantization parameter. [C] For HEVC intra coding, global chroma variance thresholds computed online for each frame modeled as a nonlinear function of higher-order moments, with decisions requiring iterative search. [D] For HEVC intra coding, per-depth CU luminance variance thresholds from a learning frame modeled as a quadratic function of the mean and standard deviation, with decisions strictly tied to a fixed quantization parameter.

paper_title: QEX: target-specific druglikeness filter enhances ligand-based virtual screening
paper_claims: A target-specific druglikeness index constructed by modeling the distributions of eight physicochemical properties from known actives and aggregating them via weighted desirability functions provides superior enrichment of actives for individual targets compared with a global druglikeness metric trained on approved drugs.

By relying solely on active compounds and capturing binding-pocket physicochemical preferences, a target-specific druglikeness index enables effective screening when inactive data are unavailable and complements oral-drug-focused indices that emphasize ADMET-related constraints.

verbatim_claims.text: We developed QEX, which is a new druglikeness index speciﬁc to individual targets.
QEX is an improvement of the quantitative estimate of druglikeness (QED) method, which is a popular quantitative evaluation method of druglikeness proposed by Bickerton et al.

QEX models the physicochemical properties of compounds that act on each target protein based on the concept of QED modeling physicochemical properties from information on US Food and Drug Administration-approved drugs.
QEX distinguished the inhibitors and non-inhibitors better than QED did.
QEX works efﬁciently even when datasets of inactive compounds are unavailable.
If both active and inactive compounds are present, QEX can be used as an initial ﬁlter to enhance the screening ability of conventional ligand-based virtual screenings.
Therefore, we proposed a target-speciﬁc QED, named QEX, which speciﬁcally screens drug candidates directed at particular targets.
In contrast, since QEX and the original QED can be modeled with only the active compound, it can be used even in cases lacking inactive compounds.
QEX was better suited for screening inhibitors of speciﬁc targets than the original QED.
QEX is easy to use when datasets of inactive compounds are not available.
If both active and inactive compounds are available, QEX can be used as an initial ﬁlter to enhance conventional ligand-based virtual screenings.

paper_title: An optimisation approach for pre-runtime scheduling of tasks and communication in an integrated modular avionic system
paper_claims: Preprocessing that tightens task timing windows and prunes successor/predecessor candidates and dependency directions preserves feasibility while reducing the search space for the scheduler.

verbatim_claims.text: In this paper we present a mathematical model for an industrially relevant avionic system and present a constraint generation procedure for the scheduling of such systems.
The computational results show that our optimisation approach can be used to create schedules for such instances within a reasonable time.

The main computational challenge of the problem stems from the huge number of tasks to be sequenced on the communication modules, and a key contribution of this paper is our constraint generation procedure to handle this.
Computational results to verify that our approach can be used to solve instances of practical relevance are presented in Sect. 6, followed by concluding comments in Sect. 7.

paper_title: An adaptive LTE listen-before-talk scheme towards a fair coexistence with Wi-Fi in unlicensed spectrum
paper_claims: Fixed LTE Licensed-Assisted Access channel occupancy durations that are much longer than single-packet Wi‑Fi transmissions, particularly when Wi‑Fi does not aggregate frames, create imbalanced channel occupancy that can yield unfair coexistence in co-channel deployments.

An adaptive LTE‑U listen‑before‑talk mechanism that performs clear channel assessment before each burst and then uses a tunable transmission opportunity followed by a tunable muting period, with subframe‑aligned reservation signaling, enables fair, technology‑agnostic coexistence with Wi‑Fi and other unlicensed systems by balancing access opportunities according to network density and traffic requirements while preserving time‑critical LTE signaling on licensed spectrum.

Fair coexistence in unlicensed bands is more appropriately defined as equal sharing of wireless resources among co‑located networks rather than among different radio technologies, with throughput and channel occupancy serving as practical fairness indicators.

verbatim_claims.text: In order to enable harmonious coexistence and fair spectrum sharing among LTE-U and Wi-Fi, an adaptive LTE-U LBT scheme is presented.
This scheme uses a variable LTE transmission opportunity (TXOP) followed by a variable muting period.
This way, co-located Wi-Fi networks can exploit the muting period to gain access to the wireless medium.
The results show that by conﬁguring the LTE-U with the appropriate TXOP and muting period values, the proposed scheme can signiﬁcantly improve the coexistence among LTE-U and Wi-Fi in a fair manner.
Finally, a preliminary algorithm is proposed on how the optimal conﬁguration parameters can be selected towards harmonious and fair coexistence.
We deﬁne a new adap- tive LTE-U transmission scheme according to which LTE cantransmit in unlicensed spectrum using a variable TXOP time.
This TXOP period is followed by a variable muting period in order to give channel access opportunities to other poten- tially co-located networks, such as Wi-Fi.
Before a TXOP , LTE must perform a CCA procedure in order to determine the availability of the channel.
This scheme can be used for LTE transmissions in the unlicensed spectrum next to the primary cell that an operator uses in the licensed spectrum similar to LTE LAA.

qa_by_claim.claim: Fixed LTE Licensed-Assisted Access channel occupancy durations that are much longer than single-packet Wi‑Fi transmissions, particularly when Wi‑Fi does not aggregate frames, create imbalanced channel occupancy that can yield unfair coexistence in co-channel deployments.

[A] LAA uses fixed, long channel occupancy bursts relative to single-packet, non-aggregated Wi‑Fi, leading to disproportionate airtime and potential unfair coexistence. [B] LAA uses very short channel occupancy bursts comparable to Wi‑Fi aggregated transmissions, producing balanced airtime and fair coexistence. [C] LAA and Wi‑Fi operate on different channels, eliminating coexistence concerns regardless of occupancy durations. [D] Wi‑Fi aggregates frames while LAA varies its occupancy adaptively, ensuring fairness independent of relative burst lengths.

Choose the correct option (A–D): In co-channel deployments where LTE LAA uses fixed channel occupancy durations that are much longer than single-packet Wi‑Fi transmissions and Wi‑Fi does not aggregate frames, what is the resulting coexistence effect? [A] Balanced channel occupancy and improved fairness. [B] Unchanged channel occupancy distribution with neutral fairness. [C] Imbalanced channel occupancy that can yield unfair coexistence. [D] Reduced LAA airtime share that benefits Wi‑Fi fairness.

[A] It performs clear channel assessment before each burst, uses a tunable transmission opportunity followed by a tunable muting period, and applies subframe-aligned reservation signaling, thereby balancing access according to network density and traffic requirements to enable fair, technology-agnostic coexistence with Wi-Fi and other unlicensed systems while preserving time-critical LTE signaling on licensed spectrum. [B] It performs clear channel assessment once per frame, uses fixed transmission and muting durations, and relies on non-aligned reservation signaling, thereby prioritizing LTE over other technologies and ignoring network density and traffic requirements while moving time-critical LTE signaling to unlicensed spectrum. [C] It skips clear channel assessment before bursts, uses tunable transmission but fixed muting without reservation signaling, thereby achieving coexistence only with Wi-Fi by equal airtime across all densities and shifting time-critical LTE signaling off licensed spectrum. [D] It uses random access without listen-before-talk, employs subframe-misaligned reservations, and fixes the muting period while adapting only LTE throughput, providing fairness only under sparse deployments and placing time-critical LTE signaling on unlicensed carriers.

Choose the correct option (A–D): Which configuration of an LTE-U listen-before-talk mechanism achieves fair, technology-agnostic coexistence with Wi-Fi and other unlicensed systems by balancing access according to network density and traffic requirements while preserving time-critical LTE signaling on licensed spectrum? [A] Performs CCA sporadically, uses fixed TXOP and muting, with asynchronous reservation signals, balancing airtime equally regardless of network conditions, and routes time-critical signaling to unlicensed channels. [C] Performs CCA before each burst, uses a tunable transmission opportunity followed by a tunable muting period, and employs subframe-aligned reservation signaling to adapt access opportunities to network density and traffic needs while maintaining time-critical LTE signaling on licensed spectrum. paper_title: Providing Reliable Communications over Static-node-assisted Vehicular Networks Using Distance-vector Routing
paper_claims: RDV is a table-driven distance-vector routing protocol for static-node-assisted vehicular delay-tolerant networks that, using statistic-based vehicle selection to estimate local movement probabilities and adaptive packet duplication to satisfy a preconfigured expected end-to-end delivery ratio, computes shortest paths with a traffic-aware metric through lightweight neighbor exchanges without roadmaps, thereby enabling reliable communication under sparse and unreliable static-node deployment with low control overhead.

verbatim_claims.text: In this paper, we propose a new table- driven distance-vector routing protocol called RDV (Reliable Distance-Vector routing) for static-node-assisted vehicular networks.
RDV provides the guarantee that the expected packet delivery ratio exceeds the preconfigured value by creating the necessary number of packet copies sent on the shortest path.
Evaluation results show that RDV provides a stably high packet delivery ratio and outperforms other conventional routing schemes in both packet delivery ratio and overhead of packet copies.

In this paper, we propose a new design of static-node- assisted vehicular networks called RDV (Reliable Distance- Vector Routing) that solves the above practical problems by introducing several new concepts in vehicular networks.
First, RDV deploys simple and light distance-vector routing instead of link-state routing to compute reliable paths between every pair of static nodes.
Thanks to the favorable characteristics of distance-vector routing, neighbor discovery and shortest-path computation are both executed autonomously within the low overhead of control messages.
Since all the participating static nodes are detected by routing protocols, RDV works without the help of roadmaps.

Second, RDV incorporates statistic- based vehicle selection (SVS) that captures the statistic on local traffic patterns of vehicles to select with high probability vehicles that carry packets to the next static nodes.
As a base of reliable routing, SVS computes the probabilities of each vehicle to reach other static nodes.

Third, RDV introduces adaptive packet duplication (APD) that adaptively creates the required number of packet copies based on the probabilities computed by SVS to achieve a certain ratio of successful packet delivery.

This function provides a better expectation value than a predefined value on packet delivery ratio even over unreliable links, which allows us to locate statistic nodes in only some of the major intersections to reduce deployment cost.

qa_by_claim.claim: RDV is a table-driven distance-vector routing protocol for static-node-assisted vehicular delay-tolerant networks that, using statistic-based vehicle selection to estimate local movement probabilities and adaptive packet duplication to satisfy a preconfigured expected end-to-end delivery ratio, computes shortest paths with a traffic-aware metric through lightweight neighbor exchanges without roadmaps, thereby enabling reliable communication under sparse and unreliable static-node deployment with low control overhead.

mcq.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which option fully characterizes RDV in terms of routing style, target network, mechanisms, path computation, information requirements, and resulting properties? [A] A table-driven distance-vector protocol for static-node-assisted vehicular delay-tolerant networks that estimates local movement probabilities via statistic-based vehicle selection, adaptively duplicates packets to meet a preconfigured expected end-to-end delivery ratio, computes shortest paths using a traffic-aware metric through lightweight neighbor exchanges without roadmaps, and thereby provides reliable communication under sparse and unreliable static-node deployment with low control overhead. [B] A reactive link-state protocol for general mobile ad hoc networks that relies on preloaded roadmaps and global traffic maps, avoids packet duplication to minimize bandwidth, and emphasizes maximum-throughput routes even when static nodes are dense, with high control overhead. [C] A geographic greedy protocol for vehicular networks without static nodes, which selects next hops solely by instantaneous GPS positions, floods packets to maximize delivery, computes minimal-hop paths without traffic awareness, and requires detailed roadmaps. [D] A table-driven distance-vector protocol for static-node-assisted vehicular DTNs that uses fixed-k replication without movement estimation, computes shortest paths by hop count with periodic full-table exchanges and city roadmaps, achieving reliability only when static nodes are dense with moderate overhead.

Choose the correct option (A–D): Which statement correctly describes RDV's operation and guarantees? [A] A reactive distance-vector scheme that uses per-destination flooding alongside map-based route planning to guarantee perfect delivery regardless of static-node availability, at the cost of high control overhead. [B] A table-driven link-state scheme for vehicular DTNs that estimates global movement patterns and disables packet duplication to conserve resources, producing energy-aware rather than traffic-aware paths that depend on roadmaps. [C] A table-driven distance-vector scheme for static-node-assisted vehicular delay-tolerant networks that uses statistic-based vehicle selection to estimate local movement probabilities and adaptively duplicates packets to meet a configured expected end-to-end delivery ratio, finding shortest paths with a traffic-aware metric via lightweight neighbor exchanges without roadmaps, which enables reliable communication even when static nodes are sparse and unreliable while keeping control overhead low. [D] A delay-tolerant routing heuristic for urban sensing that adopts opportunistic store-carry-forward without neighbor exchanges, relies exclusively on static nodes, and deliberately avoids any shortest-path computation or delivery-ratio targets.

true_false.question: RDV is a table-driven distance-vector protocol for static-node-assisted vehicular delay-tolerant networks that estimates local movement probabilities via statistic-based vehicle selection and uses adaptive packet duplication to satisfy a preconfigured expected end-to-end delivery ratio, computing shortest traffic-aware paths through lightweight neighbor exchanges without roadmaps to enable reliable communication under sparse and unreliable static-node deployment with low control overhead.

RDV is a reactive link-state protocol for vehicular delay-tolerant networks without static-node assistance that relies on roadmaps and global traffic maps, avoids packet duplication, and computes routes without a traffic-aware metric, which yields reliable communication only when static nodes are dense and results in high control overhead.

Reason (R): It proactively maintains distance vectors using traffic-aware metrics exchanged with neighbors and tunes packet replication to match a configured delivery ratio, eliminating the need for roadmaps and reducing control messaging.

paper_title: Estimation of and correction for finite motion sampling errors in small animal PET rigid motion correction
paper_claims: In rigid motion–corrected small animal PET, a voxel-wise residual motion deconvolution that derives spatially variant 3D kernels from measured motion by modeling pose uncertainty within sampling intervals and applies them via Richardson–Lucy deconvolution reduces the finite-sampling–induced, motion-speed–dependent resolution loss and associated regional quantification biases to a level comparable to motion correction using pose interpolation.

verbatim_claims.text: We calculate a tailored spatially variant deconvolution kernel from the measured motion data which is then used to deconvolve the motion corrected image using a 3D Richardson-Lucy algorithm.

In awake animal experiments, the proposed method can serve to mitigate the finite sampling factor degrading the spatial resolution as well as the resolution differences between fast-moving and slow-moving animals.
Therefore, we develop a strategy to calculate the resolution kernel that models the resolution loss in image space that is caused by the LOR positioning uncertainty due to the finite sampling.
The shape of the kernel will depend on the subject motion and tracking frame rate and can be calculated from the measured motion.

Instead, here we performed first an event-by-event motion correction to compensate the measured component of the motion and then performed the residual motion deconvolution that models uncertainty in the measured motion due to the finite motion sampling.

qa_by_claim.claim: In rigid motion–corrected small animal PET, a voxel-wise residual motion deconvolution that derives spatially variant 3D kernels from measured motion by modeling pose uncertainty within sampling intervals and applies them via Richardson–Lucy deconvolution reduces the finite-sampling–induced, motion-speed–dependent resolution loss and associated regional quantification biases to a level comparable to motion correction using pose interpolation.

paper_title: Discovering the Hidden Community Structure of Public Transportation Networks
paper_claims: A transfer network that represents atomic passenger groups as maximal cliques linked by directed transfer edges, together with a community network that connects passenger pairs using a connection-strength metric that rewards shared transfers across different vehicle trips and penalizes repeated co-presence within the same trip, captures similarity in rider travel behavior beyond contact duration alone.

In an activity-based simulation of the Twin Cities public transit system, frequent multi-trip travel corridors primarily link outlying suburbs to the city center during peak hours and epidemic spreading risk concentrates on routes that cross or connect to the city center.

verbatim_claims.text: In this paper we utilize known contact patterns from a public transit assignment model in a major metropolitan city, and propose the development of two novel network structures, each of which elucidate certain aspects of passenger travel behavior.
We first propose the development of a transfer network, which can reveal passenger groups that travel together on a given day.
Second, we propose the development of a community network, which is derived from the transfer network, and captures the similarity of travel patterns among passengers.

We then explore the application of each of these network structures to identify the most frequently used travel paths, i.e., routes and transfers, in the public transit system, and model epidemic spreading risk among passengers of a public transit network, respectively.

In the latter our conclusions reinforce previous observations, that routes crossing or connecting to the city center in the morning and afternoon peak hours are the most “dangerous” during an outbreak.
We further demonstrate potential applications of each of these novel network structures.
First, the transfer network is implemented to detect the most frequent vehicle trip combinations in the system (bus and train transfers).
Identifying the most relevant vehicle trip combinations can aid public transit authorities in timetable planning and optimizing vehicle assignments.
Second, the community network is applied to evaluate a diffusion process in a transit network, specifically infectious disease spread among passengers.
The community network proposed here can further improve the performance of such models due to its novel representation of passenger interactions.

We use the proposed network structure to simulate how a disease might spread among the vehicles of the public transportation system and the suburbs of the city, and identify the vehicle trips most likely to carry infected passengers.

qa_by_claim.claim: A transfer network that represents atomic passenger groups as maximal cliques linked by directed transfer edges, together with a community network that connects passenger pairs using a connection-strength metric that rewards shared transfers across different vehicle trips and penalizes repeated co-presence within the same trip, captures similarity in rider travel behavior beyond contact duration alone.

mcq.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): An approach combines a transfer network that represents atomic passenger groups as maximal cliques linked by directed transfer edges with a community network that connects passenger pairs using a connection-strength metric that rewards shared transfers across different vehicle trips and penalizes repeated co-presence within the same trip to capture similarity in rider travel behavior beyond contact duration alone. Which option matches this description? [A] The transfer network models atomic groups as maximal cliques with directed transfer edges, the community network connects pairs by a metric that rewards cross-trip shared transfers and penalizes repeated same-trip co-presence, and together they capture rider-behavior similarity beyond contact duration alone. [B] The transfer network models atomic groups as chains with undirected edges, the community network uses contact duration only, and the combined model does not improve upon contact duration. [C] The transfer network uses maximal cliques but undirected transfer edges, the community network rewards repeated co-presence within the same trip and ignores transfers across different trips, and the approach relies solely on contact duration. [D] The transfer network uses random subgraphs with directed edges, the community network links any pair sharing a trip without weighting, and the result captures only contact duration.

Choose the correct option (A–D): To capture similarity in rider travel behavior beyond contact duration alone, what network representations and pairwise metric are used? [A] Transfer network: maximal cliques with undirected transfer edges; community network: connection-strength rewards repeated same-trip co-presence and ignores cross-trip transfers. [B] Transfer network: chains with directed edges; community network: connection-strength uses only contact duration. [C] Transfer network: maximal cliques with directed transfer edges; community network: connection-strength rewards shared transfers across different trips and penalizes repeated co-presence within the same trip. [D] Transfer network: random subgraphs; community network: unweighted links for any co-presence.

paper_title: In vivo estimation of passive biomechanical properties of human myocardium
paper_claims: Maintaining fixed ratios among the anisotropic parameters (af:as:afs and bf:bs:bfs) and estimating only four scalars (a, b, Ka, Kb) preserves the experimentally observed shear stiffness ordering and suffices to calibrate the model from limited data.

Identified passive myocardial parameters depend more strongly on the assumed left-ventricular end-diastolic pressure than on ventricular geometry or fibre helix angle, with the stiffness nonlinearity parameter b being most sensitive.
In vivo human myocardial passive properties are less stiff than pig-derived or ex vivo human measurements, which otherwise yield unrealistically stiff diastolic behavior in human ventricular models.

Across subjects and within physiological variations of pressure and fibre orientation, the predicted shear stress–strain behavior in dominant shear modes remains similar when parameters are constrained to the estimated ranges.

verbatim_claims.text: Finite element modelling, response surface method and geneticalgorithm were used to inversel y estimate the constitutive parameters.
Due to the ill-posed nature of the inverse opti- misation problem, the myocardial properties was extracted by identifying the ranges of the parameters, instead of finding unique values.
Although uniqueness of the solution cannot be achieved, the normal ranges of the parameters produced similar mechanical responses within th e physiological ranges.

In order to avoid invasive measurements and additional scans, LV cavity volume, measur ed from routine MRI, and empirical pressure-normalised- volume relation (Klotz-curve) were used as clinical data.

An inverse estimation method, which is typically for- mulated as a non-linear optimisation problem to minimisethe differences in the measurements with respect to the unknown parameters, was used to quantify passive prop- erties of human myocardium using Holzapfel-Ogden ma-terial law.

Therefore, the study proposed a method to achieve the following objec- tives: (a) to estimate the normal ranges of Holzapfel- Ogden parameters for healthy human myocardium usingroutinely used non-invasive clinical data, and (b) to ex- plore the effect of geometry, fibre orientation and EDP on the estimation of passive parameters.

qa_by_claim.claim: Maintaining fixed ratios among the anisotropic parameters (af:as:afs and bf:bs:bfs) and estimating only four scalars (a, b, Ka, Kb) preserves the experimentally observed shear stiffness ordering and suffices to calibrate the model from limited data.

[A] The experimental shear stiffness ordering is preserved and calibration from limited data is sufficient [B] The shear stiffness ordering changes, but calibration from limited data is still sufficient [C] The shear stiffness ordering is preserved, but extensive data and additional parameters are required for calibration [D] The shear stiffness ordering changes and calibration from limited data is not possible

paper_title: A Novel Gaussian–Bernoulli Based Convolutional Deep Belief Networks for Image Feature Extraction
paper_claims: Stacking Gaussian–Bernoulli convolutional RBMs with shared convolutional filters, zero-padded reconstruction, probabilistic max pooling, learnable variances, and a momentum-plus-L2 weight update into a deep belief network and fine-tuning it with stochastic diagonal Levenberg–Marquardt yields translation-invariant, spatially coherent features from real-valued images with lower computational complexity than fully connected deep belief networks and improved generalization.

verbatim_claims.text: In this paper, for images features extracting and recognizing, a novel deep neural network called Gaussian–Bernoulli based Convolutional Deep Belief Network (GCDBN) is proposed.

The experimental results show that our model is more effective for most of images recognition tasks with comparably low computational cost than some of popular methods, which is suggested that our proposed deep network is a potentially applicable method for real-world image recognition.
In this paper, we propose a novel generative model called Gaussian–Bernoulli based Convolutional RBM (GCRBM) by combining CNN with GRBM.
Moreover, by stacking the proposed GCRBMs, a corresponding deep model is also proposed, which is called Gaussian–Bernoulli based Convolutional Deep Belief Network (GCDBN).

Several convolution and pooling layers are connected in the architecture of GCDBN, whichcan not only generate low-dimensioned and translation-invariant features, but also ensureeffective ability of feature extraction.

In addition, we use proposed updating rule and SDLMalgorithm to replace traditional BP in ﬁne-tuning GCDBN, which can not only speed up theconvergence of entire model, but also reduce the over-ﬁtting phenomenon.
From the experimental results on two benchmarking datasets, we can conclude that GCRBM can effectively improve the performance of feature extraction by introducing vari- ance and convolution.
In addition, it is also demonstrated the effectiveness of GCDBN inimage classiﬁcation.

qa_by_claim.claim: Stacking Gaussian–Bernoulli convolutional RBMs with shared convolutional filters, zero-padded reconstruction, probabilistic max pooling, learnable variances, and a momentum-plus-L2 weight update into a deep belief network and fine-tuning it with stochastic diagonal Levenberg–Marquardt yields translation-invariant, spatially coherent features from real-valued images with lower computational complexity than fully connected deep belief networks and improved generalization.

[A] Translation-invariant, spatially coherent features with lower computational complexity and improved generalization. [B] Rotation-invariant features with higher computational complexity and no generalization improvement. [C] Features limited to binary images with similar computational complexity and increased overfitting. [D] Translation-invariant features but with unchanged computational complexity and no generalization benefit.

Choose the correct option (A–D): Which configuration and training procedure produces translation-invariant, spatially coherent features from real-valued images with lower computational complexity than fully connected deep belief networks and improved generalization? [A] Stacking Bernoulli–Bernoulli fully connected RBMs without weight decay and fine-tuning by stochastic gradient descent. [B] Stacking Gaussian–Bernoulli convolutional RBMs with shared filters, zero-padded reconstruction, probabilistic max pooling, learnable variances, and momentum-plus-L2 updates into a deep belief network, then fine-tuning with stochastic diagonal Levenberg–Marquardt. [C] Training a single convolutional autoencoder with ReLU activations and Adam optimizer. [D] Stacking Gaussian–Bernoulli convolutional RBMs with average pooling and fixed variances, fine-tuned by full Newton’s method.

When Gaussian–Bernoulli convolutional RBMs with shared convolutional filters, zero-padded reconstruction, probabilistic max pooling, learnable variances, and momentum-plus-L2 weight updates are stacked into a deep belief network and fine-tuned with stochastic diagonal Levenberg–Marquardt on real-valued images, the outcome is translation-invariant, spatially coherent features alongside higher computational complexity than fully connected deep belief networks and no improvement in generalization.

paper_title: Predicting user demographics from music listening information
paper_claims: Age, gender, and nationality can be inferred from music listening histories by encoding listened artists and artist tags with TF-IDF and PCA and augmenting with behavioral scores, yielding supervised models that outperform majority baselines and remain effective with extremely sparse listening data, and the inferred attributes can be used to improve music recommendations.

verbatim_claims.text: We propose a feature modeling approach building on TermFrequency-Inverse Document Frequency (TF-IDF) for artist listening information and artist tags combined with additionally extracted features.
We show that we can substantially out- perform a baseline majority voting approach and can compete with existing approaches.
Further, regarding prediction accuracy vs. available listening data we show that even onesingle listening event per user is enough to outperform the baseline in all prediction tasks.

We conclude that personal information can be derived from music listening information, which indeed can help better tailoring rec- ommendations, as we illustrate with the use case of a music recommender system that can directly utilize the user attributes predicted by our algorithm to increase the quality of it’srecommendations.

Specifically, as the success of such a prediction system dependson its performance and accuracy, we analyze the possible negative influences on our algo-rithm in detail, particularly the influence of the available user data.
Additionally, we describe the use case of a music recommender system that candirectly utilize the user attributes predicted by our algorithm to increase the quality of it’srecommendations.

The results of our proposed approach can be utilized to enrich the input for recommender systems (e. g., to replace missing values for collaborative filtering approaches) or directlyfor recommending new items (e. g., artists that are popular in the country or within the agegroup of the user).

paper_title: Approximation of quantum control correction scheme using deep neural networks
paper_claims: Bidirectional long short-term memory networks approximate the functional mapping from normal control pulses for an ideal two-qubit system to de-noising pulses that compensate coherent drift, enabling generation of effective controls without explicit system modelling.

The learnability and accuracy of the NCP-to-DCP mapping depend on the drift structure, being easier when the drift Hamiltonian resembles the base spin-chain interaction and harder for asymmetric single-qubit drifts orthogonal to the available controls.

The learned mapping exhibits continuity and approximate symmetry with respect to local perturbations of the input pulses, as small changes in control amplitudes at nearby times or along different control axes induce statistically similar variations in the corrected pulses over mid evolution windows.
For weak Markovian incoherent noise modeled by Lindblad operators, learning-based correction of control pulses does not provide a meaningful performance gain over uncorrected pulses.

Optimizing for robustness by training on Gaussian-amplitude–perturbed copies of the generated pulses fails to produce controls with improved fluctuation resilience, indicating limitations of gradient-based learning for this robustness objective.

verbatim_claims.text: We show that a class of artiﬁcialneural networks called LSTM is able to model this functional relationship with high efﬁciency, and hence the correction scheme required to counterbalance the effect of the drift.
Our solution allows studying the mapping from quantum control pulses tosystem dynamics and analysing its behaviour with respect to the local variations in the control proﬁle.
We propose an approximation that not only incorporates the global features of the function but also describes its localproperties.

In this paper, we propose a method, based on an artiﬁcial neural network (ANN), to study the correction scheme between control pulses obtained in the ideal case,and those obtained when the system is subject to undesired dynamics.
Moreover, we demonstrate that the utilized network has high efﬁciency and can be used to analysis of the properties of the model.
We propose to use a bidirectional LSTM neural network.
We argue that this type of artiﬁcial neural network is suitable to capture time dependences present in quantum control pulses.

We have developed a method of reconstructing the relation between control pulses in an idealized case and control pulses required to implement quantum computation in the presence of undesirable dynamics.
We argue that the proposed method can be a useful tool to study the manifold of quantum control pulses in the noisy regime, and to deﬁne new theoretical approaches to control noisy quantum systems.
