paper_title: A Lagrangian cylindrical coordinate system for characterizing dynamic surface geometry of tubular anatomic structures
paper_claims: A Lagrangian cylindrical coordinate system parameterized by centerline arc length and a Greenwich-referenced angular coordinate defined from a bifurcation landmark provides a continuous, material-point mapping of tubular luminal surfaces that enables pointwise, time-resolved quantification of longitudinal and circumferential surface curvature and cross-sectional eccentricity, including its orientation.

verbatim_claims.text: To quantify the dynamic surface geometry of tubular-shaped anatomic structures, we propose a simple, rigorous Lagrangian cylindrical coordinate system to monitor well-defined surface points.
Specifically, the proposed systemenables quantification of surface curvature and cross-sectional eccentricity.

Using idealized software phantom examples, we validate the method ’s ability to accurately quantify longitudinal and circumferential surface curvature, as well as eccentricity and orientation of eccentricity.

We then apply the method to several medical imaging data sets of human vascular structures to exemplify the utility of this coordinate system for analyzing morphology and dynamic geometric changes in blood vessels throughout the body.
Here we present a method for creating a Lagrangian description of approximate cylindrical structures based on acylindrical coordinate system.

Building from a vessel centerline and lumen cross-sectional contours, this coordinate system can describe complex surface geometry, including longitudinal and circumferential curvature, cross-sectional eccentricity, and the orientation of eccentricity.

We validate this method with idealized software phantoms and demonstrate the wide potential of this method by analyzing dynamic changes of blood vessel geometry in patient-specific examples of the thoracic aorta, abdominal aorta, and iliofemoral vein.

paper_title: Automated segmentation of trabecular and cortical bone from proton density weighted MRI of the knee
paper_claims: Combining a 3D local intensity clustering-based level set that jointly estimates MRI bias fields with normal-vector intensity line profiling and variance-minimizing, thickness-constrained optimization enables automatic delineation of trabecular and cortical bone of the femur and tibia in proton density weighted knee MRI without training data, robust to intensity inhomogeneity and weak edges near ligaments.

verbatim_claims.text: In this paper, we proposed a method for automatic segmentation of knee bone structures for MRI data with a 3D local intensity clustering-based level set and a novel approach to determine the cortical boundary utilizing the normal vector ofthe trabecular surface.
Application to clinical imaging data shows that our method is robust to MRI inhomogeneity.
In this paper, we propose an automatic segmentation for trabecular and cortical bone of the femur and tibia in a clini-cally relevant MR sequence, proton density weighted contrast [ 21].
We use a local energy-based level set method to obtain a 3D rough segmentation of trabecular bone and correct the image data from the inhomogeneity problem.
Then, we optimize the trabecular boundary based on the intensity lines and propose an iterative process to detect the cortical boundary.
In this paper, we presented and evaluated an automatic workflow to segment the trabecular and cortical bone of femur and tibia with PDW sequence in MRI.

Initial results compared with manual segmentation indicate the possibility to providean automatic segmentation to researchers and clinical doctors to perform further analysis rather than the time-consuming manual segmentation.

Choose the correct option (A–D): In proton density weighted knee MRI, the combination of a bias-field–estimating 3D local intensity clustering level set, normal-vector intensity line profiling, and variance-minimizing, thickness-constrained optimization achieves which of the following? [A] Automatic delineation of trabecular and cortical bone of the femur and tibia, without training data and robust to intensity inhomogeneity and weak edges near ligaments [B] Automatic delineation of trabecular and cortical bone of the femur and tibia, but only with extensive training data and strong edges [C] Semi-automatic segmentation of trabecular bone of the tibia alone, robust to weak edges but not to intensity inhomogeneity [D] Automatic delineation of cartilage in proton density weighted knee MRI, without training data but not robust to weak edges near ligaments

true_false.question: Combining a 3D local intensity clustering-based level set that jointly estimates MRI bias fields with normal-vector intensity line profiling and variance-minimizing, thickness-constrained optimization enables automatic delineation of femoral and tibial trabecular and cortical bone in proton density weighted knee MRI without training data and remains robust to intensity inhomogeneity and weak edges near ligaments.

Combining a 3D local intensity clustering-based level set that jointly estimates MRI bias fields with normal-vector intensity line profiling and variance-minimizing, thickness-constrained optimization requires training data and is not robust to weak edges near ligaments to delineate trabecular and cortical bone of the femur and tibia in proton density weighted knee MRI.

fill_blank.question: Combining a 3D local intensity clustering-based level set that jointly estimates MRI bias fields with normal-vector intensity line profiling and variance-minimizing, thickness-constrained optimization enables automatic delineation of trabecular and cortical bone of the femur and tibia in proton density weighted knee MRI _________(without training data/with supervised training), and is robust to intensity inhomogeneity and weak edges near ligaments.

By combining a 3D local intensity clustering-based level set that jointly estimates MRI bias fields with normal-vector intensity line profiling and variance-minimizing, thickness-constrained optimization, automatic delineation of trabecular and cortical bone of the femur and tibia in _________(proton density weighted/T1-weighted) knee MRI is achieved without training data and with robustness to intensity inhomogeneity and weak edges near ligaments.

Reason (R): This is because the combination integrates joint MRI bias field estimation with boundary profiling along normals and thickness-constrained variance minimization.
